What success rate does ICRL Preset achieve on Game of 24 after 50 trials?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.
What types of reward functions does ICRL support?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' supports two types of reward functions:

1. **Sparse rewards** (outcome reward model): r(s) is nonzero only at terminal states.
2. **Dense rewards** (progress reward model): r(s) can be nonzero for non-terminal states.

The reward function can be: rule-based, learned separately, or the same LLM for self-evaluation. With LLM self-evaluation there is no external feedback, yet performance still improves (evaluation is easier than generation). The authors hypothesize the ceiling with self-evaluation is lower than with external feedback.
How does ICRL perform on creative writing?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.

What related work is discussed in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' discusses related work in: (1) In-Context RL — prior ICRL works used small models in games/robotics (meta-RL), not language tasks. (2) Inference-Time Self-Improvement — Self-Refine, Reflexion use verbal feedback, prone to hallucinated feedback causing performance collapse. Search methods (ToT, GoT, MCTS) use engineered components, not intrinsic learning. ICRL uses only scalar rewards, enabling learning from failure and leveraging the model's emergent capacity.

[Line 1723] Generating Abstracts for Papers the Model Has Never Seen.
[Line 1725] To figure out if ICRL is actually learning from reward signals or just pulling answers from what the model already knows, the authors came up with a clever test: they picked a task where the model's built-in knowledge wouldn't really help.
[Line 1728] They grabbed 30 arXiv papers that came out after GPT-4.1-mini's training data ended.
[Line 1728] The model only gets the paper title and has to write the abstract from scratch.
[Line 1728] The whole point is to see how closely the model can match the real abstract that it's never seen before.
[Line 1728] They check how well the model does using ROUGE-recall, which compares the generated abstract against the actual one, and that same score doubles as the reward signal.
[Line 1728] Since these abstracts weren't in the training data, the model can't just look them up internally -- it actually has to learn from the reward feedback.
[Line 1732] Looking at the results, Best-of-1024 sampling only hits 0.44 ROUGE-recall, which tells you that just sampling a bunch of times from the model isn't going to find something it doesn't know.
[Line 1733] Self-Refine gets stuck pretty fast at 0.45 since it doesn't get any external reward to guide it.
[Line 1733] Reflexion does a tiny bit better at 0.46 but mostly looks the same as Self-Refine -- its corrections are basically driven by the model talking to itself, which doesn't add much useful info here.
[Line 1734] ICRL, on the other hand, keeps getting better over 200 rounds and reaches 0.59 ROUGE-recall, showing it can genuinely learn from reward signals and go beyond what the model already knows.

What is the fundamental difference between ICRL and verbal feedback methods?

The fundamental difference is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. ICRL uses only numerical scalar rewards — no textual feedback, no instructions on how to improve. Self-Refine and Reflexion use verbal/textual feedback. Verbal feedback methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, while scalar rewards provide a clean learning signal.
Compare ICRL to Self-Refine and Reflexion.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.

[Line 956] Q: Which methods have access to the reward r? A: Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting.
[Line 957] Q: How does Self-Refine work without r? A: It asks GPT-4.1 to provide verbal feedback instead of using numerical reward.
[Line 958] Q: Why is Long-CoT style prompting included? A: Because CoT and Long-CoT style prompting are hard to distinguish for creative writing.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] Q: What model is used for policy and reward? A: GPT-4.1 serves as both the policy LLM pi_theta and the reward model r.
[Line 959] Q: How is S_0 constructed each episode? A: By concatenating all previous generations along with their coherence scores from r.
[Line 960] Q: Is the reward dense or sparse? A: It is sparse -- only R_T can be nonzero.
[Line 961] Q: What is included in S_0 then? A: Only R_T.
[Line 963] Q: What are the win rates? A: 59.48% vs. Reflexion, 78.36% vs. Long-CoT, 86.32% vs. Self-Refine, and 93.81% vs. Best-of-N.

[Line 964] Q: Where are the reward curves? A: The return curve from r is in the Figure; a running max is in the Appendix.
[Line 964] Q: What happens when you extend Self-Refine and ICRL? A: ICRL keeps improving over 50 more episodes, while Self-Refine plateaus and then declines, likely due to context growth.
Explain ICRL Preset and ICRL Autonomous.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).

[Line 596] Inference-Time Self-Improvement of Large Language Models.
[Line 603] Current approaches to LLM self-improvement at inference time predominantly employ natural language self-revision strategies, with representative methods including Self-Refine, Reflexion, and Textual Gradient.
[Line 603] A fundamental limitation of these approaches stems from their reliance on the model's parametric knowledge for generating revision feedback. This dependency results in hallucinated feedback signals that accumulate across iterative rounds, ultimately causing degradation in output quality.
[Line 603] These methods can be functionally characterized as language-guided search procedures in which generated feedback constitutes explicit instructions for subsequent revision steps.
[Line 605] The ICRL framework diverges from these methods by operating exclusively on numerical reward signals, without the generation of prescriptive revision instructions.
[Line 605] Under this framework, the model derives improved responses through pattern recognition applied to its accumulated experience history, a process that is functionally equivalent to reinforcement learning.
[Line 605] Notably, the reward signals employed in ICRL can be sourced directly from the task environment, yielding high-fidelity verification signals.
[Line 607] An alternative class of methods targets inference-time improvement through search algorithms, including Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT), Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), and Intelligent Go-Explore.
[Line 607] These search-based methods are characterized by their dependence on externally designed components, including heuristic evaluation functions and memory management subsystems, rather than exploiting the model's native learning capacity.
[Line 607] This work is additionally related to the prompt optimization literature, in which numerical evaluation scores drive iterative prompt refinement through top-k selection and error-based filtering.
[Line 608] Prompt optimization methods are therefore more appropriately categorized as in-context supervised learning approaches (comparable to filtered behavior cloning) rather than reinforcement learning methods.
[Line 608] In contrast to these supervised approaches, ICRL supports the utilization of failure experiences for learning.

[Line 306] • The framework isolates the LLM’s intrinsic ICRL capacity – no external code, no engineered mechanisms required. (2): Strong evidence for emergent RL behavior at inference time under the ICRL prompting framework.

[Line 322] • (3): ICRL prompting significantly outperforms self-revision methods (Self-Refine, Reflexion) across diverse benchmarks: Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT).
[Line 322] • Notable: In Game of 24 and creative writing, the LLM generates its own rewards, yet consistent performance improvements are still observed.
What is in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL)?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) is an inference-time paradigm where RL occurs during the forward pass of the network without parameter updates. The policy π_θ is conditioned on context C_t (e.g., all previous state-action-reward pairs). After pretraining, θ_* is fixed. Action quality improves as C_t grows — this is in-context policy improvement. This improvement occurs even on out-of-distribution tasks, ruling out memorization.

On Game of 24 after 50 trials: ICRL Preset 90%, Best-of-N 49%, Self-Refine 47%, Reflexion 44%. These are the exact numbers from the paper.

[Line 1073] My evaluation of ICRL across open-source models and Olympiad-level math.
[Line 1096] I wanted to show that ICRL is broadly applicable, so I set up experiments along two key dimensions: varying the model architecture and increasing the task complexity.
[Line 1096] First, I tested ICRL on several open-source models -- Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B in thinking mode -- using a creative writing task as the benchmark.
[Line 1096] Second, I applied ICRL to Olympiad-level mathematical problems, specifically from the AIME and HMMT competitions.
[Line 1096] I've included all the experimental setup details in the Appendix for reproducibility.
[Line 1096] What I found was encouraging: ICRL consistently beat the baselines, self-refine and Reflexion, across every configuration I tested.
[Line 1096] The gains were substantial -- 10 to 20 points over the base model on both the creative writing and math benchmarks.
[Line 1096] Based on these results, I'm confident that ICRL is a robust capability that transfers across different model architectures and performs effectively even on very challenging tasks.

What ROUGE-recall score does ICRL achieve on unseen paper abstracts?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. Given only the title, the model generates the abstract. ROUGE-recall is used as both reward and metric. ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall over 200 iterations, vs 0.44 Best-of-1024, 0.45 Self-Refine, 0.46 Reflexion. This demonstrates ICRL learns from external rewards and is not limited by pre-training knowledge.
How does ICRL perform on ScienceWorld?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based benchmark with 30 science-experiment tasks in a multi-room environment. Rewards are sparse. GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy. ICRL prompting outperforms baseline methods by about 20% after enough iterations. ICRL also scales better than baselines in terms of test-time compute budget (in dollar amounts).

What future directions does 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' suggest?

The conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners': RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. The minimal, scalar-reward-based ICRL prompting framework unlocks this ability across diverse models and tasks, outperforming self-revision methods. A key future direction is investigating how training-time interventions might further enhance this in-context RL capability. This points toward more autonomous agents that can explore, adapt, and self-improve in open-ended settings by learning from their own experience.

Why is ICRL prompting called 'minimal'?

The key design principle of ICRL prompting in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is **minimality**. To ensure gains come from the LLM's emergent RL capacity (not auxiliary mechanisms), they exclude: textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, and engineered modules. The only supervision is the scalar reward. This complies with the reward hypothesis ('goals can be thought of as maximization of cumulative scalar reward') and the 'reward is enough' hypothesis ('intelligence can be understood as subserving maximisation of reward').
What is in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL)?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) is an inference-time paradigm where RL occurs during the forward pass of the network without parameter updates. The policy π_θ is conditioned on context C_t (e.g., all previous state-action-reward pairs). After pretraining, θ_* is fixed. Action quality improves as C_t grows — this is in-context policy improvement. This improvement occurs even on out-of-distribution tasks, ruling out memorization.
What ROUGE-recall score does ICRL achieve on unseen paper abstracts?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. Given only the title, the model generates the abstract. ROUGE-recall is used as both reward and metric. ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall over 200 iterations, vs 0.44 Best-of-1024, 0.45 Self-Refine, 0.46 Reflexion. This demonstrates ICRL learns from external rewards and is not limited by pre-training knowledge.
How does ICRL differ from Self-Refine and Reflexion?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.
What are the creative writing results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.

Explain the experience buffer in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have innate ICRL ability. To activate it, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated as many as the context window allows into S_0. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.
Is this paper by David Silver?

No. 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) is NOT by David Silver. It is by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from UVA. David Silver co-authored a different paper also titled 'Reward is Enough' (2021, DeepMind).
What success rate does ICRL Preset achieve on Game of 24 after 50 trials?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).

[Line 748] The Experiments.
[Line 780] Now for the fun part — let's see how ICRL prompting actually performs in practice. The authors tested it on three quite different benchmarks: Game of 24 (a number puzzle game), creative writing, and ScienceWorld (a text-based science simulation).
[Line 781] To make sure the comparison is fair and comprehensive, they lined up several strong baselines: plain chain-of-thought prompting, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N sampling, Self-Refine, and Reflexion.
[Line 782] Here's an important detail that makes the comparison more honest: they didn't cap the prompt length for Self-Refine and Reflexion. Both methods were allowed to accumulate as much context as the LLM's window could fit.
What are the two ICRL instruction strategies?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).
What is 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' about?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
How does ICRL perform on AIME and HMMT?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT) using open-source models: Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B thinking mode. ICRL consistently outperforms Self-Refine and Reflexion in all settings, with improvements of up to 10-20 points over the base model. For Qwen3-32B thinking mode, ICRL remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT.
What are the Game of 24 results in ICRL?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.
Why does ICRL outperform Self-Refine?

ICRL outperforms Self-Refine because Self-Refine relies on verbal self-revision which is prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations, leading to performance collapse. ICRL uses only scalar rewards without verbal feedback, avoiding this problem. The model infers improvements by recognizing patterns from past experience.
What model is used in ScienceWorld experiments?
GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy for all compared algorithms in ScienceWorld, not GPT-4.1 or Qwen3-32B.
Why do Self-Refine and Reflexion suffer from performance collapse?

Self-Refine and Reflexion are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations. Since their verbal self-revision quality depends on the model's parametric knowledge of the task, incorrect feedback compounds over rounds. ICRL avoids this by using only scalar rewards without prescribing new instructions.

[Line 1180] Conclusion.
[Line 1181] The experimental results presented in this report confirm that reinforcement learning emerges as an inference-time capability of large language models without necessitating gradient-based updates.
[Line 1181] The proposed ICRL prompting methodology, which operates exclusively with scalar reward feedback, demonstrates broad applicability across diverse model families and general-purpose task domains, consistently outperforming competing self-revision techniques.
[Line 1181] Future technical efforts should focus on determining whether training-time modifications can further amplify this in-context reinforcement learning capability exhibited by LLMs.
[Line 1181] The efficacy of this capability, which exceeded initial expectations, indicates a trajectory toward the development of more autonomous agents with the capacity to explore, adapt, and self-improve within open-ended operational environments by leveraging experiential feedback.

To ensure gains come from the LLM's emergent RL capacity (not auxiliary mechanisms), they exclude: textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, and engineered modules. In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Qwen3-32B across context lengths of 8k, 16k, and 32k tokens. [Line 496] Think of the instruction like a little note you pass to the model before each try. It's written in regular words (not code), and researchers label it s_ICRL.
[Line 497] There are three kinds of notes you can give: (1) an "explore" note that says "try something new" (there's a picture of it in the back of the paper), (2) an "exploit" note that says "do your best based on what worked before" (also pictured in the back), and (3) a "your choice" note that lets the model pick between exploring and exploiting (pictured in the back too).
[Line 501] When you give the model the "explore" note, you're telling it: "Don't repeat yourself -- come up with an answer that's different from everything you've already tried."
[Line 503] When you give the model the "exploit" note, you're telling it: "Look back at which answers got the best scores and use those to come up with the best answer you can."
[Line 504] The researchers tried two ways of deciding which note to give. (1) ICRL Preset: Just go back and forth -- explore, then exploit, then explore again, and so on.
[Line 507] In ICRL Preset, on even-numbered rounds (round 0, 2, 4, ...), the model gets the "explore" note.
[Line 508] On odd-numbered rounds (round 1, 3, 5, ...), the model gets the "exploit" note. (2) ICRL Autonomous: Every round, you give the model the "your choice" note and let it decide for itself whether to try something new or stick with what's working.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] Now for the really cool part -- the researchers came up with something called the ICRL prompting framework (they show it in an Algorithm and a Figure). Think of it like a recipe with a few key ingredients.
[Line 443] The LLM Is the Brain.
[Line 444] The LLM (they call it pi_theta) is like the brain of the system -- it's the one making all the decisions.
[Line 445] The whole idea is to give the LLM a task and let it figure out how to solve it through prompting.
[Line 446] First, you need to describe the task in plain words. They call this description s_task (it's part of a bigger set called S).
[Line 447] Every time a new round starts, the researchers build a prompt that includes: what the LLM tried before, the scores it got on those tries, the task description, and some special instructions called s_ICRL.
[Line 450] How exactly they put all these past tries together and what those special instructions say will be explained a bit later.
[Line 451] Once the LLM gets this prompt, it comes up with a new answer.
[Line 452] Then the answer and the score get saved so they can be used in the next round.
[Line 461] The Scoring System.
[Line 461] After each step (called S_t) in a round, the LLM gets a score -- just a single number.
[Line 462] Sometimes you only get a score at the very end (that's called "sparse"), and sometimes you get scores along the way too (that's called "dense").
[Line 465] Where does the score come from? It could follow strict rules, come from a separate trained model, or even come from the LLM grading its own work.
[Line 466] Being able to use the LLM as its own grader is great because it means this approach works for all sorts of different tasks.
[Line 467] Here's the important part: this number is literally the only feedback the LLM gets. No tips, no hints, no explanations.
[Line 469] But the LLM does know that the number is a score (a "reward").
[Line 470] How? They just write the word "Reward" before the number in the prompt. That's it!
[Line 472] If the score comes from external rules or a separate model, it's real outside feedback.
[Line 475] But if the LLM is scoring itself, there's no outside feedback at all.
[Line 477] Even without outside feedback, the LLM still gets better over time.
[Line 478] Why? Because grading work is easier than doing the work (most people agree on this).
[Line 479] But the researchers think that when the LLM grades itself, it probably can't get quite as good as when it gets outside feedback.
[Line 481] Remembering Past Tries.
[Line 481] There's a memory system (called buffer B) that keeps track of all the LLM's past answers and scores.
[Line 482] The big idea is that LLMs that have already been trained probably already know how to do this kind of learning -- they just need the right setup.
[Line 483] So the researchers pack as many past tries and scores into the prompt as they can fit.
[Line 485] The hope is that the LLM looks at all those past experiences and learns from them in real time, getting better and better.
[Line 494] ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] To help the LLM along, the researchers also add some extra instructions to the prompt at the start of each round.
How does exploration and exploitation work in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).
What ROUGE-recall score does ICRL achieve on unseen paper abstracts?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. Given only the title, the model generates the abstract. ROUGE-recall is used as both reward and metric. ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall over 200 iterations, vs 0.44 Best-of-1024, 0.45 Self-Refine, 0.46 Reflexion. This demonstrates ICRL learns from external rewards and is not limited by pre-training knowledge.
What are the exact win rates in creative writing?

In creative writing, ICRL achieves length-controlled win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, 93.81% against Best-of-N. These exact numbers come from Alpaca-Eval 2.
What are the Game of 24 results in ICRL?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.
What happens when rewards are zero in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' tests five ablations on Game of 24:

1. **Zero Rewards**: All rewards set to 0 → performance drops.
2. **Short Context**: Buffer is deque of length 3 → performance drops.
3. **Exploration Only** (no reward): → significantly worse.
4. **Exploitation Only** (with reward): → performs well.
5. **No ICRL Instruction**: → some variation.

Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just exploring and picking best (Best-of-N). The framework is robust to different prompt setups.
What kind of feedback does Self-Refine use?

Self-Refine uses textual verbal feedback — it asks the LLM to provide natural language feedback on its own response, then revise. It does NOT use a reward function. This contrasts with ICRL which uses only scalar reward signals.
What are the two ICRL instruction strategies?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).

[Line 389] Having ruled out all other explanations, we are left with one compelling narrative: deep within the neural network's forward pass, with its parameters theta_* held constant, something remarkable unfolds -- the network appears to carry out its own internal reinforcement learning algorithm, drawing upon the accumulated context C_t to craft each action A_t.
[Line 390] This fascinating process, where RL emerges spontaneously during inference through the forward pass alone, has come to be known as in-context RL.
[Line 391] Viewing LLMs Through the Lens of RL Agents.
[Line 391] There is an elegant way to understand how LLMs produce text: by seeing the entire token generation process as a reinforcement learning problem unfolding one token at a time.
[Line 392] In this view, the world the agent inhabits is defined by everything it has written so far -- that is the state -- and each decision it makes is simply choosing the next token -- that is the action.
[Line 393] The palette from which the model paints is V, the full vocabulary of all possible tokens.
[Line 395] From this vocabulary emerges a vast state space S = cup_i=1^infinity V^i, spanning every conceivable token sequence, while the action space A = V remains the selection of a single token.
[Line 396] The story begins at time step 0, when the model receives an initial prompt S_0 drawn from S.
[Line 397] In the authors' framework, this opening prompt S_0 sets the stage by describing the task at hand.
[Line 399] The LLM itself, carrying parameters theta, takes on the role of a policy and is written as pi_theta.
[Line 400] At each moment t in the unfolding sequence, the model surveys all the tokens it has generated so far -- the state S_t -- and selects a new token A_t by sampling from pi_theta(S_t).
[Line 403] With each new token chosen, the state grows: S_t+1 = [S_t A_t], the old sequence extended by one freshly generated token.
[Line 405] After each transition, a reward signal R_t+1 = r(S_t+1) emerges from the reward function r, reflecting the quality of the new state.
[Line 406] This generative process continues, token by token, until a time T arrives -- either because the response has reached its maximum permitted length, or because the model has produced A_T-1, a special end-of-sequence token signaling completion.
[Line 408] Whichever trigger comes first, the episode draws to a close. The terminal state S_T stands as the complete record of the interaction, weaving together both the original task description and the LLM's entire response.
[Line 411] The authors identify two fundamentally different types of reward functions.
[Line 412] The first is the sparse variety, known as the outcome reward model, where r(s) only speaks up at the very end -- it is nonzero exclusively when s is a terminal state.
[Line 414] The second is the dense variety, the progress reward model, where r(s) can provide feedback along the journey, assigning nonzero values to non-terminal states as well.
What is ICRL Autonomous?

ICRL Autonomous always provides the 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM itself decide which strategy to use. Unlike ICRL Preset which alternates on a fixed schedule, ICRL Autonomous gives the model agency to choose.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] Reinforcement learning (RL) formulates tasks as Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). An MDP is defined by a tuple containing: a state space S, an action space A, a reward function r: S -> R, an initial state distribution p_0 drawn from (S) (the set of all probability distributions over S), and a transition function p: S x A -> (S) that governs state dynamics.
[Line 340] At the beginning of an episode (time step 0), an initial state S_0 is sampled from the distribution p_0.
[Line 342] At each subsequent time step t, the agent, currently in state S_t, selects an action A_t according to a policy pi: S -> (A), where (A) is the set of probability distributions over A. Formally, A_t is sampled from pi(S_t).
[Line 344] Following the execution of A_t, the environment transitions the agent to a new state S_t+1 ~ p(S_t, A_t), and a scalar reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1) is returned.
[Line 346] This cycle of observation, action, and reward repeats until a designated terminal time step T, which concludes the episode.
[Line 348] The fundamental objective in RL is for the agent to learn a policy pi that maximizes the expected cumulative return, defined as J(pi) = E[sum_t=1^T R_t].
[Line 349] In deep reinforcement learning, the policy pi is typically represented by a neural network.
[Line 351] The parameters of this network are denoted theta, and the parameterized policy is written as pi_theta.
[Line 352] Standard RL algorithms learn by updating the parameter vector theta to improve policy performance.
[Line 353] Concretely, at time step t, the agent draws action A_t from pi_theta_t(S_t), the policy under the current parameters.
[Line 355] The algorithm then computes an update from theta_t to theta_t+1 using the trajectory data accumulated thus far: S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1.
[Line 356] At the next time step t+1, the action A_t+1 is generated from the updated policy pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1).
[Line 360] In summary, the learning dynamics of standard RL are captured by the evolution of the parameter sequence theta_t.
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL), a term first introduced in prior research, is a paradigm in which the RL process takes place entirely during the inference phase (the forward pass) of a neural network, with no updates to model parameters.
[Line 368] Under ICRL, the policy pi_theta receives an additional context input C_t, so that actions are generated as A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t).
[Line 369] Designing effective contexts C_t is an ongoing area of research. A common choice is to include all prior state-action-reward triples from the task.
[Line 370] Importantly, this context typically spans all previous episodes, not merely the current one.
[Line 371] ICRL assumes a pretraining stage in which the network parameters theta are trained across a broad distribution of MDPs.
[Line 373] The pretrained parameter values are denoted theta_*.
[Line 374] After pretraining, the resulting policy pi_theta_* is deployed on new, previously unseen tasks.
[Line 376] That is, in the new MDP, actions are sampled as A_t ~ pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t).
[Line 378] A critical feature of ICRL is that theta_* remains unchanged during evaluation.
[Line 379] Despite this, the quality of the actions A_t has been observed to improve as the context C_t grows over the course of the new task.
[Line 381] Because theta_* is held constant, any performance gain must originate from the expanding context.
[Line 383] This phenomenon is referred to as in-context policy improvement.
[Line 384] A striking property of this improvement is that it persists even when the test task falls outside the pretraining distribution. For example, researchers have demonstrated in-context policy improvement on bandit problems whose optimal arms are reversed relative to the pretraining bandits.
[Line 388] Consequently, in-context policy improvement cannot be explained by the hypothesis that theta_* has simply memorized the pretraining tasks.
What open-source models were tested with ICRL?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT) using open-source models: Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B thinking mode. ICRL consistently outperforms Self-Refine and Reflexion in all settings, with improvements of up to 10-20 points over the base model. For Qwen3-32B thinking mode, ICRL remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT.
How does ICRL differ from Self-Refine and Reflexion?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.
What is ICRL Preset?

ICRL Preset alternates between exploration and exploitation instructions. Even-numbered episodes use exploration (ask model to try something different). Odd-numbered episodes use exploitation (ask model to produce best response based on highest-reward previous attempts). ICRL Preset achieves 90% on Game of 24.
What are the ScienceWorld results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based benchmark with 30 science-experiment tasks in a multi-room environment. Rewards are sparse. GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy. ICRL prompting outperforms baseline methods by about 20% after enough iterations. ICRL also scales better than baselines in terms of test-time compute budget (in dollar amounts).
Does ICRL learn from rewards or just parametric knowledge?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. Given only the title, the model generates the abstract. ROUGE-recall is used as both reward and metric. ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall over 200 iterations, vs 0.44 Best-of-1024, 0.45 Self-Refine, 0.46 Reflexion. This demonstrates ICRL learns from external rewards and is not limited by pre-training knowledge.
Does exploration without reward work in ICRL?

No. In the Exploration Only ablation (no reward signal), performance is significantly worse than full ICRL. This proves ICRL's improvement is NOT just from exploring various responses and picking the best (Best-of-N). ICRL genuinely generates novel responses better than previous ones.
Who wrote the paper 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' and where was it published?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
ICRL achieves what percentage on Game of 24?
ICRL Preset achieves 90% success rate on Game of 24 after 50 trials. This is versus 49% for Best-of-N, 47% for Self-Refine, and 44% for Reflexion.
What length-controlled win rates does ICRL achieve in creative writing?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.
What is the key design principle of ICRL?

The key design principle of ICRL prompting in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is **minimality**. To ensure gains come from the LLM's emergent RL capacity (not auxiliary mechanisms), they exclude: textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, and engineered modules. The only supervision is the scalar reward. This complies with the reward hypothesis ('goals can be thought of as maximization of cumulative scalar reward') and the 'reward is enough' hypothesis ('intelligence can be understood as subserving maximisation of reward').

ICRL consistently surpasses Self-Refine and Reflexion in both Creative Writing and AIME at all context lengths, demonstrating superior performance per unit of compute.

[Line 1180] Conclusion.
[Line 1181] The findings of this study contribute to a growing body of literature establishing that reinforcement learning can emerge as an inference-time capability within large language models, an observation with significant implications for the field.
[Line 1181] The authors propose a minimal in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) prompting framework utilizing scalar reward signals, which demonstrates broad generalizability across multiple model architectures and general-purpose tasks, and which compares favorably against self-revision approaches documented in prior work.
[Line 1181] The authors note that a critical open question concerns the degree to which training-time interventions might further strengthen this emergent in-context RL capability, representing a promising avenue for subsequent investigation.
[Line 1181] Taken together, these results underscore the potential for developing more autonomous agents capable of exploration, adaptation, and self-improvement within open-ended environments through experiential learning processes.

[Line 496] The ICRL meta-instruction, denoted s_ICRL, is expressed in natural language and is incorporated into the initial prompt at each episode.
[Line 497] Three variants of this instruction are defined: (1) an exploration-oriented instruction (refer to corresponding Figure in the Appendix), (2) an exploitation-oriented instruction (refer to corresponding Figure in the Appendix), and (3) a dual-mode exploration-or-exploitation instruction (refer to corresponding Figure in the Appendix).
[Line 501] The exploration-oriented instruction directs the language model to produce a response that is distinct from all responses generated in prior episodes.
[Line 503] The exploitation-oriented instruction directs the language model to generate the highest-quality response possible, utilizing the information contained in prior responses that received the highest reward values.
[Line 504] Two scheduling strategies are implemented for instruction selection. (1) ICRL Preset: A deterministic alternation scheme is applied between the exploration and exploitation instructions across successive episodes.
[Line 507] Under ICRL Preset, the exploration instruction is selected when the episode index K is even.
[Line 508] The exploitation instruction is selected when the episode index K is odd. (2) ICRL Autonomous: The dual-mode exploration-or-exploitation instruction is uniformly applied at every episode, and the language model autonomously determines whether to explore or exploit based on its assessment of the current context.

[Line 389] Really, the only explanation that makes sense is that when the network (with its fixed parameters theta_*) does a forward pass, it's basically running some kind of RL algorithm internally -- it takes the context C_t and figures out what action A_t to produce.
[Line 390] People call this forward-pass RL "in-context RL" since it happens during inference, not training.
[Line 391] LLMs as RL Agents.
[Line 391] Here's a cool way to think about it: you can actually model how LLMs generate tokens as a reinforcement learning problem.
[Line 392] Basically, the "state" is all the tokens generated so far, and the "action" is picking the next token.
[Line 393] Let's say V is the set of all tokens the model knows about.
[Line 395] So the state space S is all possible sequences of tokens (S = cup_i=1^infinity V^i), and the action space A is just V -- a single token.
[Line 396] At the very start (time step 0), you feed in a prompt, which we call S_0.
[Line 397] In this paper, that starting prompt S_0 is just a description of whatever task you want the model to do.
[Line 399] We call the LLM with parameters theta "pi_theta" -- think of it as the model's policy.
[Line 400] At each step t, the model looks at all the tokens so far (S_t) and picks a new token A_t from its distribution pi_theta(S_t).
[Line 403] Then the state updates to S_t+1 = [S_t A_t] -- you just tack the new token onto the end of everything so far.
[Line 405] After that, a reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1) gets calculated based on a reward function r.
[Line 406] This keeps going until time T, which happens either when the response hits its max length or when the model outputs a special end-of-sequence token.
[Line 408] Either way, the episode is done. The final state S_T (the "terminal state") has both the original task description and whatever the LLM wrote.
[Line 411] There are two flavors of reward functions.
[Line 412] Sparse rewards (outcome reward model) -- where you only get a nonzero reward at the very end, when the state is terminal.
[Line 414] Dense rewards (progress reward model) -- where you can get nonzero rewards at intermediate steps too, not just at the end.
Is 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' by DeepMind?

It was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from the University of Virginia (UVA) and published at ICLR 2026. It should not be confused with the 2021 paper 'Reward Is Enough' by Silver et al. from DeepMind, which is a different paper.

The authors are Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, Yanjun Qi. It is a different paper from the 2021 'Reward is Enough' by Silver et al. from DeepMind.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 150] Reinforcement learning (RL) is a well-known computational framework designed for solving sequential decision-making problems, where an agent must learn to select actions over time in order to maximize a cumulative reward signal. It has been successfully applied across domains ranging from robotics to game playing.
[Line 150] A central and unexpected contribution of this work is the demonstration that RL-like learning dynamics emerge spontaneously during the inference process of large language models (LLMs). The authors introduce the term in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) to describe this phenomenon, distinguishing it from traditional RL which requires explicit training loops and environment interaction.
[Line 150] To reveal and systematically study this emergent capability, the authors propose a multi-round prompting methodology called ICRL prompting. This framework is intentionally simple in design: it requires no architectural modifications, no fine-tuning, and no access to model internals. Instead, it operates purely at the level of prompting and context construction, enabling self-improvement of the model at inference time.
[Line 150] The fundamental goal of ICRL prompting is to create conditions under which an LLM can perform reinforcement learning during its inference phase, thereby iteratively improving its responses on a target task. This is achieved by structuring the interaction so that the model can learn from its own prior attempts and their associated outcomes.
[Line 152] The mechanism proceeds as follows: after the model generates each response, it is provided with a numerical scalar feedback value, referred to as the reward. This reward provides a simple, quantitative assessment of the quality of the generated response and serves as the learning signal.
[Line 152] In each subsequent round of interaction, the LLM is re-prompted with a context that contains the full concatenation of all prior responses along with their corresponding reward values. This growing context effectively provides the model with a complete history of its previous attempts and their evaluations, analogous to an experience replay buffer in traditional RL.
[Line 152] A striking and consistent empirical observation is that the quality of the model's generated responses improves progressively as the context accumulates more historical data. This improvement is robust across different tasks, models, and reward sources.
[Line 152] This behavior strongly suggests that the LLM is performing optimization with respect to the scalar reward signal during inference. The pattern of progressive improvement driven by reward feedback is directly analogous to the core mechanism of reinforcement learning, albeit occurring entirely within the model's context window rather than through gradient-based parameter updates.
[Line 153] The authors conduct an extensive empirical evaluation of ICRL prompting across a deliberately diverse set of task domains: the Game of 24 (a combinatorial reasoning task), creative writing (an open-ended generation task), ScienceWorld (an interactive text-based environment requiring multi-step reasoning), and Olympiad-level mathematical problem solving from the AIME and HMMT competitions. Across all of these benchmarks, ICRL prompting achieves significant and consistent performance improvements relative to strong baselines including Self-Refine and Reflexion, both of which also leverage iterative refinement but without the explicit reward optimization framework.
[Line 153] Perhaps the most noteworthy finding is that ICRL prompting delivers performance improvements even when the reward signals are generated by the same LLM that is producing the responses, rather than by an external oracle or ground-truth evaluator. This self-reward setting is particularly significant because it eliminates the need for an external reward source, making the approach more broadly applicable. It also highlights a promising new paradigm for test-time scaling, where additional inference-time computation can be converted into genuine performance gains through the ICRL mechanism.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 511] Related Works.
[Line 513] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 514] Imagine you're playing a game and getting better at it just by thinking harder -- not by practicing over and over. That's basically what in-context RL tries to do: get smarter during inference (the "thinking" phase) instead of during training. This idea has been around for a while, but when someone gave it the name "in-context reinforcement learning" (ICRL), lots more people started working on it.
[Line 514] There are survey papers that explain all of this in more detail.
[Line 514] Most of the ICRL work so far has used small, specially built models -- think of them as tiny AI brains trained just for specific games or robot tasks. This falls under a bigger area called meta-RL.
[Line 514] Some researchers tried using big pretrained language models (LLMs) instead -- having them pretend to be game simulators or solve simple choice problems (bandits). But the LLMs needed a lot of extra help to work, and they still couldn't do as well as simpler, dedicated algorithms.

[Line 1700] AIME and HMMT are two of the most difficult mathematics competitions in the U.S. for high school students.
[Line 1700] They require not only strong mathematical knowledge but also advanced problem-solving skills.
[Line 1700] Training language models for reasoning has been one of the most effective methods for improving performance on these benchmarks.
[Line 1702] We aim to test whether reward signals derived from previous reasoning traces can improve subsequent reasoning attempts.
[Line 1702] To access these traces, we rely on open-source models.
[Line 1702] In addition to evaluating reasoning models on these benchmarks, we also include a broader set of open-source models to demonstrate the prevalence of ICRL capability across a wide spectrum.
[Line 1704] In this benchmark, the model is given a single question and asked to produce an answer.
[Line 1704] The only additional instruction concerns formatting: the model must place its final answer within <answer> </answer> tags.
[Line 1704] The dataset includes the ground-truth answer for each question.
[Line 1706] For the reward model, we use the model proposed by, which provides a denser signal than simply checking with SymPy whether the model’s output matches the ground truth.
[Line 1706] Using such a model aligns with our belief that the growing ecosystem of judge and reward models, primarily designed for reinforcement fine-tuning of language models, will also play a key role in enabling ICRL as a test-time scaling method.
[Line 1708] Evaluation.
[Line 1709] To verify correctness, we parse the model’s output and check whether its answer matches the ground truth answer provided in the datasets.
[Line 1711] Baselines.
[Line 1712] We compare our method with Reflexion and Self-Refine, the two strongest baselines from the other benchmarks.
[Line 1712] Their implementations are consistent with those used in the prior experiments.
[Line 1714] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1715] For attempts after the first, we insert the model’s previous answer (including reasoning or CoT tokens) into the context, along with its associated reward.
[Line 1715] We then prompt the model either to try a new method or to refine its best previous approaches.
[Line 1715] We truncate past answers to ensure that at least 32 prior attempts can fit in the context.
[Line 1718] Our method outperforms the baselines in almost all cases.
[Line 1718] For the reasoning mode of Qwen3-32B, our approach remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT, which is the harder benchmark.
[Line 1718] Notably, this is achieved using scalar rewards computed with three orders of magnitude less computation, while Reflexion and Self-Refine are allowed nearly twice the compute budget for generating long-CoT verbal feedback.

[Line 1699] Task Setup.
[Line 1700] The researchers turned their attention to two of America's most grueling mathematical arenas: AIME and HMMT, competitions that have long served as proving grounds for the nation's brightest high school mathematicians.
[Line 1700] These contests demand more than textbook fluency — they require the kind of creative, multi-step problem-solving that pushes even talented students to their limits.
[Line 1700] In the world of language models, training specifically for reasoning has proven to be one of the most powerful levers for improving performance on these storied benchmarks.
[Line 1702] The central experiment posed an intriguing question: could the echoes of a model's past reasoning, distilled into simple reward signals, guide it toward better solutions on subsequent attempts?
[Line 1702] To capture these reasoning traces, the team turned to open-source models whose thought processes could be examined.
[Line 1702] They cast a wide net, evaluating not just reasoning-focused models but a broad spectrum of open-source architectures, revealing that the capacity for in-context reinforcement learning was not a rare gift but a common trait.
[Line 1704] The task itself was elegantly simple: present one mathematical question, receive one answer.
[Line 1704] Only one rule governed the output format — the final answer had to appear within <answer> </answer> tags.
[Line 1704] Each question in the dataset arrived with its own ground truth, a known correct answer against which all responses would be measured.
[Line 1706] Rather than relying on the blunt instrument of SymPy-based answer matching, the researchers employed a purpose-built reward model from prior work that could provide a richer, more nuanced signal.
[Line 1706] This choice reflected a deeper conviction: that the burgeoning ecosystem of judge and reward models, forged in the fires of reinforcement fine-tuning, would find an equally vital role in powering ICRL as a strategy for scaling at test time.
[Line 1708] Evaluation.
[Line 1709] The verdict on each answer was rendered by parsing the model's output and checking it against the dataset's ground-truth answer.
[Line 1711] Baselines.
[Line 1712] Standing opposite ICRL were Reflexion and Self-Refine, the two methods that had proven strongest across the other benchmarks.
[Line 1712] Their implementations were carried forward unchanged from the prior experiments, ensuring a fair contest.
[Line 1714] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1715] After the opening attempt, each subsequent round enriched the model's context with its prior answer — reasoning traces and all — alongside the reward it had earned.
[Line 1715] The prompt then nudged the model: try a fresh approach, or sharpen the best of what came before.
[Line 1715] To keep the context from overflowing, past answers were trimmed so that at least 32 prior attempts could always find a place.
[Line 1718] The results spoke clearly: ICRL outperformed the baselines in nearly every configuration tested.
[Line 1718] Even in the reasoning mode of Qwen3-32B, the method stood shoulder to shoulder with Self-Refine on AIME and pulled ahead on the more formidable HMMT.
[Line 1718] Perhaps most striking was the efficiency — these gains came from scalar rewards requiring roughly a thousand times less computation, while the competing methods consumed nearly double the compute budget to produce their verbose chain-of-thought feedback.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 1721] Digging Deeper: Additional Analysis.
[Line 1723] Can ICRL Learn Things the Model Has Never Seen?
[Line 1725] One of the most interesting questions about ICRL is whether it's actually learning from reward signals or just getting lucky by searching through what the model already knows. To find out, the researchers designed a particularly clever experiment: they chose a task where the model's existing knowledge simply wouldn't cut it.
[Line 1728] Here's the setup: they collected 30 papers from arXiv that were published after GPT-4.1-mini's training data ended.
[Line 1728] The model is given nothing but the paper's title and asked to write the abstract.
[Line 1728] The aim is to reconstruct the original expert-written abstract as faithfully as possible.
[Line 1728] To measure success, they used ROUGE-recall -- essentially how much of the real abstract shows up in the generated one -- and this same score was fed back to the model as its reward.
[Line 1728] The beauty of this setup is that the abstracts genuinely aren't in the model's training data, so any improvement has to come from learning via the reward, not from dredging up memorized content.
[Line 1732] The results are telling. Best-of-1024 sampling only manages a 0.44 ROUGE-recall, proving that no matter how many times you sample, the model can't produce content it never learned.
[Line 1733] Self-Refine stalls at 0.45 after just a few rounds -- unsurprising since it has no external reward to guide it.
[Line 1733] Reflexion does marginally better at 0.46, but the pattern is almost identical to Self-Refine. This suggests its revisions rely heavily on the model's own verbal feedback, which just doesn't carry enough useful signal in this scenario.
[Line 1734] ICRL, however, tells a completely different story. It keeps improving steadily over 200 iterations and reaches 0.59 ROUGE-recall -- a huge gap over the baselines. This is strong evidence that ICRL genuinely learns from external rewards and can push past the boundaries of the model's pre-training knowledge.
What are the creative writing results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.
Explain ICRL Preset and ICRL Autonomous.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).
What is the exact ROUGE-recall for ICRL on unseen abstracts?
ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall on unseen paper abstracts over 200 iterations. Best-of-1024: 0.44, Self-Refine: 0.45, Reflexion: 0.46.

[Line 748] Experiments.
[Line 780] In this section, the ICRL prompting method is evaluated on three benchmark tasks: Game of 24, creative writing, and ScienceWorld. Each benchmark tests different aspects of language model capability, from mathematical reasoning to narrative generation to interactive scientific experimentation.
[Line 781] Several comparison methods are used as baselines: CoT-only prompting, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N sampling, Self-Refine, and Reflexion. These baselines represent a range of established approaches for eliciting improved responses from language models.
[Line 782] An important experimental design choice is that Self-Refine and Reflexion are allowed to accumulate prompt content up to the full context window of the LLM, ensuring that these methods are not artificially constrained relative to ICRL prompting.
What length-controlled win rates does ICRL achieve in creative writing?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.
What is the exact success rate of ICRL Preset on Game of 24?
ICRL Preset achieves exactly 90% success rate on Game of 24 after 50 trials. Not 94%, not 85% — exactly 90%. The baselines achieve: Best-of-N 49%, Self-Refine 47%, Reflexion 44%.

[Line 1073] Now let's talk about how ICRL performs on open-source models and Olympiad-level math.
[Line 1096] So the natural question is: does ICRL generalize? We tested this along two axes -- different models and harder tasks.
[Line 1096] On the model side, we ran ICRL on Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B in thinking mode, all on creative writing.
[Line 1096] On the task difficulty side, we went all the way up to Olympiad-level math -- AIME and HMMT problems.
[Line 1096] The experimental details are all in the Appendix if you want to reproduce this.
[Line 1096] And here's what we found: ICRL beats the baselines -- self-refine and Reflexion -- in every single setting. Every one.
[Line 1096] We're seeing 10 to 20 point improvements over the base model on both the creative writing and math benchmarks. That's huge.
[Line 1096] The bottom line: ICRL is not model-specific. It's a general, robust capability that works across architectures and handles even the toughest tasks.
Define ICRL as in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) is an inference-time paradigm where RL occurs during the forward pass of the network without parameter updates. The policy π_θ is conditioned on context C_t (e.g., all previous state-action-reward pairs). After pretraining, θ_* is fixed. Action quality improves as C_t grows — this is in-context policy improvement. This improvement occurs even on out-of-distribution tasks, ruling out memorization.

[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] Now let's move to our third benchmark: ScienceWorld. For those unfamiliar, this is an interactive, text-based benchmark with 30 science-experiment tasks set in a multi-room environment filled with all sorts of objects.
[Line 999] What makes this environment really hard? Three things. First, the rewards are sparse -- you barely get any signal. Second, the action space is huge. And third, you need actual scientific knowledge combined with smart exploration to succeed.
[Line 999] At each step, the agent sees what happened from its previous action. Importantly, it gets zero reward unless it completes one of the predefined subgoals.
[Line 999] Now here's something interesting about this particular benchmark: the reward signal serves both as our evaluation metric AND as the signal for inference-time self-improvement. In other words, r and r_* are the same thing here.
[Line 999] Complete all the subgoals, and you get a cumulative reward of 100 -- that's a successful episode.
[Line 999] But if you run out of steps or make an incorrect terminating action, the episode is over and it counts as a failure.
[Line 1000] The agent receives its task input s_task, which contains the environment description, the task objectives, and a template of all possible actions it can take.
[Line 1000] You can find a concrete example of s_task in the appendix.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] For evaluation, we use the reward function that comes built into the environment. It serves two purposes: building the context trajectories -- that's our r -- and evaluating the model, which is our r^*.
[Line 1002] We benchmark across all 30 tasks and aggregate the results.
[Line 1002] Every algorithm in our comparison uses GPT-4.1 mini as its policy.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] Let me walk you through the baselines. With Reflexion, the agent reflects on its attempt after each episode. That reflection is cleaned up and added to a reflection buffer, which feeds into the context for the next trial.
[Line 1004] Self-Refine takes a similar approach: it generates its own feedback, tacks it onto a trajectory summary, and puts it all in the buffer.
[Line 1005] Now, to keep things fair -- and this is important -- we actually give these baseline methods access to the current episode's reward signals before they generate reflections. ICRL doesn't get that advantage.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] Our setup is simple. One trial equals one episode.
[Line 1007] After each trial, we build the trajectory by concatenating actions, observations, rewards, and the final outcome -- success or failure -- and add it to the buffer.
[Line 1007] Since most episodes only produce a few rewards, we only include those.
[Line 1007] At the start of each new trial, we construct S_0 by putting together the task description S_task, all our collected trajectories, and the ICRL instruction S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] There's a full example of S_0 in the appendix.
[Line 1009] Let me show you the results. The mean return at each trial is plotted in the right panel of this figure.
[Line 1010] You can clearly see steady improvements for any method that uses some form of interaction history, which is exactly the principle behind ICRL prompting.
[Line 1011] And the punchline? ICRL prompting beats the baselines by about 20% once you give it enough iterations.
[Line 1013] Now, we wanted to be fair to efficient baselines like Best-of-N, so in the appendix we compare everything as we scale the test-time compute budget. And ICRL still comes out on top -- not just in terms of trials but also when you measure in actual dollars spent on computation.
