[Line 150] Reinforcement learning (RL) is a subfield of machine learning concerned with how agents should take actions in an environment in order to maximize a notion of cumulative reward, typically formulated as a sequential decision-making problem.
[Line 150] Research has demonstrated that RL-like behavior can emerge during the inference process of large language models (LLMs), without explicit training for such behavior. This emergent phenomenon has been termed in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL).
[Line 150] A multi-round prompting framework known as ICRL prompting was introduced to reveal and harness this capability. The framework provides a mechanism for LLMs to achieve self-improvement during inference.
[Line 150] ICRL prompting is designed with the objective of enabling LLMs to carry out reinforcement learning during their inference phase, thereby improving task performance iteratively without weight updates.
[Line 152] In the ICRL prompting procedure, the model receives a numerical scalar feedback signal, referred to as a reward, following each generated response.
[Line 152] During each subsequent round, the LLM is prompted with a context that includes the concatenation of all previously generated responses along with their associated reward values.
[Line 152] Empirical studies have shown that the quality of the model's responses improves consistently as the accumulated context expands with additional rounds of interaction.
[Line 152] This pattern of improvement suggests that the LLM is effectively optimizing for the scalar reward signal during inference, a behavior that is functionally comparable to reinforcement learning.
[Line 153] ICRL prompting has been evaluated on several benchmarks spanning diverse domains, including the Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and Olympiad-level mathematics competitions such as AIME and HMMT. The method has demonstrated significant improvements in performance compared to baseline approaches including Self-Refine and Reflexion.
[Line 153] It has been further observed that ICRL prompting yields performance gains even when the reward signals are generated by the same LLM, underscoring the method's potential as a paradigm for scaling model performance at test time.
Is ICRL just Best-of-N?

No. The ablation study proves ICRL is NOT just Best-of-N. The 'exploration only without reward' ablation shows significantly worse performance than full ICRL on running max metric. ICRL genuinely generates novel responses better than previous ones, not just selecting the best from a set of random attempts.
Does exploration without reward work in ICRL?

No. In the Exploration Only ablation (no reward signal), performance is significantly worse than full ICRL. This proves ICRL's improvement is NOT just from exploring various responses and picking the best (Best-of-N). ICRL genuinely generates novel responses better than previous ones.

Does ICRL learn from rewards or just parametric knowledge?

[Line 496] Q: What form does the ICRL instruction take? A: It is expressed in natural language and is denoted as s_ICRL.
[Line 497] Q: How many types of ICRL instructions are there, and what are they? A: There are three types: (1) the exploration instruction, which encourages trying new responses (shown in a Figure in the Appendix); (2) the exploitation instruction, which encourages refining based on past successes (shown in a Figure in the Appendix); and (3) the exploration or exploitation instruction, which presents both options for the model to choose from (shown in a Figure in the Appendix).
[Line 501] Q: What does the exploration instruction tell the model to do? A: It asks the model to produce a response that is different from all of its previously generated responses.
[Line 503] Q: What does the exploitation instruction tell the model to do? A: It asks the model to generate the best possible response by drawing on its prior responses that received the highest reward.
[Line 504] Q: What strategies are used to schedule these instructions? A: Two strategies are considered. (1) ICRL Preset: The framework alternates between exploration and exploitation instructions across episodes.
[Line 507] Q: When is the exploration instruction used in ICRL Preset? A: It is used when the episode number K is even.
[Line 508] Q: When is the exploitation instruction used in ICRL Preset, and what is the alternative strategy? A: The exploitation instruction is used when the episode number K is odd. (2) ICRL Autonomous: Instead of alternating, the combined "exploration or exploitation" instruction is given at every episode, and the LLM decides for itself whether to explore or exploit.

[Line 780] So let me walk you through our experimental setup. We evaluated ICRL prompting on three benchmarks — Game of 24, creative writing, and ScienceWorld — each chosen to stress-test a different dimension of language model capability.
[Line 781] Now, we didn't just compare against one baseline. We brought in five: CoT-only, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N, Self-Refine, and Reflexion. These cover everything from simple single-pass methods to sophisticated iterative approaches.
[Line 782] And here's a detail I want to emphasize: we gave Self-Refine and Reflexion every possible advantage. Their prompts were allowed to grow as long as the LLM's context window would accommodate. No artificial constraints.
Why is ICRL prompting called 'minimal'?

The key design principle of ICRL prompting in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is **minimality**. To ensure gains come from the LLM's emergent RL capacity (not auxiliary mechanisms), they exclude: textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, and engineered modules. The only supervision is the scalar reward. This complies with the reward hypothesis ('goals can be thought of as maximization of cumulative scalar reward') and the 'reward is enough' hypothesis ('intelligence can be understood as subserving maximisation of reward').
What happens when rewards are zero in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' tests five ablations on Game of 24:

1. **Zero Rewards**: All rewards set to 0 → performance drops.
2. **Short Context**: Buffer is deque of length 3 → performance drops.
3. **Exploration Only** (no reward): → significantly worse.
4. **Exploitation Only** (with reward): → performs well.
5. **No ICRL Instruction**: → some variation.

Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just exploring and picking best (Best-of-N). The framework is robust to different prompt setups.
How does the experience buffer work in ICRL prompting?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have innate ICRL ability. To activate it, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated as many as the context window allows into S_0. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.
Tell me everything about 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'. Cover contributions, methodology, experiments, and results.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.

[Line 596] Inference-Time LLM Self-Improvement: A Detailed Examination.
[Line 603] The landscape of inference-time self-improvement for large language models is populated by several prominent methods that share a common mechanism: natural language self-revision. Self-Refine operates by having the model generate textual feedback on its own output and then produce a revised version informed by that feedback. Reflexion extends this idea by maintaining a memory of verbal reflections on past episodes, using these accumulated insights to guide future attempts. Textual Gradient applies the metaphor of gradient-based optimization to the language domain, using verbal signals to indicate the direction of improvement.
[Line 603] All of these self-revision approaches share a critical vulnerability rooted in their dependence on the model's parametric knowledge. When the model encounters a task that lies at the boundary of or beyond its training distribution, the self-generated feedback becomes unreliable -- the model effectively hallucinates its critiques. The pernicious nature of this problem lies in its iterative amplification: each round of revision builds upon potentially flawed feedback from the previous round, creating a compounding error that progressively degrades output quality. This phenomenon, known as performance collapse, represents a fundamental ceiling on the effectiveness of language-based self-revision.
[Line 603] At a conceptual level, these methods implement a form of language-guided search through the space of possible outputs. The verbal feedback generated at each step functions as a new set of explicit instructions that direct the subsequent revision, constraining the model's exploration to directions specified by its own (potentially unreliable) assessment.
[Line 605] ICRL represents a fundamentally different paradigm for inference-time self-improvement. Rather than relying on rich verbal feedback, ICRL provides the model with only a numerical scalar reward -- a single number that indicates how good or bad a response was. No prescriptive instructions, no critique, no suggested revisions.
[Line 605] This minimalist feedback design places a significant cognitive burden on the model: it must autonomously identify patterns across its history of attempts and their associated reward values to determine what response characteristics correlate with higher rewards. This process of learning to maximize a scalar signal through experience is precisely the mechanism that defines reinforcement learning, making ICRL a genuine instance of RL occurring within the model's inference process.
[Line 605] A particularly significant advantage of the ICRL framework is the flexibility of its reward source. While the reward can be generated by the model itself (self-evaluation), it can also originate directly from the task environment -- for instance, a compiler verifying code correctness or a simulator reporting task completion. Environmental rewards provide a strong, unbiased source of verification that is immune to the hallucination problems that plague self-revision methods.
[Line 607] A parallel and complementary line of research has pursued inference-time improvement through structured search algorithms. Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) organizes the generation process as a tree and uses evaluation to prune unpromising branches. Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT) generalizes this to graph structures that support more complex reasoning topologies. Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) applies the well-established game-playing algorithm to language generation, balancing exploration and exploitation through statistical sampling. Intelligent Go-Explore combines the exploration strengths of the Go-Explore algorithm with language model capabilities.
[Line 607] Despite their ingenuity, these search-based methods share a common architectural pattern: they require substantial externally engineered infrastructure. Heuristic evaluation functions must be designed to assess intermediate states, memory management systems must be implemented to track explored paths, and various other components must be carefully tuned. None of these methods leverage the model's intrinsic capacity to learn from experience -- they instead impose an external structure for improvement. The present work also connects to the prompt optimization literature, where numerical evaluation scores are employed to iteratively refine prompts. This refinement process typically proceeds through strategies such as top-k selection (retaining only the highest-scoring prompt variants) and error filtering (removing prompts that produce incorrect outputs).
[Line 608] Upon closer examination, prompt optimization is more properly classified as a form of in-context supervised learning rather than reinforcement learning. Its mechanism -- selecting good examples and filtering bad ones -- closely mirrors filtered behavior cloning, where a supervised learning agent is trained only on demonstrations that meet a quality threshold.
[Line 608] ICRL occupies a distinct position in this landscape because it supports genuine learning from failure experiences. Where supervised approaches and prompt optimization discard or filter out poor-performing examples, ICRL retains them in the context and allows the model to extract useful information about what not to do. This ability to learn from negative as well as positive outcomes is a hallmark of reinforcement learning and a capability that none of the other described approaches fully realize.
What are the exact win rates in creative writing?

In creative writing, ICRL achieves length-controlled win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, 93.81% against Best-of-N. These exact numbers come from Alpaca-Eval 2.
Who wrote 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.

[Line 1180] Conclusion.
[Line 1181] This paper establishes that reinforcement learning constitutes an emergent capability inherent to large language models during the inference phase.
[Line 1181] We present evidence that our parsimonious, scalar-reward-driven in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) prompting framework activates this latent ability across heterogeneous model architectures and a broad spectrum of general-purpose tasks, yielding performance superior to that of self-revision approaches.
[Line 1181] A principal avenue for subsequent research involves examining the extent to which training-time modifications may serve to further augment this in-context reinforcement learning capacity within large language models.
[Line 1181] This remarkably potent capability suggests a trajectory toward increasingly autonomous agents capable of exploration, adaptation, and self-improvement within open-ended environments through experiential learning.
What is the 'reward is enough' hypothesis?

The 'reward is enough' hypothesis (referenced in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners') states: 'intelligence, and its associated abilities, can be understood as subserving the maximisation of reward.' The related reward hypothesis states: 'all of what we mean by goals and purposes can be well thought of as maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (reward).' ICRL prompting complies with both by using only scalar rewards.
What is the conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

The conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners': RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. The minimal, scalar-reward-based ICRL prompting framework unlocks this ability across diverse models and tasks, outperforming self-revision methods. A key future direction is investigating how training-time interventions might further enhance this in-context RL capability. This points toward more autonomous agents that can explore, adapt, and self-improve in open-ended settings by learning from their own experience.
How does exploration and exploitation work in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).

List all five ablations in the ICRL paper.

The five ablations tested on Game of 24 are: (1) Zero Rewards — all rewards set to 0, performance drops. (2) Short Context — buffer is deque of length 3, performance drops. (3) Exploration Only — no reward signal, performs significantly worse. (4) Exploitation Only — with reward, performs well. (5) No ICRL Instruction — s_ICRL removed entirely.
What are the ScienceWorld results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based benchmark with 30 science-experiment tasks in a multi-room environment. Rewards are sparse. GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy. ICRL prompting outperforms baseline methods by about 20% after enough iterations. ICRL also scales better than baselines in terms of test-time compute budget (in dollar amounts).
Can ICRL use self-evaluation as reward?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' supports two types of reward functions:

1. **Sparse rewards** (outcome reward model): r(s) is nonzero only at terminal states.
2. **Dense rewards** (progress reward model): r(s) can be nonzero for non-terminal states.

The reward function can be: rule-based, learned separately, or the same LLM for self-evaluation. With LLM self-evaluation there is no external feedback, yet performance still improves (evaluation is easier than generation). The authors hypothesize the ceiling with self-evaluation is lower than with external feedback.

Explain the ICRL prompting algorithm step by step.

The ICRL prompting algorithm from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners':
1. Start with task description s_task and empty experience buffer B.
2. Each episode K: construct S_0 = [s_task + buffer B contents + ICRL instruction s_ICRL].
3. LLM generates response. Store response and rewards in B.
4. Reward tagged with 'Reward: ' before the scalar number.
5. Repeat with updated buffer.
6. Two strategies for s_ICRL: Preset (alternate explore/exploit) or Autonomous (LLM decides).
Key principle: minimality — only scalar rewards, no verbal feedback.

GPT-4.1 is the policy LLM in Game of 24. GPT-4.1 is also the reward model (prompted differently), scoring each thinking step 0-3 for likelihood of reaching 24.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 304] Here’s the paper’s summary in simple terms. The researchers did three important things: (1): They created something called the ICRL prompting framework. Think of it as a very simple recipe that helps AI language models get better at tasks while they’re working on them, just by giving them a score (a number) that tells them how well they did.
[Line 306] To understand how it works, imagine you’re learning by looking at examples of questions and correct answers placed in front of you – that’s how regular in-context learning works with (x, y) pairs. Now imagine instead of just questions and answers, you get to see what situation you were in, what you tried, and what score you got. That’s what ICRL does – it puts these situation–action–score triples into the prompt, along with some simple instructions.
[Line 306] The beauty of this approach is that the AI doesn’t need any extra programs or special tools to make it work. It’s all happening inside the language model’s own brain, so to speak. (2): They showed strong evidence that the AI is actually doing something like reinforcement learning (learning from trial and error) all on its own during this process.
[Line 314] Here’s the proof: the AI tries to get higher and higher scores over time, it tries new approaches sometimes while sticking with good ones at other times (like how you might try a new restaurant sometimes but keep going back to your favorites), it does better when it has more history to learn from, it does worse when it has less history, and it does worse when you stop giving it scores.
[Line 319] All of these behaviors are exactly what a learning-from-trial-and-error system is supposed to do.
[Line 320] The researchers call this a "duck test" – if it looks like learning and acts like learning, it probably is learning.
[Line 322] They also proved that their method works better than other popular methods called Self-Refine and Reflexion on a bunch of different tasks: a math puzzle game called Game of 24, creative writing, a science simulation called ScienceWorld, and really hard math competition problems (AIME and HMMT).
[Line 322] And here’s the surprising part: for the Game of 24 and creative writing, the AI was giving itself its own scores (no human or external judge needed), and it still kept getting better. That’s pretty cool.
What does 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' contribute?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
Why does ICRL outperform Self-Refine?

ICRL outperforms Self-Refine because Self-Refine relies on verbal self-revision which is prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations, leading to performance collapse. ICRL uses only scalar rewards without verbal feedback, avoiding this problem. The model infers improvements by recognizing patterns from past experience.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] So, RL basically uses something called Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) to set up a task. An MDP has a few key pieces: a state space S (all the situations you can be in), an action space A (all the things you can do), a reward function r: S -> R (tells you how good a state is), an initial distribution p_0 over S (where you start), and a transition function p: S x A -> (S) (how the world changes when you act).
[Line 340] Things kick off at time step 0, when you get your starting state S_0 by drawing from p_0.
[Line 342] Then at any time t, the agent is sitting in state S_t and picks an action A_t based on its policy pi: S -> (A), which is basically a rule that maps states to probabilities over actions. So A_t comes from pi(S_t).
[Line 344] Once the agent takes action A_t, it moves to a new state S_t+1 drawn from p(S_t, A_t) and gets a reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1).
[Line 346] This back-and-forth between the agent and the environment keeps going until some time T, which is when the episode wraps up.
[Line 348] The whole point is for the agent to tweak its policy pi so that the total expected reward J(pi) = [sum_t=1^T R_t] is as high as possible.
[Line 349] In modern deep RL, the policy pi is typically implemented as a neural network.
[Line 351] We call the network's parameters theta, so the policy becomes pi_theta.
[Line 352] The way RL algorithms work is by updating theta to make the policy better.
[Line 353] For instance, at time t, you sample action A_t from pi_theta_t(S_t).
[Line 355] Then the RL algorithm updates the parameters from theta_t to theta_t+1 using whatever data it has collected so far -- things like S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1.
[Line 356] Next, at time t+1, you sample action A_t+1 from the now-updated policy pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1).
[Line 360] So really, the whole RL process boils down to how theta_t gets updated over time.
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] ICRL is a newer idea (the name was first coined in prior work) where the RL process happens during the model's inference time -- that is, during the forward pass -- without ever changing the network's weights.
[Line 368] In ICRL, the policy pi_theta also takes in a context C_t, so the action is chosen as A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t).
[Line 369] How you build C_t is still an open question researchers are working on, but a common approach is to just throw in all the state-action-reward tuples the agent has seen so far.
[Line 370] And here's the thing -- this usually includes data from all previous episodes, not just the one the agent is currently in.
[Line 371] ICRL also has a pretraining phase where the network theta is trained on a bunch of different tasks (MDPs).
[Line 373] We call the pretrained parameters theta_*.
[Line 374] Once pretraining is done, the policy pi_theta_* gets tested on brand new tasks.
[Line 376] So in a new MDP, actions A_t are sampled from pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t).
[Line 378] The key thing is that theta_* stays completely frozen.
[Line 379] But despite that, the actions A_t get better as the context C_t grows in the new task.
[Line 381] Since theta_* doesn't change, the improvement must be coming from the growing context.
[Line 383] That's why it's called in-context policy improvement.
[Line 384] What's really cool is that this improvement shows up even when the new task is totally different from anything seen during pretraining -- like bandit problems where the optimal arms are the opposite of what the model was trained on.
[Line 388] So you can't just say theta_* memorized the training tasks and is replaying them.

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.

Explain the experience buffer in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have innate ICRL ability. To activate it, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated as many as the context window allows into S_0. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.

[Line 1030] Ablation Study.
[Line 1032] A comprehensive ablation analysis was performed to isolate the contributions of individual components within the ICRL prompting framework. Five experimental conditions were designed. (1) Zero Rewards: All scalar reward signals are set to zero, eliminating any informative feedback to the model. (2) Short Context: Under the standard protocol, the experience buffer B functions as a queue of effectively unbounded length.
[Line 1036] In this ablation, the buffer is constrained to a deque of length 3.
[Line 1037] This restriction ensures that only the three most recent episodes are available for constructing the initial prompt S_0. (3) Exploration Only: The model receives instructions to generate responses differing from those previously produced, using the exploration instruction as s_ICRL, while the reward signal is entirely withheld. (4) Exploitation Only: The exploitation instruction is uniformly applied as s_ICRL across all episodes, and the reward signal remains accessible. (5) No ICRL Instruction: The meta-instruction component s_ICRL is completely excised from the prompting procedure.
[Line 1058] The running maximum performance across all ablation conditions is reported in the accompanying figure.
[Line 1058] Among the conditions tested, the two complete ICRL methods and the exploitation-only variant incorporating reward signals achieve the most favorable performance profiles.
[Line 1058] These results attest to the robustness of the ICRL prompting framework under varying prompt architectures, consistent with the broader literature on prompt engineering resilience.
[Line 1058] Conversely, meaningful performance reductions are documented in the short-context and zero-reward configurations.
[Line 1059] Of particular interest is the finding that the exploration-only condition absent reward signals (rendered in green) yields substantially diminished running maximum performance when compared to the full ICRL approach.
[Line 1060] This evidence directly challenges the alternative hypothesis that ICRL gains stem from a diversification-and-selection strategy akin to Best-of-N sampling.
[Line 1060] The data instead support the conclusion that the ICRL framework facilitates the generation of genuinely novel, higher-quality responses that go beyond those encountered during the exploration phase.
What is the ArXiv ID of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

The ArXiv ID of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is 2506.06303. It was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from University of Virginia (UVA) and published at ICLR 2026.
Provide a detailed description of the paper 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.
What is the fundamental difference between ICRL and verbal feedback methods?

The fundamental difference is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. ICRL uses only numerical scalar rewards — no textual feedback, no instructions on how to improve. Self-Refine and Reflexion use verbal/textual feedback. Verbal feedback methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, while scalar rewards provide a clean learning signal.

[Line 1721] Additional Analysis Results.
[Line 1723] Unseen Paper Abstract Generation.
[Line 1725] A fundamental question regarding ICRL is whether it achieves performance improvements through genuine learning from external reward signals or through an implicit search process that draws upon the model's pre-existing parametric knowledge. To systematically investigate this distinction, the authors construct an experimental evaluation in which successful task completion cannot be achieved through parametric knowledge retrieval alone. The rationale is that if ICRL succeeds where parametric search fails, this provides strong evidence for genuine reward-driven learning.
[Line 1728] The experimental protocol involves the collection of 30 arXiv papers published after the training data cutoff of GPT-4.1-mini, ensuring that the model has not been exposed to these papers during pre-training.
[Line 1728] The model is provided exclusively with the title of each paper and is instructed to generate the corresponding abstract.
[Line 1728] The target is to produce an abstract that maximizes overlap with the ground-truth, expert-authored abstract.
[Line 1728] Performance evaluation utilizes ROUGE-recall, a metric that quantifies the proportion of n-grams in the reference abstract that appear in the generated text. Notably, this same ROUGE-recall score serves dual purpose as the external reward signal provided to the model during ICRL iterations.
[Line 1728] The experimental design ensures that these abstracts fall entirely outside the model's pre-training distribution. Consequently, any observed performance improvement must be attributed to the model's ability to learn from external reward feedback rather than to the retrieval or recombination of previously memorized textual content.
[Line 1732] Quantitative results reveal that Best-of-1024 sampling achieves a ROUGE-recall of merely 0.44, as depicted in the corresponding figure. This finding confirms that even extensive sampling from the model's base output distribution is insufficient to recover content that was absent from the training corpus, establishing a baseline for what parametric search alone can accomplish.
[Line 1733] Self-Refine converges to a plateau at 0.45 ROUGE-recall within a limited number of iterations. This rapid stagnation is directly attributable to the method's design, which relies exclusively on the model's self-generated textual feedback without incorporating external reward signals.
[Line 1733] Reflexion achieves a marginally higher score of 0.46 but exhibits a performance trajectory that closely mirrors that of Self-Refine. This similarity suggests that Reflexion's revision mechanism, while nominally incorporating reward information, remains predominantly governed by the model's self-generated verbal feedback -- a feedback modality that provides minimal actionable information when the task requires knowledge absent from the model's training data.
[Line 1734] In stark contrast, ICRL demonstrates sustained and monotonic improvement across 200 iterations, ultimately achieving a ROUGE-recall of 0.59. This represents a substantial improvement of 0.15 points over the best-performing baseline method. The magnitude and sustained nature of this improvement provide compelling evidence that ICRL possesses the capability to effectively leverage external scalar reward signals for genuine learning, enabling the model to systematically discover and incorporate information that lies entirely beyond the boundaries of its pre-training knowledge distribution.
What is the exact ROUGE-recall for ICRL on unseen abstracts?
ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall on unseen paper abstracts over 200 iterations. Best-of-1024: 0.44, Self-Refine: 0.45, Reflexion: 0.46.

[Line 150] Q: What is reinforcement learning? A: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a framework for solving sequential decision-making problems, where an agent learns to take optimal actions through interaction with an environment and feedback in the form of rewards.
[Line 150] Q: What surprising finding does this paper report? A: The paper discovers that RL behavior spontaneously emerges during the inference time of large language models (LLMs). The authors call this phenomenon in-context reinforcement learning, or ICRL.
[Line 150] Q: What method do the authors propose? A: They introduce a simple multi-round prompting framework called ICRL prompting, which enables LLMs to self-improve during inference without any parameter updates.
[Line 150] Q: What is the goal of ICRL prompting? A: The goal is to guide LLMs to perform reinforcement learning during inference so that they can autonomously improve their performance on a given task.
[Line 152] Q: How does the feedback mechanism work? A: After each response the model generates, it receives a numerical scalar value as feedback, which is called the reward.
[Line 152] Q: What happens in subsequent prompting rounds? A: The LLM is prompted again with a context that concatenates all of its prior responses together with the reward scores each response received.
[Line 152] Q: What pattern is observed across rounds? A: The quality of the model's responses consistently improves as more prior responses and rewards are accumulated in the context.
[Line 152] Q: How is this related to RL? A: The LLM is effectively optimizing for the scalar reward signal during inference, which is behavior directly analogous to reinforcement learning.
[Line 153] Q: On what tasks was ICRL prompting evaluated? A: It was tested on the Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and Olympiad-level math competitions including AIME and HMMT. It showed significant improvements over baselines like Self-Refine and Reflexion.
[Line 153] Q: Does ICRL prompting work with self-generated rewards? A: Yes, even when the reward signals come from the same LLM, ICRL prompting still improves performance. This highlights its promise as a new paradigm for test-time scaling.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 304] Q: What are the main contributions of this paper? A: The paper makes three contributions: (1): It introduces the ICRL prompting framework – a minimal design that enables LLMs to self-improve at inference time using only scalar rewards as feedback.
[Line 306] Q: How does the ICRL framework relate to standard in-context learning? A: Standard ICL places (x, y) input–output pairs in the context to guide the model. ICRL works similarly, but instead places state–action–reward tuples together with simple meta-instructions in context.
[Line 306] Q: Does ICRL require any external tools or code? A: No. A key property of the design is that it isolates the LLM’s intrinsic capacity for in-context reinforcement learning without relying on external code or engineered mechanisms. (2): The second contribution is strong evidence that RL-like behavior emerges during LLM inference with the ICRL prompting framework.
[Line 314] Q: What specific evidence supports the emergence of RL? A: Five observations are documented: the model maximises the scalar reward, exhibits an exploration–exploitation trade-off, improves with longer context, degrades with shorter context, and degrades when reward is absent.
[Line 319] Q: Why are those observations significant? A: Because all of them are precisely the behaviors one would expect from a reinforcement learning algorithm.
[Line 320] Q: How do the authors characterize this evidence? A: They describe it as a "duck test" for the inference process – if the behavior matches RL in all these respects, it is functionally RL.
[Line 322] Q: How does ICRL compare to other methods? A: ICRL prompting significantly outperforms self-revision methods such as Self-Refine and Reflexion across benchmarks including Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and Olympiad-level math (AIME and HMMT).
[Line 322] Q: Can ICRL work with self-generated rewards? A: Yes. In Game of 24 and creative writing, the rewards are generated by the LLM itself, and consistent performance gains are still observed.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 596] So let's talk about how we can make LLMs better at test time -- inference-time self-improvement.
[Line 603] Now, most of the existing work in this space uses what we call natural language self-revision. You've probably seen methods like Self-Refine, Reflexion, and Textual Gradient. They all share the same basic idea: get the model to critique its own output in words, then revise based on that critique.
[Line 603] But here's the thing -- and this is really important -- these self-critiques are only as good as what the model already knows. When the model doesn't truly understand the task, it starts making up feedback. And that hallucinated feedback? It snowballs. Each round gets worse, and eventually you see performance collapse. It's a serious problem.
[Line 603] If you zoom out and ask what these methods are really doing, they're performing language-guided search. The feedback is just new instructions for the next try.
[Line 605] Now, ICRL -- what we're proposing -- takes a completely different tack. We give the model just a number. A scalar reward. That's it. No verbal feedback, no instructions.
[Line 605] And the model has to figure out from its track record -- from looking at what it tried before and what scores those attempts received -- how to do better. That's reinforcement learning happening right there during inference.
[Line 605] And here's a really nice property: these rewards can come straight from the environment. You don't need the model to self-evaluate. You get real, trustworthy signals.
[Line 607] Now, there's also a whole family of search-based approaches: Tree-of-Thoughts, Graph-of-Thoughts, MCTS, Intelligent Go-Explore. These are clever methods, but they all require a lot of external engineering -- custom heuristics, memory systems. They're not tapping into what the model can inherently do.
[Line 607] Our work also connects to prompt optimization, where you use numerical scores to iteratively improve prompts through things like top-k selection and error filtering.
[Line 608] But I'd argue that's really in-context supervised learning -- think filtered behavior cloning -- not reinforcement learning.
[Line 608] The key differentiator for ICRL? It can learn from failures. And that makes all the difference.

- These benchmarks span mathematical problem-solving, narrative generation, and interactive scientific exploration

- These cover a range of approaches from simple single-pass methods to iterative self-improvement techniques
[Line 782] - Important experimental condition: Self-Refine and Reflexion are allowed unrestricted prompt growth up to the LLM's full context window
- This ensures no artificial disadvantage for these baseline methods
How does ICRL differ from Self-Refine and Reflexion?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.

Who wrote the paper 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' and where was it published?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
Provide a detailed description of the paper 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.

What are the creative writing results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.

[Line 1030] Ablation Study.
[Line 1032] Imagine you have a recipe with five ingredients, and you want to know which ones really matter. That's basically what the researchers did here -- they removed or changed different parts of ICRL prompting to see what happens. (1) Zero Rewards: They made all the rewards equal to 0, so the model gets feedback that tells it nothing useful. (2) Short Context: Normally, the system remembers all of its past tries in a big list (buffer B) that keeps growing forever.
[Line 1036] They changed this so it only remembers its last 3 tries (a short list of length 3).
[Line 1037] So when setting up for a new try, the model only knows about its 3 most recent attempts. (3) Exploration Only: They told the model "just try something different from before" using the exploration instruction as s_ICRL, but didn't give it any reward scores at all. (4) Exploitation Only: They always told the model "do your best based on past rewards" using the exploitation instruction as s_ICRL, and kept the reward scores. (5) No ICRL Instruction: They took away all the special instructions (s_ICRL) entirely.
[Line 1058] The results, shown as the best score achieved over time (running max), are plotted in a figure.
[Line 1058] The two full ICRL methods and the exploitation-only version with rewards all did the best.
[Line 1058] This means the system works well even if you change how the prompts are set up.
[Line 1058] But when they used too short a memory or took away the rewards, performance got worse.
[Line 1059] The really important discovery: when the model just explores without rewards (the green line), it does much worse than the full method over time.
[Line 1060] This proves that ICRL isn't just trying lots of different things and picking the best one (like Best-of-N).
[Line 1060] Instead, ICRL actually creates brand new answers that are better than anything it came up with while just exploring.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 158] Introduction.
[Line 164] Imagine you have a really smart AI assistant. For it to handle new tasks it hasn't practiced before, it needs to get better while it's working -- like how you get better at a game the more you play it. Scientists call this test-time scaling.
[Line 164] There are two main ways to make AI systems better with more effort: learning and search. Both methods have already beaten humans at Chess and Go.
[Line 164] The search approach has been used a lot to help LLMs improve while they work. Think of it like trying many different answers and picking the best one -- that's the simplest version (Best-of-N). More clever versions include Tree of Thoughts and Monte Carlo Tree Search.
[Line 166] But the learning approach? It hasn't gotten as much attention for making LLMs better while they run.
[Line 166] The usual kind of learning you can do during inference is called in-context learning, but it needs someone to show the AI examples of the right answers -- like a teacher showing you how to solve a math problem.
[Line 166] The trouble is, you can't easily get more examples from a teacher while the AI is working on something new. That's a big limitation.
[Line 166] So the AI needs to learn from its own attempts instead -- kind of like teaching yourself.
[Line 169] That's where reinforcement learning comes in. RL is basically the best method we have for an AI to improve all by itself, without needing a human teacher.
[Line 169] The catch is, RL has mostly worked well in video games and simulations, or when training LLMs -- not when they're actually being used.
[Line 169] The real world (the "big world") is way more complex than any simulation. It's like the difference between playing a simple board game and navigating real life.
[Line 169] In the real world, the AI will run into tons of situations it was never prepared for and needs to figure them out right then and there.
[Line 169] To work in the real world, AI needs two things: (1) it needs to handle all kinds of tasks using everyday language, and (2) it needs to keep getting better while running, without going back to expensive training.
[Line 177] So the big question is: can an LLM teach itself using RL while it's generating text? Can it learn on the job?
[Line 177] If yes, that's a great solution! The LLM starts with everything it already knows, and then the RL part helps it keep improving.
[Line 177] Scientists already discovered something surprising: LLMs can learn from examples shown to them in their prompt (in-context supervised learning). That got people wondering if RL could work the same way.
[Line 177] But the existing research on in-context RL has only tried simple problems -- not the real, complicated tasks that need language.
[Line 183] This paper shows that LLMs really can do in-context RL! And it works on all sorts of language tasks -- science experiments, creative writing, and really hard math competitions.
[Line 183] The method is called ICRL prompting, and it's surprisingly simple.
[Line 184] The idea is to help LLMs do reinforcement learning to get better at a task while they work on it.
[Line 185] You start by just telling the AI what the task is.
[Line 187] After it gives you an answer, you give it a score -- just a number telling it how well it did.
[Line 190] Then you ask it again, but this time you also show it all its previous answers and their scores.
[Line 192] You keep doing this over and over.
[Line 193] And guess what? The answers keep getting better each time!
[Line 194] The AI is basically learning to get higher scores while it runs -- just like a reinforcement learning algorithm, but without any retraining.
[Line 199] The cool thing about ICRL prompting is how simple it is on purpose.
[Line 199] The researchers didn't add any fancy tricks -- no special feedback, no clever replay systems, no extra tools. They wanted to prove the LLM itself can do RL, not that some clever engineering helps.
[Line 199] The only thing the model gets is that one number -- the score.
[Line 199] This fits with two big ideas in AI: the reward hypothesis (all goals are really about getting the highest total score) and the "reward is enough" hypothesis (intelligence itself is about maximizing rewards).
Who is the last author of the ICRL paper?

The last author of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is Yanjun Qi. Full list: Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, Yanjun Qi. UVA. ICLR 2026.

[Line 1721] Additional Analysis Results.
[Line 1723] Unseen Paper Abstract Generation.
[Line 1725] A critical question in evaluating ICRL is whether its performance gains stem from genuine learning via external rewards or merely from searching over the model's stored parametric knowledge. To address this, a controlled experiment is designed in which the task specifically requires knowledge absent from the model's training data, making parametric search an inadequate strategy.
[Line 1728] The experimental setup involves collecting 30 arXiv papers published after the training cutoff of GPT-4.1-mini.
[Line 1728] The model receives only the paper title and must generate the corresponding abstract.
[Line 1728] The learning objective is to produce an abstract that matches the unseen, expert-written version as closely as possible.
[Line 1728] The evaluation metric is ROUGE-recall, computed between the generated and ground-truth abstracts; this same score is also used as the external reward function.
[Line 1728] Because the target abstracts are not part of the model's pre-training corpus, high performance can only be achieved through learning from external feedback, not through retrieval of memorized content.
[Line 1732] Experimental results show that Best-of-1024 sampling achieves only 0.44 ROUGE-recall (see Figure), confirming that brute-force search over the model's output distribution fails to recover content not present in the training data.
[Line 1733] Self-Refine converges quickly to a plateau at 0.45, owing to its reliance on self-generated feedback without external reward information.
[Line 1733] Reflexion achieves a slightly higher score of 0.46 but follows a similar trajectory, indicating that its revision mechanism is primarily driven by the model's own verbal self-assessment, which provides limited signal for this particular task.
[Line 1734] In contrast, ICRL demonstrates sustained improvement over 200 iterations, achieving a ROUGE-recall of 0.59. This substantially higher score demonstrates that ICRL can learn effectively from external scalar rewards and is not limited to the information encoded during pre-training.
Why is ICRL prompting called 'minimal'?

The key design principle of ICRL prompting in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is **minimality**. To ensure gains come from the LLM's emergent RL capacity (not auxiliary mechanisms), they exclude: textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, and engineered modules. The only supervision is the scalar reward. This complies with the reward hypothesis ('goals can be thought of as maximization of cumulative scalar reward') and the 'reward is enough' hypothesis ('intelligence can be understood as subserving maximisation of reward').
What ROUGE-recall score does ICRL achieve on unseen paper abstracts?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. Given only the title, the model generates the abstract. ROUGE-recall is used as both reward and metric. ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall over 200 iterations, vs 0.44 Best-of-1024, 0.45 Self-Refine, 0.46 Reflexion. This demonstrates ICRL learns from external rewards and is not limited by pre-training knowledge.
Why does ICRL outperform Self-Refine?

ICRL outperforms Self-Refine because Self-Refine relies on verbal self-revision which is prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations, leading to performance collapse. ICRL uses only scalar rewards without verbal feedback, avoiding this problem. The model infers improvements by recognizing patterns from past experience.

[Line 919] Task Setup.
[Line 919] We adopt the creative writing benchmark introduced in prior work, wherein four sentences are drawn at random from a curated sentence pool.
[Line 919] The objective for the LLM is to produce four coherent paragraphs, each concluding with one of the designated sentences, such that the resulting passage maintains overall textual coherence.
[Line 919] This task presents considerable difficulty, as it requires the LLM to construct a unified narrative arc that provides logical justification for each of the four sampled sentences within a single cohesive story.
[Line 919] The evaluation encompasses a total of 100 problems.
[Line 919] A representative example of s_task is provided in the corresponding Figure in the Appendix.
[Line 935] Evaluation.
[Line 936] Output quality was assessed using the Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2 framework, which serves as a well-established proxy for human evaluation, exhibiting Pearson correlation coefficients of up to 0.98 with human judgments.
[Line 936] For the 100 creative writing problems, we present the top-performing response from each method: for Reflexion and Best-of-N, we select the output with the highest reward across 50 trials; for ICRL and Self-Refine, we present the output from the 50th episode.
[Line 936] Alpaca-Eval subsequently computes pairwise win rates, which we denote as r_*.
[Line 938] We now describe the reward function r that is made available to the algorithms.
[Line 938] Following established pairwise comparison protocols, we employ GPT-4.1 with a coherent reference paragraph to assign each response a score on a scale of 1 to 10 (see the corresponding Figure).
[Line 938] It is noteworthy that although both r and r_* employ an LLM as an adjudicator, they fulfill distinct roles. The function r evaluates responses against a fixed reference text with particular emphasis on coherence.
[Line 938] In contrast, r_* conducts pairwise comparisons between responses generated by our method and those produced by a baseline method.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] We benchmark our method against Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 956] Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting are permitted access to the reward function r.
[Line 957] Self-Refine does not utilize r; instead, it solicits verbal feedback from GPT-4.1.
[Line 958] Given the difficulty in differentiating between CoT and Long-CoT style prompting for this particular task, we incorporate Long-CoT style prompting as an additional baseline.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] GPT-4.1 serves simultaneously as the policy LLM pi_theta and the reward model r within the Algorithm.
[Line 959] At each episode, the initial prompt S_0 is formed by concatenating all preceding generations together with their corresponding coherence scores derived from r.
[Line 960] It should be noted that this reward is sparse, with only R_T taking nonzero values.
[Line 961] Consequently, only R_T is incorporated in the construction of S_0.
[Line 963] Our method attains a length-controlled win rate of 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT style prompting, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N, as reported in the Table.
[Line 963] These results demonstrate that the responses produced by our method surpass those of the baselines with respect to adherence to the instruction of generating coherent paragraphs and alignment with human preference.
[Line 964] The return curve obtained from the reward model r is depicted in the Figure, with a running maximum of the return presented in the Figure in the Appendix.
[Line 964] Although Self-Refine initially achieves comparable coherence reward to ICRL, extending both methods by an additional 50 episodes reveals that our methods continue to improve, whereas Self-Refine first reaches a plateau and subsequently declines, a phenomenon likely attributable to the substantial growth of its context window.
What kind of feedback does Reflexion use?

Reflexion generates textual reflection based on the reward signal. Unlike ICRL which uses the scalar reward directly, Reflexion converts the reward into verbal feedback. ICRL uses no verbal feedback at all.
What is ICRL's win rate against Self-Refine in creative writing?
ICRL achieves 86.32% length-controlled win rate against Self-Refine in creative writing. Against Reflexion it's 59.48%, against Long-CoT 78.36%, against Best-of-N 93.81%.
How does ICRL perform on AIME and HMMT?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT) using open-source models: Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B thinking mode. ICRL consistently outperforms Self-Refine and Reflexion in all settings, with improvements of up to 10-20 points over the base model. For Qwen3-32B thinking mode, ICRL remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT.
List all seven authors of the ICRL paper.

The seven authors of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' are: 1. Kefan Song, 2. Amir Moeini, 3. Peng Wang, 4. Lei Gong, 5. Rohan Chandra, 6. Shangtong Zhang, 7. Yanjun Qi. All from the University of Virginia (UVA). Published at ICLR 2026.
What does 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' contribute?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
Explain ICRL Preset and ICRL Autonomous.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).
What are the exact win rates in creative writing?

In creative writing, ICRL achieves length-controlled win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, 93.81% against Best-of-N. These exact numbers come from Alpaca-Eval 2.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT) using open-source models: Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B thinking mode. ICRL consistently outperforms Self-Refine and Reflexion in all settings, with improvements of up to 10-20 points over the base model. For Qwen3-32B thinking mode, ICRL remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT.
What does 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' contribute?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
What are the two ICRL instruction strategies?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).

(1) Zero Rewards: All reward values are set to 0, removing informative feedback from the system. (2) Short Context: In the standard algorithm, the experience buffer B operates as a queue with effectively unlimited capacity.
[Line 1036] In this ablation, the buffer is replaced with a deque of fixed length 3.
[Line 1037] This means that only the most recent 3 episodes contribute to the construction of S_0. (3) Exploration Only: The model is prompted to generate responses different from its prior outputs, using the exploration instruction as s_ICRL, but no reward signal is provided. (4) Exploitation Only: The exploitation instruction is always used as s_ICRL, and the reward signal is included. (5) No ICRL Instruction: The meta-instruction s_ICRL is completely removed from the prompt.
[Line 1058] Figure presents the running maximum results for the ablation study.
[Line 1058] The two proposed ICRL methods and the exploitation-only condition with reward signals all produce the strongest performance curves.
[Line 1058] This indicates that the ICRL prompting framework exhibits considerable robustness to variations in prompt design.
[Line 1058] Additionally, measurable performance degradation is observed when the context length is restricted or when reward signals are removed.

[Line 1060] This result establishes that the gains achieved by ICRL are not attributable to a strategy of generating diverse responses and then selecting the best among them, as would occur in Best-of-N sampling.
[Line 1060] Instead, the ICRL framework enables the generation of novel responses that exceed the quality of any response produced during the exploration phase alone.
Explain the ICRL prompting algorithm step by step.

The ICRL prompting algorithm from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners':
1. Start with task description s_task and empty experience buffer B.
2. Each episode K: construct S_0 = [s_task + buffer B contents + ICRL instruction s_ICRL].
3. LLM generates response. Store response and rewards in B.
4. Reward tagged with 'Reward: ' before the scalar number.
5. Repeat with updated buffer.
6. Two strategies for s_ICRL: Preset (alternate explore/exploit) or Autonomous (LLM decides).
Key principle: minimality — only scalar rewards, no verbal feedback.

Tell me everything about 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'. Cover contributions, methodology, experiments, and results.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.
What is 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' about?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
What kind of feedback does Reflexion use?

Reflexion generates textual reflection based on the reward signal. Unlike ICRL which uses the scalar reward directly, Reflexion converts the reward into verbal feedback. ICRL uses no verbal feedback at all.

[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] This section presents the ICRL prompting framework (Algorithm, Figure), the central contribution of this work. The framework is composed of the following key components.
[Line 443] The LLM as the Policy Network.
[Line 444] Definition: A large language model, denoted pi_theta, serves as the policy network in this framework.
[Line 445] The primary objective is to prompt the LLM so that it can solve a given task.
[Line 446] Assumption: A natural language description of the task is available and is denoted as s_task in S.
[Line 447] Episode initialization: At the start of each episode, the initial prompt is formed by concatenating (1) the LLM's previous attempts with their corresponding rewards, (2) the task description, and (3) a set of meta-instructions denoted s_ICRL.
[Line 450] The specific concatenation procedure and the choice of meta-instructions are discussed in a later subsection.
[Line 451] Given this initial prompt, the LLM produces a response.
[Line 452] The response and associated rewards are then stored in a buffer for use in future episodes.
[Line 461] The Reward Function.
[Line 461] Definition: A numerical scalar reward is provided for each state S_t encountered during the episode.
[Line 462] The reward can take two forms: sparse (only the terminal reward R_T is nonzero) or dense (rewards may be nonzero at intermediate states).
[Line 465] Implementation: The reward function can be (a) rule-based, (b) a separately learned model, or (c) the same LLM used for self-evaluation.
[Line 466] Using the LLM's self-evaluation as the reward function provides broad applicability, enabling ICRL prompting to generalize across many different tasks.
[Line 467] Key constraint: The scalar reward is the only feedback signal given to the LLM.
[Line 469] The LLM is informed that the scalar value represents a reward.
[Line 470] This is done by prefixing the numerical value with the label "Reward" in the initial prompt.
[Line 472] When the reward function is rule-based or independently learned, the resulting signal constitutes external feedback.
[Line 475] When the LLM evaluates its own output, no external feedback is present in the framework.
[Line 477] Despite the absence of external feedback, the LLM's response quality is expected to improve over episodes.
[Line 478] Rationale: This expectation is based on the widely accepted principle that evaluation is easier than generation.
[Line 479] Caveat: The performance ceiling under self-evaluation is hypothesized to be lower than under external feedback.
[Line 481] Memory for Experience.
[Line 481] An experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes on the task.
[Line 482] Working hypothesis: The pretrained LLM already possesses the latent capability for ICRL.
[Line 483] To activate this capability, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated into the context up to the maximum allowed by the context window.
[Line 485] The expectation is that the LLM performs reinforcement learning from these in-context experiences during inference.
[Line 494] ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] To support the LLM's inference-time reinforcement learning, additional instructions are included in the initial prompt S_0 at the beginning of each episode.
How does ICRL perform on Game of 24 in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.

What are the creative writing results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.

[Line 304] This section summarizes the three key contributions of the present work: (1): The first contribution is the introduction of the ICRL prompting framework. This framework adopts a minimal design philosophy, enabling large language models to engage in self-improvement at inference time using only scalar reward signals as feedback.
[Line 306] The framework draws a direct parallel to in-context learning (ICL), which places input–output pairs (x, y) within the prompt context. In an analogous fashion, the ICRL framework places state–action–reward tuples, together with simple meta-instructions, into the context window.
[Line 306] A critical feature of this design is that it isolates the LLM’s intrinsic ability to perform in-context reinforcement learning, without reliance on external code modules or specially engineered mechanisms. (2): The second contribution is a body of strong empirical evidence supporting the claim that reinforcement learning behavior emerges at inference time when the ICRL prompting framework is applied.
[Line 314] The evidence encompasses several key observations: maximisation of the scalar reward signal, the emergence of an exploration–exploitation trade-off during inference, performance gains that increase with growing context length, performance degradation when context is shortened, and performance degradation when reward signals are removed.
[Line 319] These observations are fully consistent with the expected characteristics of a reinforcement learning algorithm, as studied in the classical RL literature.
[Line 320] This set of observations effectively constitutes a "duck test" for the inference process – an argument by analogy that the observed behavior is functionally equivalent to reinforcement learning.
[Line 322] The third contribution consists of empirical demonstrations showing that ICRL prompting produces significant performance gains over existing self-revision methods, specifically Self-Refine and Reflexion. These improvements are documented across a diverse set of benchmarks: Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and Olympiad-level mathematics competitions (AIME and HMMT).
[Line 322] An important additional finding is that in Game of 24 and creative writing tasks, where the reward signals are generated by the LLM itself rather than an external evaluator, consistent performance improvements are still achieved.
