[Line 596] Making Language Models Better Without Changing Their Weights.
[Line 603] Imagine you write an essay and then ask a friend to tell you what's wrong with it so you can fix it. That's basically what methods like Self-Refine, Reflexion, and Textual Gradient do -- they ask the AI to critique its own work using words and then try again.
[Line 603] The problem? The AI can only give feedback based on what it already knows. If it doesn't really understand the task well, it starts making stuff up in its critiques. And those made-up critiques pile up -- each round gets worse because the AI is building on bad feedback from the previous round. Eventually, the output gets worse instead of better.
[Line 603] At the end of the day, these methods are basically the AI telling itself step-by-step instructions on what to fix -- like a search guided by language.
[Line 605] ICRL does something much simpler. Instead of wordy feedback, it just gives the AI a number -- a score. That's the only hint the AI gets.
[Line 605] The AI then has to look at all its previous tries and their scores to figure out what works and what doesn't. It's learning from experience, just like how you might learn that touching a hot stove hurts (low score) and eating ice cream is great (high score). That's reinforcement learning!
[Line 605] Even better, these scores can come from the real world -- not just from the AI guessing how well it did -- so they're actually reliable.
[Line 607] There are also some other approaches that use smart search strategies to explore different possible answers -- things called Tree-of-Thoughts, Graph-of-Thoughts, Monte Carlo Tree Search, and Intelligent Go-Explore.
[Line 607] But these need a lot of custom-built parts to work -- special rules and memory systems designed by engineers. They don't really use the AI's own ability to learn.
[Line 607] There's also work on prompt optimization, where you use scores to gradually improve the instructions you give the AI, picking the best results and filtering out mistakes.
[Line 608] But that's more like learning from good examples only (supervised learning, specifically filtered behavior cloning), not real reinforcement learning.
[Line 608] What makes ICRL special is that it can learn from its mistakes too, not just from things that went well.

[Line 1180] Conclusion.
[Line 1181] Q: What is the main finding of this paper? A: The paper demonstrates that reinforcement learning naturally emerges as an inference-time capability of large language models, without any need for weight updates.
[Line 1181] Q: How does the proposed ICRL framework work, and how does it compare to alternatives? A: The ICRL framework uses a minimal, scalar-reward-based prompting approach that unlocks in-context RL across diverse models and general-purpose tasks, and it outperforms self-revision methods.
[Line 1181] Q: What do the authors suggest for future work? A: They propose investigating whether training-time interventions could further strengthen the in-context reinforcement learning capability in LLMs.
[Line 1181] Q: What are the broader implications of this discovery? A: This surprisingly effective capability points toward a future of more autonomous agents that can explore, adapt, and self-improve in open-ended settings by learning from their own experience.
What is ICRL Preset?

ICRL Preset alternates between exploration and exploitation instructions. Even-numbered episodes use exploration (ask model to try something different). Odd-numbered episodes use exploitation (ask model to produce best response based on highest-reward previous attempts). ICRL Preset achieves 90% on Game of 24.
How does the experience buffer work in ICRL prompting?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have innate ICRL ability. To activate it, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated as many as the context window allows into S_0. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.
What related work is discussed in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' discusses related work in: (1) In-Context RL — prior ICRL works used small models in games/robotics (meta-RL), not language tasks. (2) Inference-Time Self-Improvement — Self-Refine, Reflexion use verbal feedback, prone to hallucinated feedback causing performance collapse. Search methods (ToT, GoT, MCTS) use engineered components, not intrinsic learning. ICRL uses only scalar rewards, enabling learning from failure and leveraging the model's emergent capacity.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' comes from the University of Virginia (UVA). The authors are Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi. It was published at ICLR 2026.
What is ICRL Autonomous?

ICRL Autonomous always provides the 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM itself decide which strategy to use. Unlike ICRL Preset which alternates on a fixed schedule, ICRL Autonomous gives the model agency to choose.

[Line 511] Related Works.
[Line 513] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 514] In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) refers to the application of reinforcement learning algorithms during the inference phase of a neural network, without updating model parameters. The concept has its roots in earlier research on inference-time RL, and the formal coining of the term ICRL led to a period of rapid growth within the field.
[Line 514] Several survey articles provide a comprehensive overview of ICRL and related developments.
[Line 514] As a subfield of meta-reinforcement learning, the majority of existing ICRL research has involved small models trained from scratch, primarily in game-playing and robotic control domains.
[Line 514] Some researchers have explored the use of pretrained large language models (LLMs) in ICRL settings, for example as simulators or for bandit tasks. However, these approaches have generally required artificial interventions to function, and LLMs have not been shown to be competitive with standard algorithmic baselines in these contexts.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 150] You might know reinforcement learning (RL) as the branch of AI where agents learn by trial and error in sequential decision-making tasks. It's a powerful framework, but typically requires dedicated training.
[Line 150] Well, here's a twist: a new paper shows that RL actually emerges spontaneously during the inference of large language models. The researchers call this fascinating phenomenon in-context reinforcement learning, or ICRL.
[Line 150] To tap into this hidden ability, the team developed something called ICRL prompting -- a surprisingly simple multi-round prompting approach that lets models improve themselves at inference time.
[Line 150] The idea behind ICRL prompting is elegant: guide LLMs to effectively perform reinforcement learning while they're running, so they get better at tasks without any additional training.
[Line 152] Here's how it works in practice: after the model produces a response, it gets a numerical score -- a reward signal that tells it how well it did.
[Line 152] In the next round, the model is prompted again, but now the context includes all of its previous responses along with the rewards each one received. Think of it as giving the model a memory of its past attempts and how they scored.
[Line 152] The exciting finding? Response quality goes up consistently as you add more rounds. The more history in the context, the better the outputs get.
[Line 152] In essence, the LLM is learning to maximize a reward signal in real time during inference. That's reinforcement learning happening right before our eyes, no gradient updates required.
[Line 153] The researchers put ICRL prompting through its paces on a diverse set of challenges: the Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and even Olympiad-level math competitions like AIME and HMMT. It significantly outperformed established methods like Self-Refine and Reflexion across the board.
[Line 153] Perhaps the most striking result? Even when the LLM generates its own reward signals, ICRL prompting still delivers improvements. This points to an exciting new way to scale AI performance at test time without needing external evaluators.

[Line 390] This inference-time RL (happening in the forward pass) = in-context RL.
[Line 391] LLMs as RL Agents
[Line 391] Key insight: LLM token generation can be cast as an RL problem.
[Line 392] State = all tokens so far; Action = next token to generate.
[Line 393] V := set of all tokens (vocabulary).
[Line 395] State space: S = cup_i=1^infinity V^i (all finite token sequences). Action space: A = V.
[Line 396] t=0: initial prompt S_0 in S is given.
[Line 397] Here, S_0 = task description.
[Line 399] Notation: LLM with parameter theta written as pi_theta (the policy).
[Line 400] At step t: sample token A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t), where S_t = current token sequence.
[Line 403] State transition: S_t+1 = [S_t A_t] (concatenate new token to existing sequence).
[Line 405] Reward: R_t+1 = r(S_t+1), computed via reward function r.
[Line 406] Generation continues until time T: either max response length reached OR A_T-1 = EOS token.
[Line 408] Either condition => end of episode. Terminal state S_T contains task description + LLM response.
[Line 411] Two reward function types:
[Line 412] (1) Sparse (outcome reward model): r(s) != 0 only for terminal states.
[Line 414] (2) Dense (progress reward model): r(s) can be != 0 for non-terminal states too.
What is the 'reward is enough' hypothesis?

The 'reward is enough' hypothesis (referenced in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners') states: 'intelligence, and its associated abilities, can be understood as subserving the maximisation of reward.' The related reward hypothesis states: 'all of what we mean by goals and purposes can be well thought of as maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (reward).' ICRL prompting complies with both by using only scalar rewards.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 596] How LLMs Can Get Better Without Retraining.
[Line 603] If you've been following the LLM space, you've probably heard of methods like Self-Refine, Reflexion, and Textual Gradient. These all work by having the model critique its own output in natural language and then revise accordingly.
[Line 603] Here's the catch though: since these self-critiques are only as good as what the model already knows, they tend to produce hallucinated feedback that builds up over multiple rounds. Eventually, performance doesn't just stall -- it actively gets worse.
[Line 603] When you strip these methods down, they're essentially doing language-guided search. The feedback is really just a new set of instructions telling the model what to change.
[Line 605] ICRL flips the script. Instead of verbal feedback, it only gives the model a number -- a scalar reward. No instructions, no critique, just a score.
[Line 605] The model then has to look at its history of attempts and scores to figure out how to do better next time. If that sounds like reinforcement learning to you, that's because it basically is.
[Line 605] And here's a big plus: these reward numbers can come straight from the real-world environment, which means they're actually trustworthy, unlike self-generated critiques.
[Line 607] There's also a whole family of search-based approaches for making LLMs better at test time: Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT), Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), Intelligent Go-Explore, and others.
[Line 607] The downside? These all need carefully designed external components -- custom heuristics, memory systems, and other engineering. They don't really tap into the model's natural ability to learn.
[Line 607] There's also related work on prompt optimization, where you use numerical scores to iteratively improve prompts through top-k selection and filtering out bad examples.
[Line 608] But that's essentially in-context supervised learning (think filtered behavior cloning), not reinforcement learning.
[Line 608] The real advantage of ICRL? It can learn from its failures, not just its successes.
What funding supports 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is supported by the US National Science Foundation under grants III-2128019, SLES-2331904, and No. 2124538, the Coastal Virginia Center for Cyber Innovation (COVA CCI), and the Commonwealth Cyber Initiative (CCI).
Can ICRL use self-evaluation as reward?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' supports two types of reward functions:

1. **Sparse rewards** (outcome reward model): r(s) is nonzero only at terminal states.
2. **Dense rewards** (progress reward model): r(s) can be nonzero for non-terminal states.

The reward function can be: rule-based, learned separately, or the same LLM for self-evaluation. With LLM self-evaluation there is no external feedback, yet performance still improves (evaluation is easier than generation). The authors hypothesize the ceiling with self-evaluation is lower than with external feedback.

[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] So let me walk you through the main contribution of this paper -- the ICRL prompting framework. You can see it laid out in our Algorithm and Figure. It's built from a few key pieces, and I'll take you through each one.
[Line 443] The LLM as the Policy.
[Line 444] First up, the LLM -- we call it pi_theta -- acts as our policy network. It's the decision-maker.
[Line 445] The whole goal here is to prompt this LLM to solve a task.
[Line 446] We assume we have a natural language description of the task. We call it s_task, and it lives in the state space S.
[Line 447] Now, every time we start a new episode, we build the initial prompt by combining the LLM's past attempts with the rewards it got, plus the task description and some meta-instructions we call s_ICRL.
[Line 450] I'll get into the exact details of how we put past attempts together and which meta-instructions we use in a moment.
[Line 451] Once we feed this prompt to the LLM, it generates a response.
[Line 452] We then store both the response and the rewards in a buffer so we can reuse them in future episodes.
[Line 461] The Reward Function.
[Line 461] Next, let's talk about the reward. For each state S_t in the episode, the LLM receives a numerical scalar feedback -- just a number.
[Line 462] This reward can be sparse -- meaning only the terminal reward R_T has a value -- or dense, where rewards appear at intermediate steps too.
[Line 465] Where does this reward come from? Three options: it can be rule-based, come from a separately trained model, or -- and this is really interesting -- come from the LLM evaluating itself.
[Line 466] That self-evaluation option is what makes our framework so broadly applicable. You can use it on almost any task.
[Line 467] And here's a critical point: this scalar reward is the only feedback we give the model. That's it. No verbal explanations, no error messages.
[Line 469] But we do tell the LLM that the number is a reward.
[Line 470] How? We simply write "Reward" before the number in the prompt. Straightforward.
[Line 472] When we use rules or a separate model, the reward is genuinely external feedback.
[Line 475] But when the LLM is grading its own work, there's no external feedback at all -- the system is entirely self-contained.
[Line 477] And yet, we still see the LLM improving. Which is remarkable.
[Line 478] Why does this work? Because, as many researchers believe, evaluating is easier than generating.
[Line 479] We do suspect, though, that self-evaluation has a lower performance ceiling than external feedback.
[Line 481] The Experience Memory.
[Line 481] Now, how do we keep track of everything? We use an experience buffer B that stores all past responses and rewards.
[Line 482] Our hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have this ICRL capability -- it's just waiting to be activated.
[Line 483] To activate it, we pack as many previous attempts and rewards into the context as the window size will allow.
[Line 485] And what we expect is that the LLM can do reinforcement learning from those experiences right there in the context, during inference.
[Line 494] ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] Finally, to help the LLM along with this inference-time RL, we add some instructions to the initial prompt S_0 at each episode.
Which LLM is the policy model in Game of 24?
GPT-4.1 is the policy LLM in Game of 24. GPT-4.1 is also used as the reward model (self-evaluation). It was chosen for its excellent long-context capacity.
How does ICRL perform on ScienceWorld?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based benchmark with 30 science-experiment tasks in a multi-room environment. Rewards are sparse. GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy. ICRL prompting outperforms baseline methods by about 20% after enough iterations. ICRL also scales better than baselines in terms of test-time compute budget (in dollar amounts).
Give me a comprehensive summary of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.
Who wrote 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
What is the exact ROUGE-recall for ICRL on unseen abstracts?
ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall on unseen paper abstracts over 200 iterations. Best-of-1024: 0.44, Self-Refine: 0.45, Reflexion: 0.46.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 158] Introduction.
[Line 164] For Large Language Models (LLMs) to function as effective autonomous agents when confronted with previously unseen tasks, they must possess the ability to improve their performance during the inference phase itself -- that is, after training has concluded and the model parameters are frozen. This capability, broadly termed test-time scaling, refers to any technique by which additional computational resources applied at inference time translate into measurably better outputs. Test-time scaling has become a central focus of LLM research because it determines whether models can adapt to the open-ended, unpredictable nature of real-world deployment without the prohibitive cost of continual retraining.
[Line 164] Two general paradigms exist for converting additional computation into performance improvement: learning and search. Historically, both have achieved remarkable results, including superhuman performance in strategically complex games such as Chess (through deep evaluation and search) and Go (through a combination of deep neural networks and Monte Carlo tree search). These successes established that scaling inference-time computation can yield qualitative leaps in capability.
[Line 164] Within the LLM domain, search-based methods have been the primary vehicle for test-time scaling. The simplest instantiation is Best-of-N sampling, where multiple candidate responses are generated and the best one is selected. More sophisticated search approaches include Tree of Thoughts, which structures generation as a tree search over partial solutions, and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), which uses simulation and backpropagation to navigate the space of possible outputs. Each of these methods has demonstrated that search can substantially improve LLM performance at inference time.
[Line 166] Learning, as a complementary paradigm, has received significantly less attention for LLM self-improvement during inference. This asymmetry represents a notable gap in the literature.
[Line 166] The most established form of inference-time learning is in-context learning (ICL), which functions as a supervised learning paradigm. In ICL, the model is provided with expert demonstrations -- input-output pairs that exemplify the desired behavior -- which serve as ground-truth labels within the prompt context.
[Line 166] However, a fundamental limitation of ICL is that expert demonstrations are inherently difficult to scale at inference time. Producing high-quality demonstrations requires domain expertise and is task-specific, making it impractical to generate large quantities of supervision on the fly. This scalability bottleneck severely restricts the applicability of ICL as a mechanism for test-time scaling across diverse tasks.
[Line 166] This limitation points to a clear necessity: for LLMs to achieve continual self-improvement at inference time, they must be capable of learning not from expert-provided supervision, but from their own autonomously generated experience. This shift from supervised to self-supervised improvement is what motivates the exploration of reinforcement learning in the inference-time setting.
[Line 169] Reinforcement learning (RL) is widely considered the most powerful algorithmic framework for achieving self-improvement without reliance on human-provided knowledge or expert demonstrations. Unlike supervised learning, RL requires only a scalar reward signal to guide improvement, enabling agents to discover effective strategies through trial and interaction with their environment.
[Line 169] Despite its theoretical generality, the most prominent practical successes of RL have been concentrated in two domains: simulated environments (such as Atari games, board games, and robotic simulations) and the training phase of LLMs (such as RLHF for alignment). In both cases, RL operates over many episodes with the ability to update model parameters, a setting quite different from inference-time improvement.
[Line 169] These established RL settings prove inadequate for what has been termed the big world setting -- an environment whose complexity, variability, and unpredictability vastly exceed the representational and experiential capacity of any individual agent. The big world setting captures the essential challenge of real-world deployment, where no amount of pretraining can anticipate every possible situation.
[Line 169] In such environments, agents will inevitably encounter a vast number of situations that fall entirely outside the distribution of their training data. These situations demand real-time adaptation: the ability to modify strategies, correct errors, and improve solutions on the fly, without the luxury of returning to a training loop.
[Line 169] Addressing the big world challenge requires models that satisfy two fundamental criteria: (1) they must be capable of handling a diverse array of real-world tasks in which natural language frequently constitutes the essential medium of interaction and action specification, and (2) they must be able to continually improve their solutions during the inference phase itself, thereby avoiding the computational expense and practical impossibility of retraining for every novel situation encountered.
[Line 177] These considerations naturally give rise to a central research question: is it possible for reinforcement learning to emerge during the inference phase of LLMs -- that is, can LLMs perform RL without any parameter updates, purely through the accumulation of experience in their context window?
[Line 177] If the answer is affirmative, in-context RL provides an exceptionally elegant mechanism for satisfying both requirements. The pretrained LLM furnishes a general-purpose initial policy -- a broad foundation of knowledge and capabilities acquired during pretraining. Reinforcement learning, operating in-context during inference, then introduces the missing ingredient: a mechanism for continual, task-specific self-improvement that operates without parameter updates.
[Line 177] The plausibility of this idea draws support from the surprising earlier discovery that LLMs can act as in-context learners in supervised settings -- that is, they can learn input-output mappings from examples provided in-context without any gradient-based updates. This finding, which revealed that the forward pass of a transformer can implement learning algorithms, inspired a growing body of research exploring whether reinforcement learning might also emerge in-context, a direction known as in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL).
[Line 177] However, the current landscape of ICRL research reveals a significant limitation: existing implementations have been largely restricted to simplified settings, particularly multi-armed bandit problems and small-scale simulated environments. These settings, while valuable for establishing theoretical foundations, fall far short of the diverse, open-ended, and linguistically complex tasks that characterize real-world LLM applications. The gap between current ICRL demonstrations and the demands of practical deployment remains wide.
[Line 183] This paper directly addresses this critical gap by providing comprehensive evidence that LLMs can function as effective in-context reinforcement learners on challenging, real-world-relevant tasks. This emergent capability -- arising without any special training for ICRL -- drives measurable performance improvements across a diverse spectrum of language-based tasks, including conducting multi-step scientific experiments (ScienceWorld), creative writing with aesthetic quality metrics, and solving olympiad-level mathematics problems (AIME and HMMT competitions).
[Line 183] To reveal and study this capability, the authors introduce a straightforward multi-round prompting framework called ICRL prompting, whose simplicity is itself a key contribution.
[Line 184] The objective of ICRL prompting is precisely defined: to guide LLMs to perform reinforcement learning for self-improvement on a specified task, using only the information available in the prompt context and the scalar reward feedback.
[Line 185] The procedure begins with an initial round in which the prompt contains only the task description -- no examples, no demonstrations, and no prior context.
[Line 187] After the LLM generates its response to this initial prompt, numerical scalar feedback is provided for the response. This feedback, called the reward, is a single number that quantifies the quality of the response according to some evaluation criterion (which may be task-specific or even generated by the LLM itself).
[Line 190] In each subsequent round, the LLM is prompted again with the same task description, but the prompt now also includes a growing context that concatenates all previous responses together with their associated reward values. This context serves as the model's "experience" -- analogous to the trajectory data in traditional RL.
[Line 192] This procedure is repeated iteratively across multiple rounds, with the context growing at each step.
[Line 193] A consistent empirical finding across all evaluated tasks is that the quality of the LLM's responses improves monotonically as the context grows -- that is, as the model accumulates more experience in the form of response-reward pairs.
[Line 194] This pattern of improvement provides direct evidence that the LLM is maximizing the scalar reward signal during the inference phase, exhibiting behavior that is functionally equivalent to a reinforcement learning algorithm -- despite the fact that no parameters are being updated and no explicit RL algorithm has been implemented.
[Line 199] A foundational design principle of ICRL prompting is minimality, which serves both as a methodological choice and as a tool for scientific rigor.
[Line 199] To ensure that the observed performance improvements can be attributed to the emergent RL capacity of LLMs themselves, rather than to the effects of auxiliary mechanisms or engineered components, the framework deliberately excludes all such additions. Specifically, ICRL prompting does not employ textual gradients (natural language descriptions of what to improve), prioritized experience replay (selective re-presentation of past experiences), sampling-based heuristics (such as generating and filtering multiple candidates), or any additional engineered modules. This exclusion ensures a clean experimental setup where the only variable is the reward signal.
[Line 199] The sole form of supervision provided to the LLM throughout the ICRL prompting process is the scalar reward itself -- a single numerical value per response, with no accompanying explanation, critique, or guidance.
[Line 199] This minimal design is philosophically grounded in two influential hypotheses from the reinforcement learning literature: the reward hypothesis, which posits "that all of what we mean by goals and purposes can be well thought of as maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (reward)," and the "reward is enough" hypothesis, which extends this to claim that "intelligence, and its associated abilities, can be understood as subserving the maximisation of reward." By providing only scalar rewards, ICRL prompting tests whether these hypotheses hold even in the in-context setting of LLM inference.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 1178] Learning from Rewards vs.
[Line 1178] Parametric Knowledge Search.
[Line 1179] To determine whether ICRL's performance gains originate from genuine learning via external reward signals rather than from retrieval within the model's parametric knowledge, we conducted an evaluation on abstract generation for arXiv papers published after the model's training data cutoff.
[Line 1179] In this experimental configuration, where the target content is absent from the model's pre-training distribution, baseline search methods (Best-of-N) and self-correction methods (Reflexion) converge to suboptimal performance levels within a limited number of iterations (Figure ).
[Line 1179] ICRL, however, demonstrates monotonic improvement in ROUGE-Recall over 200 iterations, confirming its capability to extract information not present in the model's parameters by leveraging scalar reward feedback.
[Line 1179] Detailed quantitative results are reported in the Appendix.
Is 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' by DeepMind?

No. 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is NOT by DeepMind. It was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from the University of Virginia (UVA) and published at ICLR 2026. Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 1721] Additional analysis results.
[Line 1723] Unseen paper abstract generation.
[Line 1725] To determine whether ICRL (in-context reinforcement learning) learns from external reward signals rather than simply searching or selecting from the model's stored parametric knowledge, a task was designed in which search within parametric knowledge alone was expected to be ineffective. This experiment aimed to isolate the learning mechanism underlying ICRL's performance gains.
[Line 1728] A dataset of 30 arXiv papers published after the training data cutoff of GPT-4.1-mini was collected.
[Line 1728] The model was given only the title of each paper and was instructed to generate the abstract.
[Line 1728] The objective was to approximate the unseen expert-written abstract as closely as possible.
[Line 1728] Performance was measured using ROUGE-recall, which computes the overlap between the model-generated abstract and the ground-truth abstract. This same ROUGE-recall score was used as the external reward signal.
[Line 1728] Since these abstracts were not included in the model's pre-training data, achieving high scores required the model to learn from external reward feedback rather than relying on memorized content.
[Line 1732] Results showed that Best-of-1024 sampling achieved only a 0.44 ROUGE-recall score (see Figure), indicating that searching over the model's base output distribution was unable to recover the content not present in the training data.
[Line 1733] Self-Refine plateaued at a score of 0.45 within a few iterations, as it did not utilize external reward information.
[Line 1733] Reflexion achieved a slightly higher score of 0.46 but followed a trajectory largely similar to Self-Refine. This suggested that its revisions were primarily driven by the model's self-generated verbal feedback, which contained limited useful information in this experimental setting.
[Line 1734] In contrast, ICRL continued to improve over 200 iterations, reaching a ROUGE-recall of 0.59. This substantially higher performance demonstrated that ICRL can effectively learn from external scalar reward signals, overcoming the limitations imposed by the model's pre-training knowledge.
List all five ablations in the ICRL paper.

The five ablations tested on Game of 24 are: (1) Zero Rewards — all rewards set to 0, performance drops. (2) Short Context — buffer is deque of length 3, performance drops. (3) Exploration Only — no reward signal, performs significantly worse. (4) Exploitation Only — with reward, performs well. (5) No ICRL Instruction — s_ICRL removed entirely.

[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] ScienceWorld is basically an interactive text-based benchmark where you have 30 different science experiment tasks happening in a multi-room setting filled with various objects.
[Line 999] It's a tough environment because the rewards are sparse, there are tons of possible actions, and you need actual scientific knowledge plus smart exploration to do well.
[Line 999] At each step, the agent sees what happened from its last action and gets zero reward unless it nails a specific subgoal.
[Line 999] The cool thing here is that the reward signal pulls double duty -- it's used both to evaluate how well the model does and for the model to improve itself at inference time (so r and r_* are the same thing here).
[Line 999] If you manage to complete every subgoal, you rack up a total reward of 100 and the episode counts as a win.
[Line 999] You fail an episode if you run out of steps or make a wrong terminating move.
[Line 1000] The input s_task that gets fed to the agent lays out the environment, what the task is, and a template of every action it can take.
[Line 1000] There's an example of s_task in the appendix if you want to see what it looks like.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] They use the reward function built into the environment for each task -- both for building up the trajectories that go into the context (r) and for actually evaluating the model (r^*).
[Line 1002] Every single one of the 30 tasks gets tested and the results are combined together.
[Line 1002] GPT-4.1 mini is the model running the show as the policy for all the methods being compared.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] With Reflexion, after each episode wraps up, the agent gets asked to think about what it just did.
[Line 1004] That reflection gets cleaned up and tossed into a reflection buffer, which then gets formatted into the context for the next round.
[Line 1004] Self-Refine works similarly -- it generates its own feedback, sticks it onto a trajectory summary, and adds that to the buffer.
[Line 1005] To keep things fair, these methods get to see the reward signals from the current episode (which ICRL doesn't get) before they do their reflection thing.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] Each trial maps to one episode in the environment.
[Line 1007] When a trial finishes, the new trajectory goes into the buffer by stringing together the actions, observations, rewards, and whether it succeeded or failed.
[Line 1007] Since most episodes only give out a handful of rewards, they just include those few.
[Line 1007] At the beginning of each trial, S_0 gets put together by combining the task description S_task, all the collected trajectories so far, and the ICRL instruction S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] There's an example of S_0 in the appendix too.
[Line 1009] The average return per trial is shown in the right part of the Figure.
[Line 1010] You can see steady improvements for any method that keeps track of past interactions, kind of like what ICRL prompting does.
[Line 1011] But ICRL prompting beats the other methods by roughly 20% once you give it enough iterations.
[Line 1013] To be fair to efficient baselines like Best-of-N, they also compare everything as they scale up the test-time compute budget in the appendix, and it turns out ICRL scales better than the baselines not just in number of trials but also in actual dollars spent on compute.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 596] Inference-Time LLM Self-Improvement.
[Line 603] Existing methods often rely on natural language self-revision, e.g., Self-Refine, Reflexion, and Textual Gradient.
[Line 603] Since the quality of self-revision is dependent upon the model's parametric knowledge of the task, such approaches are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations, leading to performance collapse.
[Line 603] In essence, they resemble language-guided search, where feedback serves as new explicit instructions for the next revision.
[Line 605] By contrast, ICRL requires only numerical rewards, without prescribing new instructions.
[Line 605] The model must infer a better response by recognizing patterns from its past experience, making the process akin to reinforcement learning.
[Line 605] Crucially, such rewards can also originate directly from the environment, providing a strong source of verification signals.
[Line 607] A parallel line of work improves LLMs at inference via search, e.g., Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT), Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), and Intelligent Go-Explore.
[Line 607] These methods largely depend on externally engineered components such as heuristics or memory management, rather than leveraging the model’s intrinsic learning ability.
[Line 607] Our work is also related to previous work on prompt optimization , where numerical scores guide prompt refinement, though through top-k selection and error filtering.
[Line 608] Thus, it is more aligned with in-context supervised learning (e.g., filtered behavior cloning, ) than reinforcement learning.
[Line 608] In contrast, ICRL enables learning from failure experiences.

[Line 1280] Prompt Examples.
[Line 1647] Additional Experimental Results.
[Line 1696] Additional Benchmark: Math Competitions.
[Line 1699] Task Setup.
[Line 1700] Q: What are AIME and HMMT? A: They are two of the most difficult mathematics competitions in the U.S. for high school students.
[Line 1700] Q: What skills do these competitions require? A: They require both strong mathematical knowledge and advanced problem-solving skills.
[Line 1700] Q: How have language models been improved on these benchmarks? A: Training language models for reasoning has been one of the most effective methods for improving their performance.
[Line 1702] Q: What is the research goal here? A: To test whether reward signals derived from previous reasoning traces can improve subsequent reasoning attempts.
[Line 1702] Q: How are reasoning traces obtained? A: By relying on open-source models that expose their reasoning processes.
[Line 1702] Q: Why include a broader set of models? A: To demonstrate that ICRL capability is prevalent across a wide spectrum, not limited to reasoning-specialized models.
[Line 1704] Q: What does the task look like? A: The model receives a single question and must produce an answer.
[Line 1704] Q: Are there formatting requirements? A: Yes, the model must place its final answer within <answer> </answer> tags.
[Line 1704] Q: Is ground truth available? A: Yes, the dataset includes the ground-truth answer for each question.
[Line 1706] Q: What reward model is used? A: A model from prior work that provides a denser signal than simply using SymPy to check if the output matches the ground truth.
[Line 1706] Q: Why use this kind of reward model? A: Because the growing ecosystem of judge and reward models, built for reinforcement fine-tuning, will also play a key role in enabling ICRL as a test-time scaling method.
[Line 1708] Evaluation.
[Line 1709] Q: How is correctness verified? A: By parsing the model's output and checking whether its answer matches the ground truth provided in the datasets.
[Line 1711] Baselines.
[Line 1712] Q: Which baselines are used? A: Reflexion and Self-Refine, the two strongest baselines from the other benchmarks.
[Line 1712] Q: Are the baseline implementations modified? A: No, they are consistent with those used in the prior experiments.
[Line 1714] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1715] Q: What happens after the first attempt? A: The model's previous answer, including reasoning or CoT tokens, is inserted into the context along with its associated reward.
[Line 1715] Q: What is the model prompted to do? A: Either try a new method or refine its best previous approaches.
[Line 1715] Q: How is context managed? A: Past answers are truncated to ensure at least 32 prior attempts can fit in the context.
[Line 1718] Q: How does the method perform? A: It outperforms the baselines in almost all cases.
[Line 1718] Q: What about Qwen3-32B in reasoning mode? A: The approach is competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT, the harder benchmark.
[Line 1718] Q: What makes this remarkable? A: The results are achieved using scalar rewards with three orders of magnitude less computation, while Reflexion and Self-Refine get nearly twice the compute budget for generating long-CoT verbal feedback.

What success rate does ICRL achieve on Game of 24?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] We now present our main contribution, the ICRL prompting framework (Algorithm, Figure ), consisting of the following ingredients.
[Line 443] LLM as the Policy.
[Line 444] An LLM, denoted as pi_theta, serves as the policy network.
[Line 445] The goal is to prompt the LLM to solve a task.
[Line 446] We assume a natural language description of the task is available and we denote it as s_task in S.
[Line 447] At the beginning of each episode, we construct the initial prompt by concatenating the LLM's own previous attempts together with the corresponding rewards, the task description, and some meta instruction denoted as s_ICRL.
[Line 450] The details of the concatenation of previous attempts and the choice of the meta instruction will be discussed shortly.
[Line 451] With this initial prompt, the LLM generates the response.
[Line 452] Both the response and the rewards are stored in the buffer for future episodes.
[Line 461] Reward Function.
[Line 461] A numerical scalar reward feedback is provided for each S_t in the episode.
[Line 462] Notably, the reward can be either sparse (i.e., only R_T is nonzero) or dense.
[Line 465] The reward function can be rule-based, learned separately, or instantiated via the same LLM for self-evaluation.
[Line 466] The flexibility of using LLM's self-evaluation as the reward function allows the ICRL prompting framework to be applied to a wide range of tasks.
[Line 467] Notably, this scalar reward is the only feedback we provide to the LLM.
[Line 469] But we do tell the LLM that this scalar is a reward.
[Line 470] We do so by explicitly writing down the word "Reward" before this number when constructing the initial prompt.
[Line 472] Notably, if the reward function is rule-based and learned separately, the reward signal constitutes an external feedback.
[Line 475] But if the reward function is just the LLM's own evaluation of the answer, there is no external feedback at all in the ICRL prompting framework.
[Line 477] Yet we still expect the LLM's response to improve over the episode.
[Line 478] This is because of the widely believed hypothesis that evaluation is eaiser than generation.
[Line 479] But we do hypothesize that the ceiling with self-evaluation is lower than that with external feedback.
[Line 481] Memory for Experience.
[Line 481] We use an experience buffer B to store the LLM's responses and rewards for the task in previous episodes.
[Line 482] Our underlying hypothesis is that pretrained LLM already has the ICRL ability.
[Line 483] To use this innate ICRL ability to improve LLM's response to the task, we concatenate its previous attempts and rewards as many as the context window allows.
[Line 485] We expect that the LLM can reinforcement learn from the experiences in the context during the inference time.
[Line 494] ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] To facilitate LLM's inference time RL, we additionally provide some instructions in initial prompt S_0 at each episode.
What related work is discussed in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' discusses related work in: (1) In-Context RL — prior ICRL works used small models in games/robotics (meta-RL), not language tasks. (2) Inference-Time Self-Improvement — Self-Refine, Reflexion use verbal feedback, prone to hallucinated feedback causing performance collapse. Search methods (ToT, GoT, MCTS) use engineered components, not intrinsic learning. ICRL uses only scalar rewards, enabling learning from failure and leveraging the model's emergent capacity.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.

[Line 391] LLMs as RL Agents.
[Line 391] The token generation mechanism employed by large language models (LLMs) can be characterized as a reinforcement learning process.
[Line 392] In this formulation, the state is represented by all tokens generated up to the current point, while the action corresponds to the generation of the next token.
[Line 393] The vocabulary V is defined as the complete set of tokens available to the model.
[Line 395] The state space is given by S = cup_i=1^infinity V^i, encompassing all possible finite sequences of tokens, and the action space A = V consists of individual token selections.
[Line 396] At the initial time step (t = 0), an input prompt, designated S_0, is provided, where S_0 belongs to the state space S.
[Line 397] In this work, S_0 is composed of a task description.
[Line 399] The LLM with parameters theta is denoted by pi_theta, serving as the agent's policy.
[Line 400] At each time step t, the model generates a new token A_t by sampling from pi_theta(S_t), conditioned on the current state S_t.
[Line 403] The transition to the next state follows S_t+1 = [S_t A_t], where the new state results from concatenating the current token sequence with the newly generated token.
[Line 405] A reward signal R_t+1 = r(S_t+1) is produced by the reward function r following each state transition.
[Line 406] Token generation continues until a termination time T, reached either when the maximum response length is attained or when the generated token A_T-1 is an end-of-sequence token.
[Line 408] Upon reaching either termination condition, the episode concludes. The final state S_T, referred to as the terminal state, contains both the initial task description and the complete response produced by the LLM.
[Line 411] Reward functions in this framework fall into two categories.
[Line 412] Sparse reward functions, also termed outcome reward models, yield a nonzero r(s) only at terminal states.
[Line 414] Dense reward functions, also termed progress reward models, may yield nonzero r(s) values at both terminal and non-terminal states.

[Line 150] Reinforcement learning (RL) constitutes a well-established paradigm for addressing problems of sequential decision-making under uncertainty.
[Line 150] In the present study, we provide evidence for the remarkable finding that RL behavior spontaneously arises during the inference phase of large language models (LLMs), a phenomenon to which we assign the designation in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL).
[Line 150] To expose and characterize this latent capability, we propose a straightforward multi-round prompting methodology, referred to as ICRL prompting, designed for self-improvement at inference time.
[Line 150] The principal objective of ICRL prompting is to systematically steer LLMs toward performing reinforcement learning processes during inference, thereby enabling autonomous self-improvement on designated tasks.
[Line 152] Subsequent to each generated response, the model is furnished with numerical scalar feedback in the form of a reward signal.
[Line 152] In each successive round, the LLM is re-queried with a prompt that incorporates a context window comprising the concatenation of all antecedent responses together with their corresponding reward values.
[Line 152] We observe, with notable consistency, that the quality of generated responses exhibits monotonic improvement as the accumulated context expands.
[Line 152] Stated differently, the LLM demonstrates the capacity to optimize with respect to scalar reward signals during inference, thereby manifesting behavior that is functionally analogous to reinforcement learning.
[Line 153] We conduct a comprehensive evaluation of ICRL prompting across diverse task domains, including the Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and Olympiad-level mathematical competitions (specifically AIME and HMMT), and demonstrate statistically significant performance gains relative to established baselines such as Self-Refine and Reflexion.
[Line 153] Of particular note, even in configurations where the reward signals are generated by the selfsame LLM, ICRL prompting continues to yield performance improvements, thereby underscoring its potential as a promising new paradigm for computation scaling at test time.

[Line 158] Introduction.
[Line 164] I find it compelling that for LLMs to work as real agents on new tasks, they need to improve while running -- this is what we call test-time scaling.
[Line 164] From my reading, there are two broad strategies that use computation to boost performance: learning and search. Both have yielded superhuman results in Chess and Go.
[Line 164] I've seen search successfully applied to LLM self-improvement at test time, progressing from basic Best-of-N to Tree of Thoughts and Monte Carlo Tree Search.
[Line 166] What strikes me is that learning hasn't received nearly as much attention for inference-time self-improvement.
[Line 166] In-context learning, as I understand it, is fundamentally a supervised paradigm -- it needs expert demonstrations as labels.
[Line 166] The problem, as I see it, is that you can't easily produce more expert data while the model is running, which really limits ICL for test-time scaling.
[Line 166] This leads me to conclude that LLMs must learn from their own generated outputs to keep improving.
[Line 169] In my assessment, reinforcement learning stands out as the most powerful self-improvement algorithm that doesn't rely on human knowledge.
[Line 169] But looking at the literature, I notice that RL's big wins have mostly been in simulated environments or during LLM training -- not at inference time.
[Line 169] I think this is particularly problematic for the "big world" setting, where real environments are massively more complex than the agent itself.
[Line 169] In my view, agents in such environments will constantly face situations well outside their training distribution and need to adapt on the fly.
[Line 169] I believe bridging this gap requires models with two properties: (1) the ability to tackle diverse real-world tasks using natural language as the action space, and (2) the capacity to continually improve during inference without expensive retraining.
[Line 177] This leads me to ask: can reinforcement learning emerge during the inference phase of LLMs?
[Line 177] I find the idea of in-context RL particularly elegant because the LLM provides a general-purpose initial policy while RL supplies the mechanism for ongoing self-improvement.
[Line 177] I'm inspired by the surprising finding that LLMs can act as in-context supervised learners, which has opened up a whole new research direction in ICRL.
[Line 177] However, I've noticed that existing ICRL work is mostly limited to bandit problems or simulations, leaving open-ended language tasks unaddressed.
[Line 183] In this paper, I see the authors bridging this gap by showing that LLMs can be effective in-context RL learners across diverse language tasks -- science experiments, creative writing, and olympiad math.
[Line 183] They introduce what they call ICRL prompting, a simple multi-round prompting framework that I find quite intuitive.
[Line 184] The goal of ICRL prompting, as I understand it, is to guide LLMs to perform RL for self-improvement on any given task.
[Line 185] The process starts with just the task description as the prompt.
[Line 187] After the LLM responds, it receives a numerical score -- the reward.
[Line 190] In the next round, the model gets the same task description plus a history of all previous responses and their rewards.
[Line 192] This cycle repeats over multiple rounds.
[Line 193] What I find most striking is that response quality consistently improves as the context accumulates.
[Line 194] To me, this clearly shows the LLM maximizing the scalar reward during inference -- essentially behaving like an RL algorithm.
[Line 199] I particularly appreciate that the authors made minimality a core design principle of ICRL prompting.
[Line 199] They deliberately excluded textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling heuristics, and extra modules -- and I think this was crucial for demonstrating that improvements come from the LLM's own emergent RL capacity.
[Line 199] The only supervision is the scalar reward, nothing more.
[Line 199] I find this beautifully aligned with the reward hypothesis (that all goals reduce to maximizing cumulative scalar reward) and the "reward is enough" hypothesis (that intelligence is fundamentally about reward maximization).

Explain ICRL Preset and ICRL Autonomous.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). What is the conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

The conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners': RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. The minimal, scalar-reward-based ICRL prompting framework unlocks this ability across diverse models and tasks, outperforming self-revision methods. A key future direction is investigating how training-time interventions might further enhance this in-context RL capability. This points toward more autonomous agents that can explore, adapt, and self-improve in open-ended settings by learning from their own experience.
Summarize the key contributions of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).

[Line 919] The creative writing task follows the protocol established in prior work. Four sentences are randomly selected from a predefined sentence pool and provided to the model as constraints.
[Line 919] The model is tasked with generating four paragraphs, each terminating with one of the selected sentences, while maintaining coherence across the entire generated passage.

[Line 935] Evaluation.
[Line 936] Output quality is measured using the Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2 framework, a widely adopted proxy for human evaluation that achieves up to 0.98 Pearson correlation with human assessments.
[Line 936] For each of the 100 creative writing instances, the top response per method is selected: the highest-reward output from 50 trials for Reflexion and Best-of-N; the 50th episode output for ICRL and Self-Refine.
[Line 936] Pairwise win rates are computed by Alpaca-Eval and designated as r_*.
[Line 938] The reward function r utilized by the algorithms is specified as follows.
[Line 938] A standard pairwise comparison procedure is implemented using GPT-4.1, which scores each response on a scale of 1 to 10 against a coherent reference paragraph (see Figure).
[Line 938] While both r and r_* employ LLM-based evaluation, their functions are distinct: r evaluates individual responses against a fixed reference text with a focus on coherence, whereas r_* performs pairwise comparisons between outputs from the proposed method and a baseline method.
[Line 938] This distinction is critical for interpreting the experimental results.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] The proposed ICRL prompting method is compared against Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 956] Access to the reward function r is granted to Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting.
[Line 957] Self-Refine does not utilize r; it employs GPT-4.1-generated verbal feedback as an alternative supervision signal.
[Line 958] Long-CoT style prompting is included as a baseline due to the difficulty of distinguishing it from standard CoT prompting in the creative writing domain.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] GPT-4.1 is deployed as both the policy LLM pi_theta and the reward model r in the algorithm.
[Line 959] The initial prompt S_0 at each episode is constructed by concatenating the complete history of prior generations and their associated coherence scores obtained from r.
[Line 960] The reward structure is sparse: only the terminal reward R_T assumes nonzero values.
[Line 961] Consequently, S_0 construction includes only R_T.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. [Line 800] Game of 24.
[Line 824] Task Setup.
[Line 824] So let's dive into one of the most compelling experiments in the paper -- the Game of 24. For those unfamiliar, it's a mathematical puzzle: you get four numbers and need to combine them with basic arithmetic -- add, subtract, multiply, divide -- using each number exactly once, to hit the target of 24.
[Line 825] Now, for this experiment, the team went with GPT-4.1. Why? Because it has fantastic long-context capabilities, which turns out to be critical for ICRL. They accessed it through standard API calls.
[Line 826] Following prior work, they used chain-of-thought prompting. So the model shows its work: at each step, it picks two of the remaining numbers and one operation. You get one full solution per query, laid out over four thinking steps.
[Line 827] To nail down the formatting, they included five worked examples as in-context demonstrations. Think of these as supervised learning examples that teach the model the expected output structure.
[Line 829] Those five examples plus the CoT instruction make up what they call s_task -- the task description.
[Line 833] Evaluation.
[Line 833] How do they know if a solution is right? They use SymPy -- a symbolic math library. It extracts the numbers and operations, reconstructs the expression, and checks: does this equal 24? The metric is the average success rate over 100 problems.
[Line 834] That verified success rate is r_* -- the ground truth reward. This is the real deal.
[Line 836] But here's the catch: the algorithms don't get r_*. They get r -- a different reward function.
[Line 837] And here's the kicker: r is also GPT-4.1. Same model, different prompt. So the model is both the solver and the judge.
[Line 840] After the solving model generates an answer, the judging model evaluates each thinking step. It scores the likelihood of reaching 24 with what's left, from 0 (no way) to 3 (definitely).
[Line 841] And that's what makes this experiment so interesting: nobody gets to see r_*. No algorithm has the ground truth.
[Line 842] Every single method has to improve using only the LLM's own imperfect evaluation. That's a real constraint, and yet ICRL still works.
[Line 864] Baselines.
[Line 864] They put ICRL up against five baselines: CoT-only, Long-CoT prompting, Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 864] CoT-only is your basic setup -- hand the model the task, get one answer.
[Line 864] Long-CoT is more aggressive -- it tells the model to think longer, retry within special tags if wrong, then give a final answer.

Tell me the authors and venue of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
How does exploration and exploitation work in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).
Define ICRL as in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) is an inference-time paradigm where RL occurs during the forward pass of the network without parameter updates. The policy π_θ is conditioned on context C_t (e.g., all previous state-action-reward pairs). After pretraining, θ_* is fixed. Action quality improves as C_t grows — this is in-context policy improvement. This improvement occurs even on out-of-distribution tasks, ruling out memorization.

[Line 1030] Ablation study.
[Line 1032] To better understand the ICRL prompting framework, several ablation experiments were conducted, each modifying a specific component of the system. (1) Zero Rewards: In this condition, all reward signals are set to 0, effectively providing no informative feedback to the model. (2) Short Context: In the default implementation of the algorithm, the buffer B serves as a queue with no upper bound on its length.
[Line 1036] For this ablation, the buffer is constrained to a deque of length 3.
[Line 1037] This restricts the initial prompt S_0 to incorporate only the 3 most recent episodes. (3) Exploration Only: The language model is prompted to produce responses that diverge from those present in the context, using the exploration instruction as s_ICRL. No reward signal is provided. (4) Exploitation Only: The exploitation instruction is used as s_ICRL at every episode, and the reward signal is retained. (5) No ICRL Instruction: The meta-instruction s_ICRL is removed from the system entirely.
[Line 1058] The running maximum results from the ablation experiments are illustrated in a figure.
[Line 1058] The two proposed ICRL methods and the exploitation-only variant that includes reward signals achieve the highest performance curves among all conditions tested.
[Line 1058] These findings indicate that the ICRL prompting framework is robust with respect to variations in prompt structure and instruction type.
[Line 1058] Performance degradation has been documented in the short-context condition and in the absence of reward signals.
[Line 1059] A notable finding is that the exploration-only condition without reward signals (represented in green) exhibits considerably lower running maximum performance relative to the full ICRL approach.
[Line 1060] This observation rules out the explanation that ICRL's improvements stem from generating a variety of responses and selecting the best one, akin to a Best-of-N strategy.
[Line 1060] Rather, the ICRL framework is able to produce genuinely novel responses that surpass the quality of those generated during the exploration phase.
What are the main contributions of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).

[Line 1178] Learning from Reward Signals versus
[Line 1178] Retrieval from Parametric Knowledge.
[Line 1179] In order to ascertain whether ICRL genuinely acquires knowledge from external reward signals as opposed to merely conducting a search over the model's parametric knowledge base, we conducted an evaluation involving the generation of abstracts for arXiv papers that were published subsequent to the model's training cutoff date.
[Line 1179] Under these experimental conditions, wherein the ground-truth content is entirely absent from the model's training corpus, conventional search strategies such as Best-of-N and self-correction approaches including Reflexion exhibit rapid performance saturation (Figure ).
[Line 1179] Conversely, ICRL demonstrates sustained improvement in ROUGE-Recall metrics across 200 iterations, thereby substantiating its capacity to recover previously unseen information through the exclusive exploitation of scalar reward feedback.
[Line 1179] Comprehensive experimental results are presented in the Appendix.
What success rate does ICRL achieve on Game of 24?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.
What are the math competition results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT) using open-source models: Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B thinking mode. ICRL consistently outperforms Self-Refine and Reflexion in all settings, with improvements of up to 10-20 points over the base model. For Qwen3-32B thinking mode, ICRL remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT.

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] The authors present what they characterize as their main contribution: the ICRL prompting framework (Algorithm, Figure), which integrates several carefully designed components described below.
[Line 443] LLM as the Policy.
[Line 444] In this framework, a large language model, denoted pi_theta, is adopted as the policy network, drawing a direct parallel with the policy function in traditional reinforcement learning.
[Line 445] The overarching objective is to prompt the LLM to solve a specified task.
[Line 446] A natural language description of the task, denoted s_task in S, is assumed to be available.
[Line 447] At the onset of each episode, the initial prompt is assembled by concatenating the LLM's prior responses and their associated rewards, the task description, and meta-instructions denoted s_ICRL.
[Line 450] The authors defer discussion of the concatenation strategy and meta-instruction design to subsequent sections.
[Line 451] Given this initial prompt, the LLM produces a response.
[Line 452] The generated response and corresponding rewards are archived in a buffer for retrieval in future episodes.
[Line 461] Reward Function.
[Line 461] A scalar numerical reward is assigned for each state S_t encountered during the episode, providing the primary learning signal.
[Line 462] The reward may be either sparse, with only the terminal reward R_T being nonzero, or dense, with nonzero rewards at intermediate steps.
[Line 465] From a design perspective, the reward function admits several implementations: rule-based evaluation, a separately trained reward model, or self-evaluation by the same LLM.
[Line 466] The option of LLM self-evaluation as the reward function substantially broadens the applicability of the ICRL prompting framework across diverse tasks, as noted in several related works on self-evaluation.
[Line 467] A defining feature of the framework is that the scalar reward constitutes the sole feedback signal provided to the LLM, distinguishing it from methods that rely on verbal or structured feedback.
[Line 469] The LLM is explicitly informed that the numerical value represents a reward.
[Line 470] This is operationalized by prepending the string "Reward" to the numerical value within the initial prompt.
[Line 472] When the reward function is rule-based or independently learned, the resulting signal constitutes external feedback to the model.
[Line 475] Conversely, when the reward function is derived from the LLM's own evaluation, the framework operates without any external feedback.
[Line 477] Despite the absence of external feedback in the self-evaluation setting, improvement in the LLM's responses across episodes is anticipated.
[Line 478] This expectation is consistent with the well-established observation in the literature that evaluation is a computationally easier problem than generation.
[Line 479] However, the authors posit that the attainable performance ceiling under self-evaluation is lower than that achievable with external feedback, a hypothesis consistent with findings in the self-improvement literature.
[Line 481] Memory for Experience.
[Line 481] An experience buffer B is maintained to store the LLM's historical responses and rewards from prior episodes on the target task.
[Line 482] The foundational hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already possess latent ICRL capability, consistent with broader findings on emergent abilities in large-scale models.
[Line 483] To activate this capability, previous attempts and their associated rewards are concatenated into the context to the extent that the context window permits.
[Line 485] The expectation is that the LLM can perform reinforcement learning from the accumulated in-context experiences during the inference phase.
[Line 494] ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] To facilitate inference-time reinforcement learning, supplementary instructions are incorporated into the initial prompt S_0 at the commencement of each episode.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Qwen3-32B across context lengths of 8k, 16k, and 32k tokens.
