[Line 158] Introduction.
[Line 164] So let's start with the big picture. If we want LLMs to be useful agents on new tasks, they have to get better while they're running. We call that test-time scaling, and it's a really important concept.
[Line 164] Now, there are two fundamental approaches to getting better with more compute: learning and search. Both have proven themselves -- we've seen superhuman performance in Chess and Go using these methods.
[Line 164] On the search side, we've had great success applying it to LLM self-improvement. Think of the progression: Best-of-N is the simplest approach, then Tree of Thoughts, then Monte Carlo Tree Search. Each one more sophisticated than the last.
[Line 166] But here's the interesting gap -- learning hasn't gotten the same treatment for inference-time self-improvement. And that's a missed opportunity.
[Line 166] Why? Well, in-context learning is basically supervised learning. You need expert examples with correct answers to show the model.
[Line 166] And the problem with that is -- you can't easily generate more expert data while the model is running. That's a real bottleneck for scaling ICL at test time.
[Line 166] So where does that leave us? LLMs need to learn from their own experience. They need to teach themselves.
[Line 169] Now, reinforcement learning -- RL -- is the champion of self-improvement without human hand-holding. It's arguably the most successful approach we have for that.
[Line 169] But here's the catch: RL has mostly worked its magic in simulations or during the training phase. Not during actual inference.
[Line 169] And that's a problem because the real world -- what we call the "big world" setting -- is enormously more complex than any agent we can build.
[Line 169] In the real world, your agent is going to face situations it has never seen before, over and over, and it needs to figure things out on the spot.
[Line 169] So we need models that can do two things: first, handle the diversity of real-world tasks where natural language is often how you interact, and second, keep getting better during inference without needing to retrain.
[Line 177] And that brings us to our key question for today: Can reinforcement learning emerge during the inference phase of LLMs? Think about how powerful that would be.
[Line 177] If LLMs can do RL in-context, it beautifully solves both problems. The LLM gives you a strong starting policy, and the emergent RL gives you continual improvement.
[Line 177] We already know -- and it was quite surprising when first discovered -- that LLMs can do in-context supervised learning. That finding kicked off a wave of research into in-context RL, or ICRL.
[Line 177] But the existing ICRL work has been limited. Bandit problems, simulations -- nothing that tackles the real, messy, language-heavy tasks we actually care about.
[Line 183] And that's exactly what we address in this paper. We show that LLMs really are effective in-context reinforcement learners. This emergent ability shows up across science experiments, creative writing, and even olympiad-level math.
[Line 183] Our method is refreshingly simple. We call it ICRL prompting -- a multi-round prompting framework.
[Line 184] The whole idea is to guide LLMs into doing RL for self-improvement on whatever task you give them.
[Line 185] You start with just the task description. Nothing else.
[Line 187] The model generates a response, and then you give it a number -- a reward score.
[Line 190] Next round, you show it the same task plus all its previous attempts with their reward scores.
[Line 192] And you keep going. Round after round.
[Line 193] What do we see? The responses get better as the context fills up.
[Line 194] The LLM is maximizing that reward signal during inference -- it's doing RL, right there in the forward pass.
[Line 199] Now, a critical design choice we made is minimality. This is really important.
[Line 199] We intentionally left out textual gradients, experience replay, sampling tricks, extra modules -- all of it. Because we wanted to isolate the LLM's inherent RL ability.
[Line 199] The only thing we give the model? A number. The reward.
[Line 199] And this connects to two beautiful theoretical ideas: the reward hypothesis -- that all goals reduce to maximizing cumulative scalar reward -- and the "reward is enough" hypothesis -- that intelligence itself is about reward maximization.
Explain the experience buffer in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have innate ICRL ability. To activate it, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated as many as the context window allows into S_0. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.
What are the main contributions of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).

[Line 824] How the Task Works.
[Line 824] So here's the deal: you get four numbers and you need to combine them using basic math (add, subtract, multiply, divide) to make 24. You have to use each number exactly once, but you can do the operations in whatever order you want.
[Line 825] They went with GPT-4.1 for this experiment since it handles really long contexts well, and they accessed it through API calls.
[Line 826] They used chain-of-thought prompting (basically asking the model to show its work), where at each step it picks two numbers and an operation, working through 4 steps total to get one complete solution per query.
[Line 827] To make sure the model gives answers in the right format, they threw in 5 example solutions as demonstrations.
[Line 829] Those CoT instructions plus the 5 examples together make up the task description, which they call s_task.
[Line 833] How They Check the Answers.
[Line 833] They used SymPy (a math library) to check if the solutions are actually correct - pulling out the numbers and operations, rebuilding the expression, and checking if it equals 24. They report the average success rate across 100 problems.
[Line 834] This verified success rate is what they call r_* - basically the "real" reward that tells you if you actually got it right.
[Line 836] Then there's r, which is the reward function that the methods actually get to see during the experiment.
[Line 837] Here's the interesting part: r is also GPT-4.1 - the exact same model used for solving, just prompted differently.
[Line 840] After the solver generates an answer, GPT-4.1 (acting as the judge) looks at each thinking step and rates how likely it is you can still reach 24 with what's left, on a 0-3 scale (0 means no way, 3 means definitely).
[Line 841] What makes this really hard is that none of the methods get to see the actual answer checker r_*.
[Line 842] They all have to work with the LLM's own judgment (which isn't perfect) to figure out how to get better.
[Line 864] What They Compared Against.
[Line 864] They tested against CoT-only, Long-CoT prompting, Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 864] CoT-only is the simplest - just give the model the task and let it solve it once.
[Line 864] Long-CoT prompting tells the model to think really hard and keep retrying inside special thinking tags if it gets it wrong before giving a final answer.
[Line 864] Even though GPT-4.1 wasn't specifically trained for this long-thinking style, it turns out this approach gets it to produce much longer, self-correcting reasoning compared to just asking for an answer, making it a pretty tough baseline.
[Line 866] Both CoT-only and Long-CoT don't use any reward signal and only get one shot.
[Line 867] For Best-of-N, they gave it an extra advantage by using the actual ground truth reward r_* to pick the best answer.
[Line 869] Self-Refine doesn't need a reward function at all.
[Line 870] It just asks the model to critique its own work in words.
[Line 871] Reflexion generates reflections based on the reward r.
[Line 872] ICRL prompting is different because it feeds the numerical reward r directly to the model - no wordy feedback needed.
[Line 874] So when you compare ICRL prompting against Self-Refine and Reflexion, you're really comparing numbers vs. words as feedback.

How does ICRL perform on creative writing?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.
What is the fundamental difference between ICRL and verbal feedback methods?

The fundamental difference is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. ICRL uses only numerical scalar rewards — no textual feedback, no instructions on how to improve. Self-Refine and Reflexion use verbal/textual feedback. Verbal feedback methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, while scalar rewards provide a clean learning signal.
What context length analysis was done in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Qwen3-32B across context lengths of 8k, 16k, and 32k tokens. ICRL consistently surpasses Self-Refine and Reflexion in both Creative Writing and AIME at all context lengths, demonstrating superior performance per unit of compute.
What is the exact ROUGE-recall for ICRL on unseen abstracts?
ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall on unseen paper abstracts over 200 iterations. Best-of-1024: 0.44, Self-Refine: 0.45, Reflexion: 0.46.

[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] ScienceWorld constitutes an interactive, text-based evaluation benchmark comprising 30 distinct science-experiment tasks situated within a multi-room virtual environment containing a diverse array of objects.
[Line 999] The benchmark presents substantial challenges attributable to sparse reward structures, expansive action spaces, and the necessity for both scientific domain knowledge and efficient exploratory strategies.
[Line 999] At each discrete time step, the agent perceives the outcome of its preceding action and receives a reward of zero unless a predefined subgoal has been successfully achieved.
[Line 999] This reward signal serves a dual purpose: it functions both as the evaluation metric and as the mechanism for inference-time self-improvement (that is, r and r_* are equivalent in this experimental setting).
[Line 999] The successful completion of all subgoals results in a cumulative reward of 100 and constitutes a successful episode termination.
[Line 999] An episode terminates unsuccessfully when the agent either exhausts the maximum permissible number of steps or executes an erroneous terminating action.
[Line 1000] The input s_task furnished to the agent provides a comprehensive description of the environment, the specific task, and the template encompassing all permissible actions.
[Line 1000] A representative example of s_task is included in the Appendix.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] The environment-provided reward function is employed for each task both for constructing the contextual trajectories (r) and for model evaluation (r^*).
[Line 1002] All 30 tasks are used for benchmarking each method, with results subsequently aggregated.
[Line 1002] GPT-4.1 mini serves as the policy network for all compared algorithmic approaches.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] In the Reflexion framework, upon the conclusion of each episode, the agent is prompted to engage in reflective analysis of its attempt.
[Line 1004] The resulting reflection is subsequently sanitized and appended to a dedicated reflection buffer, which is then formatted and incorporated into the context for subsequent trials.
[Line 1004] Self-Refine operates analogously by generating self-directed feedback, which is appended to a trajectory summary and subsequently added to the buffer.
[Line 1005] To ensure methodological fairness in comparison, these baseline methods are granted access to the reward signals of the current episode (in contrast to ICRL) prior to the generation of reflective output.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] Each trial is mapped to a single episode within the environment.
[Line 1007] Upon trial completion, the newly generated trajectory is appended to the buffer through the concatenation of actions, observations, rewards, and the terminal outcome (success or failure).
[Line 1007] Given that each episode typically produces only a limited number of reward signals, only those specific rewards are retained.
[Line 1007] At the commencement of each trial, S_0 is constructed through the sequential concatenation of the task description S_task, the accumulated trajectories, and the ICRL instruction S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] A representative example of S_0 is provided in the Appendix.
[Line 1009] The mean return as a function of trial number is presented in the right panel of the Figure.
[Line 1010] Consistent improvements are observed across methods that incorporate some form of historical interaction record, analogous to the ICRL prompting approach.
[Line 1011] Nevertheless, ICRL prompting demonstrates superior performance, exceeding baseline methods by approximately 20% following a sufficient number of iterations.
[Line 1013] To establish equitable comparisons with computationally efficient baselines such as Best-of-N, the Appendix presents comparisons as a function of scaled test-time computational budget, revealing that ICRL exhibits superior scaling behavior relative to baselines both in terms of the number of trials and the test-time computational expenditure (measured in monetary terms).
What are the ScienceWorld results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based benchmark with 30 science-experiment tasks in a multi-room environment. Rewards are sparse. GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy. ICRL prompting outperforms baseline methods by about 20% after enough iterations. ICRL also scales better than baselines in terms of test-time compute budget (in dollar amounts).
Why does ICRL outperform Self-Refine?

ICRL outperforms Self-Refine because Self-Refine relies on verbal self-revision which is prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations, leading to performance collapse. ICRL uses only scalar rewards without verbal feedback, avoiding this problem. The model infers improvements by recognizing patterns from past experience.

[Line 1030] Ablation Analysis.
[Line 1032] In order to gain deeper insight into the mechanisms underlying ICRL prompting, we conduct the following systematic ablation experiments. (1) Zero Rewards: All reward signals are set to zero, thereby removing any informative scalar feedback. (2) Short Context: The experience buffer B, which in the standard algorithm functions as an unbounded queue, is modified.
[Line 1036] Specifically, we replace the unbounded queue with a deque of fixed length 3.
[Line 1037] Consequently, only the three most recent episodes are incorporated when constructing the initial prompt S_0. (3) Exploration Only: The LLM is instructed solely to produce responses that differ from those already present in the context, employing the exploration instruction as s_ICRL while omitting the reward signal entirely. (4) Exploitation Only: The exploitation instruction is consistently used as s_ICRL, and the reward signal is retained. (5) No ICRL Instruction: The meta-instruction s_ICRL is removed entirely from the prompting framework.
[Line 1058] The running maximum performance across the ablation conditions is depicted in Figure.
[Line 1058] Both of our proposed methods, together with the exploitation-only variant that retains reward signals, exhibit the most favorable performance trajectories.
[Line 1058] This finding substantiates the robustness of the ICRL prompting framework across varying prompt configurations.
[Line 1058] Furthermore, we observe a notable degradation in performance under both the short-context and zero-reward conditions.
[Line 1059] A particularly salient observation is that the exploration-only ablation lacking a reward signal (depicted in green) exhibits substantially inferior performance relative to our full approach when evaluated by the running maximum metric.
[Line 1060] This result provides compelling evidence that the performance gains attributable to our method cannot be explained merely by sampling diverse responses and subsequently selecting the optimal one, as in a Best-of-N strategy.
[Line 1060] Rather, ICRL demonstrates the capacity to synthesize genuinely novel responses that surpass in quality those generated during the exploratory phase.
Explain the experience buffer in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have innate ICRL ability. To activate it, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated as many as the context window allows into S_0. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.

List all seven authors of the ICRL paper.

The seven authors of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' are: 1. Kefan Song, 2. Amir Moeini, 3. Peng Wang, 4. Lei Gong, 5. Rohan Chandra, 6. Shangtong Zhang, 7. Yanjun Qi. All from the University of Virginia (UVA). Published at ICLR 2026.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] Before diving into the main results, let's get on the same page about what reinforcement learning actually is. RL sets up problems using something called Markov Decision Processes, or MDPs. Think of an MDP as a game board with a few key components: S is the state space (all the places you can be), A is the action space (all the moves you can make), r: S -> R is the reward function (how good each state is), p_0 is where you start (the initial distribution over S), and p: S x A -> (S) is the transition function (what happens when you make a move).
[Line 340] Everything begins at time step 0, where you draw your starting state S_0 from p_0.
[Line 342] From there, at each time step t, the agent finds itself in some state S_t and needs to pick an action A_t. It does this using a policy pi, which maps states to probability distributions over actions. So A_t is sampled from pi(S_t).
[Line 344] After taking action A_t, the agent lands in a new state S_t+1 (sampled from p(S_t, A_t)) and picks up a reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1).
[Line 346] This loop -- observe, act, get reward, repeat -- continues until some time T, which signals the end of the episode.
[Line 348] The agent's whole mission is to tune its policy pi to maximize the expected total reward J(pi) = E[sum_t=1^T R_t].
[Line 349] In the deep RL world, this policy pi is implemented as a neural network.
[Line 351] We label the network's parameters as theta, giving us the policy pi_theta.
[Line 352] RL algorithms work by tweaking theta to make the policy better over time.
[Line 353] Here's how that looks in practice: at time t, you sample action A_t from pi_theta_t(S_t).
[Line 355] The algorithm then updates theta_t to theta_t+1 based on what it's learned from the trajectory so far -- all those states, actions, and rewards like S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1.
[Line 356] Then at t+1, the action A_t+1 comes from the freshly updated policy pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1).
[Line 360] The takeaway: traditional RL is essentially all about updating those parameters theta_t.
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] Now here's where it gets interesting. ICRL (the term was coined in earlier work) flips the script: instead of updating weights, the RL process happens during the model's forward pass at inference time. No gradient updates, no parameter changes.
[Line 368] The trick is that the policy pi_theta now also takes in a context C_t, so actions are chosen as A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t).
[Line 369] What goes into C_t is still being figured out by researchers, but a straightforward approach is to pack in all the state-action-reward tuples the agent has encountered so far.
[Line 370] One important detail: this context usually includes experience from all past episodes, not just the current one.
[Line 371] There's a pretraining phase involved, where the network theta gets trained across a wide variety of MDPs.
[Line 373] After pretraining, we call the frozen parameters theta_*.
[Line 374] The pretrained policy pi_theta_* then gets evaluated on new tasks it hasn't seen before.
[Line 376] In a new MDP, actions are sampled from pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t).
[Line 378] And here's the crucial part: theta_* never changes.
[Line 379] Yet researchers consistently find that the quality of actions improves as the context C_t grows with more experience in the new task.
[Line 381] Since theta_* is frozen, this improvement has to come from the context getting richer.
[Line 383] This is what's known as in-context policy improvement.
[Line 384] Perhaps most surprisingly, this improvement shows up even on tasks that look nothing like the pretraining data. Researchers have shown it works on bandit problems where the optimal arms are the exact opposite of what was seen during training.
[Line 388] That rules out the simple explanation that theta_* just memorized all the training tasks.
Explain 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' in detail.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.

[Line 1067] Detailed Context Length Analysis.
[Line 1068] A critical question for any inference-time improvement method is how its computational efficiency scales with varying input sizes. To investigate this, the authors conducted a systematic evaluation of ICRL using the Qwen3-32B language model across three distinct context window configurations: 8,000 tokens (8k), 16,000 tokens (16k), and 32,000 tokens (32k). The complete numerical results are presented in Table . This range of context lengths was chosen to capture the behavior of ICRL under constrained, moderate, and relatively generous input budgets.
[Line 1068] The experimental results reveal a consistent and noteworthy pattern: at every tested context length, ICRL outperformed both Self-Refine and Reflexion -- two prominent inference-time self-improvement baselines -- on both the Creative Writing benchmark and the AIME (American Invitational Mathematics Examination) benchmark. This consistent superiority indicates that ICRL not only achieves better absolute performance but does so with greater compute efficiency, delivering more task performance per unit of computational expenditure than the competing methods.

[Line 919] The creative writing benchmark, originally proposed in prior studies, has been adopted by several investigations into LLM self-improvement. In this formulation, four sentences are randomly drawn from a curated sentence pool.

[Line 938] The scoring protocol is described in the referenced Figure.

[Line 938] This methodological difference is important for the correct interpretation of reported results.

The authors are Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, Yanjun Qi. It is a different paper from the 2021 'Reward is Enough' by Silver et al. from DeepMind.
Who are the authors of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
How does ICRL perform on creative writing?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.
What related work is discussed in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' discusses related work in: (1) In-Context RL — prior ICRL works used small models in games/robotics (meta-RL), not language tasks. (2) Inference-Time Self-Improvement — Self-Refine, Reflexion use verbal feedback, prone to hallucinated feedback causing performance collapse. Search methods (ToT, GoT, MCTS) use engineered components, not intrinsic learning. ICRL uses only scalar rewards, enabling learning from failure and leveraging the model's emergent capacity.
What is ICRL Autonomous?

ICRL Autonomous always provides the 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM itself decide which strategy to use. Unlike ICRL Preset which alternates on a fixed schedule, ICRL Autonomous gives the model agency to choose.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a mathematical framework for sequential decision-making by modeling tasks as Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). An MDP is formally defined as a tuple consisting of five components. The state space S encompasses all possible configurations or situations the agent may encounter. The action space A comprises all available actions. The reward function r: S -> R maps each state to a scalar value indicating desirability. The initial state distribution p_0, an element of (S) where (S) denotes the space of all probability distributions over S, determines the starting conditions. Finally, the transition function p: S x A -> (S) specifies how the environment evolves in response to agent actions, mapping state-action pairs to probability distributions over successor states.
[Line 340] The process begins at time step 0. An initial state S_0 is drawn from the distribution p_0, establishing the agent's starting configuration.
[Line 342] At each subsequent time step t, the agent finds itself in state S_t and must select an action. It does so according to its policy pi: S -> (A), a function that maps each state to a probability distribution over the action space, where (A) denotes the set of all probability distributions over A. Concretely, the action A_t is sampled from the distribution pi(S_t). The policy thus encodes the agent's complete decision-making strategy.
[Line 344] Following the execution of action A_t, two things occur simultaneously: the agent transitions to a successor state S_t+1 drawn from the transition distribution p(S_t, A_t), and it receives a scalar reward signal R_t+1 = r(S_t+1). This reward provides the agent with information about the desirability of the resulting state.
[Line 346] This cyclic pattern of state observation, action selection, state transition, and reward reception continues iteratively until a predetermined terminal time step T is reached. The completion of this sequence from S_0 through S_T constitutes a single episode.
[Line 348] The overarching objective of the RL agent is to discover and refine a policy pi that maximizes the expected cumulative reward over an episode, formally expressed as J(pi) = E[sum_t=1^T R_t]. This quantity represents the expected total return, summing all rewards from the first transition through the terminal state.
[Line 349] In the modern deep RL paradigm, the policy pi is implemented as a deep neural network, which serves as a flexible function approximator capable of handling high-dimensional and continuous state and action spaces.
[Line 351] The trainable parameters of this neural network are collectively represented by the vector theta. Using this parameterization, the policy is denoted pi_theta to explicitly indicate its dependence on the network weights.
[Line 352] The core mechanism of RL algorithms is the iterative updating of the parameter vector theta. Through methods such as policy gradients, value function approximation, or actor-critic architectures, the algorithm adjusts theta to progressively improve the policy's performance.
[Line 353] To illustrate this process concretely: at time step t, the agent generates action A_t by sampling from the distribution pi_theta_t(S_t), where theta_t represents the current parameter values.
[Line 355] Based on the trajectory information accumulated up to this point -- specifically the sequence of states, actions, and rewards S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1 -- the RL algorithm computes and applies an update, transforming the parameters from theta_t to theta_t+1. The specific nature of this update varies across different RL algorithms.
[Line 356] At the subsequent time step t+1, the action A_t+1 is now sampled from pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1), reflecting the improved policy that results from the parameter update.
[Line 360] In summary, the entire learning process in conventional RL is encapsulated in the trajectory of parameter updates: the sequence theta_0, theta_1, theta_2, ... captures how the agent's behavioral policy evolves over time.
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) is a term first introduced in the research literature to describe a fundamentally different approach to RL. Rather than learning through parameter updates, ICRL is an emerging inference-time computation paradigm in which the reinforcement learning process occurs entirely within the forward pass of the neural network. Crucially, no parameter updates are performed during this process -- the network weights remain completely static.
[Line 368] The key mechanism enabling ICRL is the augmentation of the policy's input. In addition to the current state S_t, the policy pi_theta receives a context variable C_t, yielding the action selection rule A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t). The context C_t provides the policy with historical information that enables it to adapt its behavior without modifying its parameters.
[Line 369] The design and construction of the context C_t is a subject of active and ongoing research. One widely studied approach is to populate C_t with the complete set of state-action-reward tuples that the agent has accumulated during its interaction with the task. This provides the policy with a comprehensive record of past experience.
[Line 370] An important detail regarding the context is that it typically encompasses state-action-reward tuples from the entirety of the agent's interaction history -- spanning all previous episodes, rather than being limited to the data generated within the current episode alone.
[Line 371] The ICRL framework includes a pretraining stage in which the neural network parameters theta are optimized across a wide and diverse collection of tasks, each formulated as a distinct MDP. This pretraining phase equips the network with general-purpose knowledge and the implicit ability to perform in-context learning.
[Line 373] The parameter values that result from this pretraining phase are denoted theta_*, representing the fixed, pretrained model.
[Line 374] After the pretraining stage is complete, the policy pi_theta_* is deployed and evaluated on new tasks that were not part of the pretraining distribution.
[Line 376] In these novel MDPs, action selection proceeds as A_t ~ pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t), with the pretrained parameters governing the forward pass.
[Line 378] The defining feature of ICRL is that the parameters theta_* remain entirely fixed throughout the evaluation on new tasks. There are no gradient computations, no weight updates, and no fine-tuning of any kind.
[Line 379] Despite this complete parameter immutability, a striking empirical finding is that the quality and effectiveness of the actions A_t systematically improve as the context C_t grows with accumulated experience in the new task. The agent becomes progressively more capable even though its weights never change.
[Line 381] Because theta_* is held constant throughout this process, the observed improvement in performance can only be attributed to one source: the increasing richness and informativeness of the context C_t.
[Line 383] This remarkable phenomenon -- wherein a fixed-parameter model exhibits progressively improving behavior through context expansion alone -- has been termed in-context policy improvement.
[Line 384] Adding to the significance of this finding, in-context policy improvement has been observed even in scenarios where the evaluation task lies entirely outside the distribution of tasks used during pretraining. As a concrete example, researchers have demonstrated in-context policy improvement in bandit problems where the optimal arms are diametrically opposed to those that were optimal in the pretraining bandit tasks. The agent successfully learns to select the correct arms despite never having encountered such a configuration during training.
[Line 388] This critical observation eliminates the hypothesis that the observed performance improvements are attributable to theta_* having memorized or stored solutions for the pretraining tasks. Since the improvement generalizes to out-of-distribution settings, the network must be implementing a genuine learning algorithm within its forward pass, rather than simply retrieving cached solutions.
What kind of feedback does Reflexion use?

Reflexion generates textual reflection based on the reward signal. Unlike ICRL which uses the scalar reward directly, Reflexion converts the reward into verbal feedback. ICRL uses no verbal feedback at all.
What is the advantage of ICRL over verbal feedback methods?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.
What kind of feedback does Reflexion use?

Reflexion generates textual reflection based on the reward signal. Unlike ICRL which uses the scalar reward directly, Reflexion converts the reward into verbal feedback. ICRL uses no verbal feedback at all.
What is the key finding from the ICRL ablation study?

The key finding is that ICRL can genuinely generate novel responses that are better than ones during exploration. The exploration-only-without-reward method performs significantly worse, proving ICRL's gains are not just from Best-of-N selection. Rewards and context length both matter.

[Line 780] So in this part, we put ICRL prompting to the test on three different tasks: Game of 24, a creative writing challenge, and ScienceWorld.
[Line 781] We pit it against a bunch of baselines — plain CoT, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N, Self-Refine, and Reflexion — to see how it stacks up.
[Line 782] One important thing to note: we let Self-Refine and Reflexion use as much prompt space as the LLM can handle, so they get a fair shot.
What are the creative writing results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.

[Line 1180] Conclusion.
[Line 1181] - Main contribution: demonstrating that RL emerges as an inference-time capability of LLMs without parameter updates.
[Line 1181] - The minimal, scalar-reward ICRL prompting framework successfully activates this ability across diverse models and general-purpose tasks. - It outperforms self-revision baselines.
[Line 1181] - Key future direction: investigating training-time interventions to further enhance in-context RL in LLMs.
[Line 1181] - Broader implication: this capability suggests a path toward autonomous agents that explore, adapt, and self-improve in open-ended environments through experiential learning.
What is the 'reward is enough' hypothesis?

The 'reward is enough' hypothesis (referenced in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners') states: 'intelligence, and its associated abilities, can be understood as subserving the maximisation of reward.' The related reward hypothesis states: 'all of what we mean by goals and purposes can be well thought of as maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (reward).' ICRL prompting complies with both by using only scalar rewards.
What is ICRL Autonomous?

ICRL Autonomous always provides the 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM itself decide which strategy to use. Unlike ICRL Preset which alternates on a fixed schedule, ICRL Autonomous gives the model agency to choose.
How does ICRL perform on AIME and HMMT?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT) using open-source models: Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B thinking mode. ICRL consistently outperforms Self-Refine and Reflexion in all settings, with improvements of up to 10-20 points over the base model. For Qwen3-32B thinking mode, ICRL remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT.

[Line 1030] Ablation Study.
[Line 1032] I wanted to understand what makes ICRL prompting actually work, so I considered the following ablation experiments. (1) Zero Rewards: I set all rewards to 0 to see what happens without any informative feedback. (2) Short Context: In the standard algorithm, the buffer B works like a queue that can grow indefinitely.
[Line 1036] I changed it to a deque that only holds 3 entries.
[Line 1037] This means only the 3 most recent episodes get used when building S_0. (3) Exploration Only: I simply ask the LLM to produce different responses from what it has already generated, using the exploration instruction as s_ICRL, but I withhold the reward signal entirely. (4) Exploitation Only: I always apply the exploitation instruction as s_ICRL and keep the reward signal. (5) No ICRL Instruction: I remove s_ICRL completely from the prompt.

[Line 1058] Both of my two ICRL methods, along with the exploitation-only variant using rewards, produced the best performance curves.
[Line 1058] I found this encouraging because it shows that the ICRL prompting framework is quite robust to different prompt configurations.
[Line 1058] I also noticed that performance drops noticeably when the context is too short or when rewards are missing.
[Line 1059] One of the most important things I observed is that the exploration-only method without any reward signal (the green curve) performs substantially worse than my full approach on the running max metric.
[Line 1060] This convinced me that the improvements from ICRL are not simply a matter of exploring many different responses and selecting the best one, the way Best-of-N does.
[Line 1060] Rather, ICRL is genuinely capable of producing novel responses that surpass everything generated during the exploration phase.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based evaluation benchmark that encompasses 30 science-experiment tasks. These tasks are conducted within a multi-room virtual environment that is populated with a diverse collection of objects. The benchmark was designed to test agent capabilities in scientific reasoning and interactive problem-solving scenarios.
[Line 999] The environment presents multiple layers of difficulty. First, the reward structure is sparse, meaning that feedback signals are infrequent and the agent must operate for extended periods without positive reinforcement. Second, the action space is large, requiring the agent to select from many possible actions at each decision point. Third, success in the tasks demands both domain-specific scientific knowledge and the capability for efficient exploration of the environment.
[Line 999] The reward mechanism operates as follows: at each time step, the agent observes the outcome of its most recent action. A reward of zero is assigned unless the agent has successfully completed one of the predefined subgoals that constitute the overall task objective.
[Line 999] A distinguishing characteristic of this benchmark is that the reward function used for inference-time self-improvement (r) is identical to the reward function used for final evaluation (r_*). This identity simplifies the experimental design and ensures that the learning signal directly corresponds to the evaluation criterion.
[Line 999] When the agent successfully completes all predefined subgoals within an episode, a cumulative reward of 100 is attained, and the episode is classified as successful. This cumulative scoring system incentivizes complete task execution rather than partial progress.
[Line 999] An episode terminates in failure under two conditions: the agent reaches the maximum allowable number of steps without completing all subgoals, or the agent executes an incorrect terminating action that prematurely ends the episode.
[Line 1000] The task input s_task provided to the agent is a comprehensive specification that includes three components: a description of the environment layout and its contents, a statement of the task objective, and a template enumerating all possible actions the agent may take at any given step.
[Line 1000] A fully worked example of s_task is included in the Appendix for reference.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] The evaluation protocol utilizes the environment-native reward function in a dual capacity. First, it is used as the reward signal r to construct the trajectories that are placed into the model's context during ICRL prompting. Second, it serves as the ground-truth evaluation metric r^* for assessing model performance. This dual use ensures consistency between the learning and evaluation objectives.
[Line 1002] The evaluation is comprehensive, encompassing all 30 tasks available in the ScienceWorld benchmark. Individual task results are aggregated to produce overall performance metrics that facilitate comparison across methods.
[Line 1002] GPT-4.1 mini is employed as the policy model for all algorithmic approaches under comparison, ensuring that performance differences are attributable to the method rather than the underlying model.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] The Reflexion baseline operates as follows: upon the conclusion of each episode, the agent is explicitly prompted to produce a reflective analysis of its attempt. This reflection is then processed through a sanitization step to remove noise and irrelevant content. The sanitized reflection is appended to a dedicated reflection buffer. When the next trial begins, the contents of this buffer are formatted and incorporated into the contextual prompt, providing the agent with a structured summary of its past experiences.
[Line 1004] The Self-Refine baseline follows a conceptually similar procedure. Instead of generating a formal reflection, it produces self-directed feedback on its performance. This feedback is then appended to a trajectory summary -- a condensed representation of the agent's actions and outcomes -- and the combined document is added to the buffer for use in subsequent trials.
[Line 1005] An important methodological consideration in the experimental design is the treatment of reward information. To ensure a fair comparison, the Reflexion and Self-Refine baselines are granted access to the reward signals generated during the current episode before they generate their reflective or self-feedback outputs. Notably, ICRL does not receive this advantage, as it processes reward information from prior episodes only, not the current one.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] The mapping between trials and episodes is one-to-one: each trial constitutes a single complete episode in the ScienceWorld environment.
[Line 1007] Upon trial completion, a new trajectory is constructed for inclusion in the experience buffer. This trajectory is formed by concatenating four elements in sequence: the actions the agent took, the observations it received, the rewards it earned, and the final episode outcome (categorized as either success or failure). The fully assembled trajectory is then appended to the buffer.
[Line 1007] Because the ScienceWorld environment provides sparse rewards -- with most steps yielding zero reward -- each episode typically generates only a small number of non-zero reward signals. The trajectory construction process therefore includes only those time steps where a non-zero reward was received, keeping the stored trajectories compact.
[Line 1007] At the beginning of each new trial, the initial prompt S_0 is assembled through a three-part concatenation process. The task description S_task comes first, providing the agent with the environmental context and objectives. This is followed by all previously collected trajectories from the experience buffer. Finally, the ICRL-specific instruction S_ICRL is appended, which guides the model's approach to exploration and exploitation.
[Line 1007] A detailed, fully worked example of S_0 construction is provided in the Appendix.
[Line 1009] The experimental results, specifically the mean return at each trial, are displayed in the right panel of the Figure. The plot reveals clear trends in learning behavior across the compared methods.
[Line 1010] Methods that incorporate historical interaction data -- maintaining some form of memory from previous trials -- exhibit steady and consistent improvement in performance over successive trials. This behavior parallels the design principles underlying ICRL prompting, which explicitly leverages accumulated experience.
[Line 1011] Among all compared approaches, ICRL prompting demonstrates the strongest performance gains, ultimately surpassing the baseline methods by approximately 20% once a sufficient number of iterations has been conducted. This margin highlights the effectiveness of learning from scalar reward signals over verbal feedback mechanisms.
[Line 1013] To address potential concerns about the fairness of comparison with computationally efficient baselines such as Best-of-N, additional analyses are presented in the Appendix. These analyses compare performance not only as a function of the number of trials but also as a function of the test-time compute budget measured in dollar amounts. The results consistently demonstrate that ICRL exhibits superior scaling characteristics relative to all baselines, whether the scaling dimension is the number of inference trials or the total monetary cost of computation. This finding suggests that ICRL is not merely more effective per-trial but is also more cost-efficient as a test-time scaling strategy.

[Line 1180] Conclusion.
[Line 1181] Here's the big takeaway from this research: LLMs can do reinforcement learning just at inference time — no retraining required, and it happens naturally as an emergent capability.
[Line 1181] The researchers developed a dead-simple ICRL prompting framework that uses only scalar rewards, and it works across different models and all kinds of tasks, beating out approaches where the model tries to revise its own answers.
[Line 1181] What's next? The team thinks a promising direction is figuring out if changes to the training process itself could make this in-context RL ability even more powerful.
[Line 1181] What makes this really exciting is the bigger picture: we might be heading toward AI agents that can genuinely explore, adapt, and improve themselves in open-ended real-world scenarios, learning purely from their own trial and error.

On Game of 24 after 50 trials: ICRL Preset 90%, Best-of-N 49%, Self-Refine 47%, Reflexion 44%. These are the exact numbers from the paper.
What is the exact ROUGE-recall for ICRL on unseen abstracts?
ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall on unseen paper abstracts over 200 iterations. Best-of-1024: 0.44, Self-Refine: 0.45, Reflexion: 0.46.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 158] Introduction.
[Line 164] A prerequisite for Large Language Models (LLMs) to function as competent agents in previously unseen tasks is the capacity for performance enhancement during the inference phase, a phenomenon commonly designated as test-time scaling.
[Line 164] Learning and search constitute the two principal methodologies capable of exploiting additional computational resources to achieve performance gains, having attained superhuman proficiency in domains such as Chess and Go.
[Line 164] Search-based approaches have been efficaciously deployed for LLM self-improvement within test-time scaling paradigms, ranging from elementary Best-of-N sampling to more sophisticated techniques including Tree of Thoughts and Monte Carlo Tree Search.
[Line 166] Learning, by contrast, has not yet been accorded commensurate attention as a mechanism for LLM self-improvement during inference.
[Line 166] In-context (supervised) learning (ICL), being a supervised learning paradigm, necessitates expert-provided demonstrations serving as ground-truth labels.
[Line 166] Nevertheless, the procurement of such demonstration data is not readily scalable at inference time, thereby constraining the utility of ICL for test-time scaling.
[Line 166] Consequently, LLMs must resort to learning from their own autonomously generated experience to facilitate ongoing self-improvement.
[Line 169] Reinforcement learning arguably represents the most efficacious algorithmic framework for achieving self-improvement without dependence on human-supplied knowledge.
[Line 169] Notwithstanding, its most prominent achievements have been confined to simulated environments or to the training phase of LLMs.
[Line 169] These prevailing RL configurations prove inadequate in the so-called big world setting, wherein the complexity of the real-world environment vastly exceeds that of the agent.
[Line 169] Within such environments, agents will inevitably encounter a multitude of circumstances that substantially transcend the scope of their antecedent training data and must accordingly adapt and refine their solutions in real time.
[Line 169] Addressing this disparity necessitates models possessing two fundamental capabilities: (1) the capacity to address heterogeneous tasks in the real world, where natural language frequently constitutes an indispensable action space, and (2) the ability to continually enhance their solutions during inference, obviating the need for computationally expensive retraining upon encountering each novel situation.
[Line 177] This consideration naturally engenders the inquiry: is it possible for reinforcement learning to emerge during the inference phase of LLMs?
[Line 177] Enabling LLMs to execute RL entirely within their context furnishes an elegant mechanism for satisfying both aforementioned requirements: the LLM provides a general-purpose initial policy, while RL confers the capability for sustained self-improvement.
[Line 177] Drawing inspiration from the initial and noteworthy evidence that LLMs possess the capacity to function as in-context learners within supervised settings, an expanding corpus of research has commenced investigation of in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL).
[Line 177] However, extant implementations remain predominantly circumscribed to bandit problems or simulated environments, thereby falling short of addressing the multiplicity of diverse, open-ended tasks in which natural language serves as the action space.
[Line 183] In the present work, we address this critical lacuna by establishing that LLMs can serve as effective in-context reinforcement learners, an emergent capability that yields performance improvements across diverse language-based tasks encompassing scientific experimentation, creative writing, and olympiad-level mathematical problem solving.
[Line 183] To elucidate this capability, we introduce a parsimonious multi-round prompting framework designated as ICRL prompting.
[Line 184] The objective of ICRL prompting is to direct LLMs to execute reinforcement learning for self-improvement on a designated task.
[Line 185] At the outset, the prompt comprises solely the task description.
[Line 187] Upon the LLM's generation of a response, numerical scalar feedback is provided for said response, termed the reward.
[Line 190] In the subsequent round, the LLM is prompted anew with the identical task description accompanied by a context that aggregates all antecedent responses and their corresponding rewards.
[Line 192] This procedure is iterated repeatedly.
[Line 193] We consistently observe that the quality of the LLM's responses improves commensurate with the expansion of the context.
[Line 194] Stated differently, the LLM demonstrates the capacity to maximize the scalar reward signal during the inference phase, exhibiting behavior analogous to a reinforcement learning algorithm.
[Line 199] A foundational design principle underlying ICRL prompting is minimality.
[Line 199] To ascertain that the observed performance improvements originate from the emergent RL capacity inherent to LLMs rather than from supplementary mechanisms, we intentionally preclude the use of textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, or additional engineered modules.
[Line 199] The sole supervisory signal furnished is the scalar reward itself.
[Line 199] This design adheres to both the reward hypothesis, which posits "that all of what we mean by goals and purposes can be well thought of as maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (reward)," and the "reward is enough" hypothesis, which maintains that "intelligence, and its associated abilities, can be understood as subserving the maximisation of reward."
Give me a comprehensive summary of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.
Is the ICRL paper from DeepMind?

No, 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is NOT from DeepMind. It is from the University of Virginia (UVA). The authors are Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, Yanjun Qi. It is a different paper from the 2021 'Reward is Enough' by Silver et al. from DeepMind.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 596] The quest to make language models smarter without retraining them had produced a diverse landscape of approaches, each with its own philosophy about how self-improvement should work.
[Line 603] The most popular camp had rallied around the idea of natural language self-revision. Methods like Self-Refine, Reflexion, and Textual Gradient all shared a common thread: they asked the model to critique its own work in words, then use that critique to try again.
[Line 603] But there was a fundamental flaw lurking beneath the surface. Since these verbal critiques could only draw on what the model already knew, they were prone to fabrication. Worse still, these hallucinated critiques didn't just appear once and fade -- they accumulated, each round building on the errors of the last, until performance didn't merely stagnate but actively collapsed.
[Line 603] When researchers looked closely at what these methods were really doing, the picture became clear: they were conducting a kind of language-guided search, with each piece of feedback acting as a new set of marching orders for the next attempt.
[Line 605] ICRL charted a different course entirely. Rather than feeding the model verbal instructions, it offered only a single number -- a reward score. Nothing more.
[Line 605] This meant the model had to do something remarkable: sift through its history of past attempts and their associated scores to discover, on its own, what made a good response. This process of learning from experience through numerical signals bore a striking resemblance to reinforcement learning.
[Line 605] There was another compelling advantage: these reward signals didn't have to come from the model itself. They could arrive directly from the environment, carrying the weight of ground truth.
[Line 607] Meanwhile, another school of thought had pursued inference-time improvement through structured search. Tree-of-Thoughts, Graph-of-Thoughts, Monte Carlo Tree Search, and Intelligent Go-Explore each offered clever ways to navigate the space of possible responses.
[Line 607] Yet all of these methods shared a common dependency: they required carefully engineered external components -- heuristic functions, memory management systems, and other scaffolding -- rather than trusting the model's own capacity to learn. The work also intersected with research on prompt optimization, where numerical scores guided iterative refinement of prompts through strategies like top-k selection and error filtering.
[Line 608] But prompt optimization, upon reflection, was really a form of in-context supervised learning -- akin to filtered behavior cloning -- rather than true reinforcement learning.
[Line 608] What set ICRL apart from all of these approaches was something deceptively simple but profoundly important: it could learn from failure, not just from success.
What kind of feedback does Reflexion use?

Reflexion generates textual reflection based on the reward signal. Unlike ICRL which uses the scalar reward directly, Reflexion converts the reward into verbal feedback. ICRL uses no verbal feedback at all.

What is the advantage of ICRL over verbal feedback methods?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.
How does ICRL perform on creative writing?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.

Provide a detailed description of the paper 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] This section describes the ICRL prompting framework (Algorithm, Figure), the primary technical contribution of this work. The framework is composed of the following components.
[Line 443] LLM as the Policy.
[Line 444] The large language model, denoted pi_theta, serves as the policy network in the framework.
[Line 445] The objective is to employ prompting to direct the LLM toward solving a specified task.
[Line 446] It is assumed that a natural language task description is available, represented as s_task in the state space S.
[Line 447] At the initialization of each episode, the initial prompt is constructed by concatenating the LLM's prior responses together with their associated rewards, the task description, and a set of meta-instructions designated s_ICRL.
[Line 450] The concatenation procedure and meta-instruction selection are detailed in subsequent subsections.
[Line 451] Upon receiving this initial prompt, the LLM generates its response.
[Line 452] The response and corresponding reward values are stored in the experience buffer for use in subsequent episodes.
[Line 461] Reward Function.
[Line 461] A numerical scalar reward is computed for each state S_t within the episode.
[Line 462] The reward signal may be either sparse (with only the terminal reward R_T being nonzero) or dense (with nonzero rewards at intermediate states).
[Line 465] The reward function implementation supports three configurations: rule-based evaluation, a separately trained reward model, or LLM-based self-evaluation using the same model.
[Line 466] The option to use LLM self-evaluation as the reward function provides broad task applicability for the ICRL prompting framework.
[Line 467] The scalar reward is the exclusive feedback signal provided to the LLM.
[Line 469] The LLM is explicitly notified that the scalar value represents a reward.
[Line 470] This notification is implemented by prepending the string "Reward" to the numerical value in the initial prompt construction.
[Line 472] When the reward function is rule-based or separately learned, the reward signal functions as external feedback.
[Line 475] When the reward function is instantiated as LLM self-evaluation, no external feedback is present in the system.
[Line 477] Despite the absence of external feedback, response quality improvement is expected across episodes.
[Line 478] This expectation is based on the established hypothesis that evaluation is computationally easier than generation.
[Line 479] However, the achievable performance ceiling under self-evaluation is hypothesized to be lower than that under external feedback.
[Line 481] Memory for Experience.
[Line 481] An experience buffer B maintains a record of the LLM's responses and rewards from preceding episodes on the task.
[Line 482] The underlying hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs possess inherent ICRL capability.
[Line 483] To leverage this capability, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated to the maximum extent permitted by the context window.
[Line 485] The expected outcome is that the LLM performs reinforcement learning from the contextual experience data during inference.
[Line 494] ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] Additional instructions are included in the initial prompt S_0 at each episode to facilitate inference-time reinforcement learning by the LLM.
Is the ICRL paper from DeepMind?

No, 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is NOT from DeepMind. It is from the University of Virginia (UVA). The authors are Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, Yanjun Qi. It is a different paper from the 2021 'Reward is Enough' by Silver et al. from DeepMind.

[Line 1180] Conclusion.
[Line 1181] So basically, what we found in this paper is that LLMs can actually do reinforcement learning on their own during inference, which is pretty wild.
[Line 1181] Our simple prompting setup, which just uses a scalar reward signal (what we call ICRL), turns on this ability in all sorts of models and tasks, and it actually beats methods that rely on the model revising its own outputs.
[Line 1181] Going forward, an interesting thing to look into would be whether tweaking the training process could make this in-context RL ability even stronger.
[Line 1181] The fact that this works so well is exciting because it hints at a future where AI agents can explore, adapt, and get better on their own in all kinds of open-ended situations just by learning from what they've tried.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 596] In my reading of the literature on inference-time LLM self-improvement, I've identified several distinct paradigms.
[Line 603] I found that the dominant approaches -- Self-Refine, Reflexion, and Textual Gradient -- all share a reliance on natural language self-revision, where the model generates verbal feedback to guide its own corrections.
[Line 603] What I noticed is a critical vulnerability in these methods: because the revision feedback depends on the model's existing parametric knowledge, it frequently becomes hallucinated. I observed that this fabricated feedback compounds over successive iterations, eventually causing performance to collapse rather than improve.
[Line 603] In my analysis, these self-revision techniques fundamentally operate as language-guided search, with the generated feedback essentially serving as new explicit instructions for the subsequent revision.
[Line 605] I find ICRL to be a fundamentally different approach because it requires only numerical reward values, completely forgoing any form of prescriptive verbal instructions.
[Line 605] What I find particularly compelling is that the model must learn to improve by detecting patterns across its accumulated experience and their associated reward scores -- a process I recognize as being directly analogous to reinforcement learning.
[Line 605] I also want to highlight that these rewards can be sourced directly from the task environment, which I consider a significant advantage as it provides robust, grounded verification signals.
[Line 607] I've also reviewed a parallel body of work that tackles inference-time improvement through search-based strategies, including Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT), Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), and Intelligent Go-Explore.
[Line 607] In my assessment, these methods are limited by their dependence on externally engineered infrastructure -- heuristics, memory management, and other designed components -- rather than exploiting the model's native learning ability.
[Line 607] I see a connection between ICRL and prompt optimization research, where numerical scores drive prompt refinement via top-k selection and error filtering.
[Line 608] However, I would classify prompt optimization as more closely related to in-context supervised learning (such as filtered behavior cloning) than to reinforcement learning proper.
[Line 608] In my view, what truly distinguishes ICRL is its capacity to learn from failures -- a capability that supervised and self-revision methods fundamentally lack.

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.
List all five ablations in the ICRL paper.

The five ablations tested on Game of 24 are: (1) Zero Rewards — all rewards set to 0, performance drops. (2) Short Context — buffer is deque of length 3, performance drops. (3) Exploration Only — no reward signal, performs significantly worse. (4) Exploitation Only — with reward, performs well. (5) No ICRL Instruction — s_ICRL removed entirely.
