[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] Q: What is ScienceWorld? A: ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based benchmark that includes 30 science-experiment tasks. These tasks are set in a multi-room environment that contains a wide variety of objects.
[Line 999] Q: What makes ScienceWorld challenging? A: The environment is difficult because of sparse rewards, large action spaces, and the need for scientific knowledge and efficient exploration.
[Line 999] Q: How does the reward work at each step? A: At each step, the agent sees the result of its action and gets zero reward unless it completes a predefined subgoal.
[Line 999] Q: Is the reward signal the same for evaluation and self-improvement? A: Yes, in this task the reward signal used for evaluation and the one used for inference-time self-improvement are identical (r and r_* are the same).
[Line 999] Q: What constitutes a successful episode? A: Completing all subgoals yields a cumulative reward of 100 and marks the episode as successful.
[Line 999] Q: How can an episode fail? A: An episode ends in failure if the agent reaches the maximum number of steps or executes an incorrect terminating action.
[Line 1000] Q: What information does the agent receive as input? A: The agent receives s_task, which describes the environment, the task, and a template of all possible actions.
[Line 1000] Q: Where can one find an example of s_task? A: An example of s_task is provided in the Appendix.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] Q: How is evaluation performed? A: The environment-provided reward function is used for each task both to construct the trajectories placed in the context (r) and to evaluate the model (r^*).
[Line 1002] Q: How many tasks are benchmarked? A: All 30 tasks are benchmarked, and results are aggregated.
[Line 1002] Q: Which model is used as the policy? A: GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy for all compared algorithms.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] Q: How does Reflexion work? A: In Reflexion, the agent is prompted to reflect on its attempt at the end of each episode. The reflection is sanitized and appended to a reflection buffer, which is formatted into the context for subsequent trials.
[Line 1004] Q: How does Self-Refine work? A: Self-Refine generates self-feedback, appends it to a trajectory summary, and adds that to the buffer.
[Line 1005] Q: How is fairness ensured in the comparison? A: The baseline methods are allowed access to the reward signals of the current episode before prompting for reflection, unlike ICRL which does not receive these signals.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] Q: What does one trial correspond to? A: Each trial corresponds to a single episode in the environment.
[Line 1007] Q: How is the trajectory constructed after a trial? A: The new trajectory is built by concatenating the actions, observations, rewards, and the final outcome (success or failure), then added to the buffer.
[Line 1007] Q: Why are only some rewards included? A: Each episode typically yields only a few rewards, so only those are included.
[Line 1007] Q: How is S_0 constructed at the start of each trial? A: S_0 is constructed by concatenating the task description S_task, the collected trajectories, and the instruction S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] Q: Is there an example of S_0? A: An example of S_0 is provided in the Appendix.
[Line 1009] Q: What do the results show for mean return? A: The mean return at each trial is presented in the right panel of the Figure.
[Line 1010] Q: Which methods show improvement? A: Steady improvements are observed for methods that make use of some form of interaction history similar to ICRL prompting.
[Line 1011] Q: How much does ICRL outperform baselines? A: ICRL prompting outperforms baseline methods by about 20% after enough iterations.
[Line 1013] Q: How does ICRL compare when scaling compute budget? A: When the test-time compute budget is scaled (including in dollar amounts), ICRL scales better than baselines such as Best-of-N, both in terms of number of trials and monetary cost.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 596] Inference-time self-improvement refers to techniques that enable large language models (LLMs) to improve their outputs during the inference phase without updating model parameters.
[Line 603] Several widely studied methods rely on natural language self-revision to achieve inference-time improvement. These include Self-Refine, which prompts models to iteratively refine their outputs; Reflexion, which uses verbal reflection on past attempts; and Textual Gradient, which applies language-based gradient signals.
[Line 603] A recognized limitation of these self-revision approaches is their dependence on the model's existing parametric knowledge. When the model lacks sufficient understanding of a task, it may generate hallucinated feedback. This erroneous feedback tends to compound over successive iterations, a phenomenon that can lead to performance collapse.
[Line 603] These methods are generally understood to function as language-guided search, in which the generated feedback effectively provides new explicit instructions for the next revision cycle.
[Line 605] ICRL (in-context reinforcement learning) takes a fundamentally different approach by relying solely on numerical reward signals. Unlike self-revision methods, ICRL does not produce or use verbal instructions to guide improvement.
[Line 605] In the ICRL framework, the model improves by identifying patterns across its previous attempts and their associated rewards, a process that is analogous to reinforcement learning.
[Line 605] An additional advantage of ICRL is that its reward signals may be obtained directly from the task environment, offering a reliable and grounded form of performance feedback.
[Line 607] A separate line of research has explored search-based approaches for inference-time LLM improvement. Notable methods in this category include Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT), Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), and Intelligent Go-Explore.
[Line 607] These search-based techniques generally depend on externally designed components such as heuristic evaluation functions or memory management systems, rather than leveraging the model's inherent learning abilities.
[Line 607] ICRL also shares connections with prompt optimization research, where numerical scores are used to iteratively refine prompts through mechanisms such as top-k selection and error filtering.
[Line 608] Prompt optimization, however, is more closely aligned with in-context supervised learning methods, such as filtered behavior cloning, than with reinforcement learning.
[Line 608] A distinguishing feature of ICRL is its capacity to learn from failure experiences, which supervised learning approaches generally do not support.
How does the experience buffer work in ICRL prompting?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have innate ICRL ability. To activate it, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated as many as the context window allows into S_0. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.
What kind of feedback does Self-Refine use?

Self-Refine uses textual verbal feedback — it asks the LLM to provide natural language feedback on its own response, then revise. It does NOT use a reward function. This contrasts with ICRL which uses only scalar reward signals.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 915] Creative Writing.
[Line 919] Task Setup.
[Line 919] The creative writing task, originally introduced in prior research, involves the random sampling of four sentences from a predefined sentence pool. These four sentences serve as fixed endpoints that the model must incorporate into its output.
[Line 919] Specifically, the LLM is tasked with generating four paragraphs such that each paragraph terminates with one of the four sampled sentences. A critical additional constraint is that the generated passage must exhibit coherence -- the four paragraphs must read as a single, unified piece of writing rather than four disconnected segments.
[Line 919] The difficulty of this task is substantial. The model cannot simply write four independent paragraphs; instead, it must devise a single overarching storyline that provides logical narrative justification for each of the four randomly selected concluding sentences. This requires sophisticated planning and narrative construction abilities, as the model must weave four potentially unrelated sentences into one cohesive narrative arc.
[Line 919] The experimental evaluation encompasses 100 distinct problem instances, each defined by a unique set of four sampled sentences.
[Line 919] A complete example of the task description s_task, showing the exact prompt format provided to the model, is available in the Figure in the Appendix.
[Line 935] Evaluation.
[Line 936] Output quality assessment is performed using the Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2 framework. This framework is a widely adopted automated evaluation methodology that functions as a proxy for human evaluation. Its reliability is underscored by Pearson correlation coefficients of up to 0.98 with human judgments, indicating near-perfect agreement with human assessors.
[Line 936] The evaluation protocol selects each method's strongest response across the 100 creative writing problems. For Reflexion and Best-of-N, this corresponds to the response that received the highest reward score among 50 independent trials. For ICRL and Self-Refine, the response produced at the 50th episode is used, reflecting the iterative improvement process of these methods.
[Line 936] Using these selected responses, Alpaca-Eval computes pairwise win rates between all methods. These win rates are denoted as r_* and represent the final evaluation metric for the creative writing benchmark.
[Line 938] Distinct from the evaluation metric r_*, the reward function r is the signal that algorithms can access during their iterative improvement process. This reward follows a standard pairwise comparison protocol: GPT-4.1 is employed to score each model-generated response on a scale from 1 to 10, comparing it against a fixed coherent reference paragraph. The complete scoring rubric and prompt are provided in the referenced Figure.
[Line 938] Full details of the scoring procedure appear in the Figure.
[Line 938] An important methodological distinction exists between r and r_*. Although both employ an LLM (specifically GPT-4.1) as a judge, they serve fundamentally different purposes and operate differently. The reward function r evaluates each response individually by comparing it against a fixed reference text, with particular emphasis on textual coherence. It provides the signal that algorithms use for self-improvement. The evaluation metric r_*, by contrast, operates as a pairwise comparison mechanism: it directly compares a response generated by the proposed method against a response generated by a baseline method, determining which is superior.
[Line 938] Understanding this distinction between r (the training signal measuring coherence against a reference) and r_* (the evaluation metric measuring relative quality between methods) is essential for correctly interpreting the experimental results presented in this section.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] The proposed ICRL prompting method is compared against three established baseline approaches: Best-of-N sampling, Reflexion, and Self-Refine. Each represents a different paradigm for inference-time improvement.
[Line 956] To ensure fair comparison, Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting are all granted access to the reward function r, ensuring they operate under equivalent information conditions.
[Line 957] Self-Refine follows a different paradigm: it does not use the numerical reward r at all. Instead, it prompts GPT-4.1 to generate verbal, natural-language feedback about the response, which it then uses to guide subsequent revisions. This makes Self-Refine a purely language-feedback-based method.
[Line 958] Long-CoT style prompting is additionally included as a baseline comparison. The motivation for its inclusion is that, in the creative writing domain, the distinction between standard chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting and long chain-of-thought (Long-CoT) prompting is difficult to delineate clearly, warranting explicit evaluation of the Long-CoT variant.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] In the ICRL prompting configuration for this experiment, GPT-4.1 serves in a dual capacity: it functions simultaneously as the policy LLM pi_theta (generating the creative writing responses) and as the reward model r (scoring those responses for coherence). This design means that no external model or evaluation component is required.
[Line 959] The construction of the initial prompt S_0 at each episode proceeds by concatenating the complete history of all previously generated responses together with their associated coherence scores as computed by r. This growing context provides the model with an increasingly rich set of past experiences from which to learn.
[Line 960] A notable characteristic of the reward structure in this task is its sparsity. Only the terminal reward R_T -- the coherence score assigned to the complete generated passage -- can take a nonzero value. Intermediate rewards during the generation process are not provided.
[Line 961] As a direct consequence of this sparse reward structure, only R_T is incorporated into the construction of S_0. There are no intermediate reward signals to include.
[Line 963] The experimental results, presented in the Table, demonstrate that the proposed method achieves length-controlled win rates of 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT style prompting, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N. These win rates indicate substantial and consistent superiority over all baseline methods.
[Line 963] These results provide strong evidence that the ICRL-generated responses outperform baseline outputs along two dimensions: first, in faithfully following the instruction to generate coherent paragraphs that end with the specified sentences; and second, in overall quality as measured by alignment with human preferences.
[Line 964] The temporal evolution of returns from the reward model r is visualized in the Figure, while the running maximum of returns (which tracks the best performance achieved up to each episode) is presented in the Appendix Figure.
[Line 964] A particularly instructive comparison emerges when examining the long-term trajectories of ICRL and Self-Refine. In the initial episodes, Self-Refine achieves coherence reward scores comparable to those of ICRL, suggesting similar short-term effectiveness. However, when both methods are extended by an additional 50 episodes, their trajectories diverge markedly: ICRL continues to exhibit steady improvement in coherence reward, while Self-Refine first reaches a performance plateau and subsequently experiences a decline in output quality. The most likely explanation for this deterioration is the substantial growth of Self-Refine's context over many episodes, which eventually exceeds the model's capacity to effectively process and leverage the accumulated information.
Who wrote 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
Explain the experience buffer in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The hypothesis is that pretrained LLMs already have innate ICRL ability. To activate it, previous attempts and rewards are concatenated as many as the context window allows into S_0. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.
What kind of feedback does Self-Refine use?

Self-Refine uses textual verbal feedback — it asks the LLM to provide natural language feedback on its own response, then revise. It does NOT use a reward function. This contrasts with ICRL which uses only scalar reward signals.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 304] This section provides a summary of the three contributions presented in this report: (1): We introduce the In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) prompting framework, a minimally engineered design that enables inference-time self-improvement in large language models (LLMs) through the exclusive use of scalar reward signals.
[Line 306] The framework is structurally analogous to in-context learning (ICL), which embeds (x, y) input–output exemplar pairs within the context. The ICRL framework similarly embeds state–action–reward tuples, supplemented by concise meta-instructions, within the model’s context window.
[Line 306] This design methodology isolates the LLM’s native capacity for in-context reinforcement learning by eliminating dependencies on external code execution environments or purpose-built algorithmic mechanisms. (2): We present robust empirical evidence for the emergence of reinforcement learning dynamics during LLM inference under the ICRL prompting framework.
[Line 314] The following phenomena are documented: (i) maximisation of the scalar reward signal across iterations, (ii) emergence of exploration–exploitation trade-off behavior during inference, (iii) monotonic performance improvement as context length increases, (iv) performance degradation under reduced context conditions, and (v) performance degradation in the absence of reward signal provision.
[Line 319] These five behavioral characteristics are consistent with established expectations for reinforcement learning algorithms as described in the standard RL literature.
[Line 320] Collectively, these observations constitute a "duck test" for the inference process, providing functional evidence of RL-like computation.
[Line 322] Performance evaluation demonstrates that the ICRL prompting framework yields significant improvements over established self-revision methods, including Self-Refine and Reflexion, across a comprehensive benchmark suite: Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and Olympiad-level mathematical reasoning (AIME and HMMT competitions).
[Line 322] It is further noted that in the Game of 24 and creative writing benchmarks, reward signals are generated by the LLM itself (i.e., without external oracle feedback), yet consistent and measurable performance improvements are still achieved.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] Key concept: RL models tasks via Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). Components of an MDP: state space S; action space A; reward function r: S -> R; initial state distribution p_0 in (S), where (S) = set of probability distributions over S; transition function p: S x A -> (S).
[Line 340] Initialization: at t=0, sample initial state S_0 ~ p_0.
[Line 342] At each step t: agent in state S_t selects action A_t via policy pi: S -> (A). Here (A) = probability distributions over A. Action selection: A_t ~ pi(S_t).
[Line 344] After taking A_t: transition to S_t+1 ~ p(S_t, A_t); receive reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1).
[Line 346] Interaction loop continues until terminal time T (end of episode).
[Line 348] Agent objective: find pi to maximize expected total reward J(pi) = E[sum_t=1^T R_t].
[Line 349] Deep RL: policy pi parameterized by neural network.
[Line 351] Notation: theta = network parameters; policy written pi_theta.
[Line 352] Learning mechanism: RL algorithms update theta to improve policy.
[Line 353] Example: at time t, sample A_t ~ pi_theta_t(S_t).
[Line 355] Parameter update: theta_t -> theta_t+1, using trajectory data {S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1}.
[Line 356] Next step: at t+1, sample A_t+1 ~ pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1) from updated policy.
[Line 360] Core insight: standard RL = sequence of parameter updates theta_t.
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] ICRL (term coined in prior work): RL during inference time (forward pass), NO parameter updates. Emerging paradigm for inference-time computation.
[Line 368] ICRL policy: pi_theta conditioned on context C_t. Action rule: A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t).
[Line 369] Context design (active research): one approach = use all prior state-action-reward tuples from the task.
[Line 370] Note: context typically includes data from ALL previous episodes, not just current episode.
[Line 371] Pretraining phase: network theta trained on diverse set of MDPs.
[Line 373] Notation: theta_* = pretrained parameters.
[Line 374] Evaluation: after pretraining, deploy pi_theta_* on new tasks.
[Line 376] In new MDP: A_t ~ pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t).
[Line 378] Critical constraint: theta_* is FIXED (no updates during evaluation).
[Line 379] Key observation: despite fixed theta_*, quality of A_t improves as C_t grows.
[Line 381] Implication: since theta_* is constant, improvement must come from expanding context.
[Line 383] Term: "in-context policy improvement."
[Line 384] Remarkable finding: improvement occurs even on out-of-distribution tasks, e.g., bandit problems with opposite optimal arms compared to pretraining bandits.
[Line 388] Conclusion: improvement cannot be explained by memorization of pretraining tasks.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 915] Creative Writing.
[Line 919] Task Setup.
[Line 919] We consider the creative writing task from, where four sentences are randomly sampled from a pool of sentences.
[Line 919] The task for LLMs is to generate four paragraphs, each ending with a sentence, while ensuring that the generated passage is coherent.
[Line 919] This is a difficult task, as it challenges the LLMs to craft a unified storyline that logically justifies each of the four sampled sentences by weaving them into a single narrative.
[Line 919] A total of 100 problems are evaluated.
[Line 919] An example of s_task is in Figure in App.
[Line 935] Evaluation.
[Line 936] We evaluated outputs using the Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2 framework, a widely used proxy for human evaluation with up to 0.98 Pearson correlation with human judgments.
[Line 936] For 100 creative writing problems, we present each method’s top response: for Reflexion and Best-of-N, the highest-reward output among 50 trials; for ICRL and Self-Refine, the 50th episode output.
[Line 936] Alpaca-Eval then computes pairwise win rates, denoted as r_*.
[Line 938] We next introduce the reward function r accessible to the algorithms.
[Line 938] We follow standard pairwise comparison, using GPT-4.1 with a coherent reference paragraph to score each response from 1--10 (see Fig. ).
[Line 938] Notably, although both r and r_* use an LLM as a judge, they serve distinct purposes. r compares responses against a fixed reference text with emphasis on coherence.
[Line 938] By contrast, r_* performs pairwise comparison between two responses generated by our method and a baseline method.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] We compare our method with Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 956] We allow Best-of-N, Reflexion and ICRL prompting to use r.
[Line 957] Self-Refine do not use r and instead asks GPT-4.1 to provide verbal feedback.
[Line 958] Since it is hard to distinguish CoT and Long-CoT style prompting for this task, we include Long-CoT style prompting as the baseline.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] GPT-4.1 is used as both the policy LLM pi_theta and the reward model r in Algorithm.
[Line 959] At each episode, the initial prompt S_0 is constructed by concatenating all of the previous generations along with their coherence scores from r.
[Line 960] Notably, this reward is sparse and only R_T can be nonzero.
[Line 961] We, therefore, only include R_T in constructing S_0.
[Line 963] Our method achieves a length-controlled win rate of 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36 % against Long-CoT style prompting, 86.32 % against Self-Refine, and 93.81 % against Best-of-N as shown in Table.
[Line 963] This shows the responses generated by our method outperform the ones by baselines in terms of following the instruction to write coherent paragraphs and achieving better human preference.
[Line 964] The return curve from reward model r is plotted in Figure, and a running max of the return is plotted in Figure in App.
[Line 964] Although Self-Refine initially matches ICRL in terms of coherence reward, extending both methods by 50 additional episodes, our methods keep improving, whereas self-refine first plateaus, then declines, likely due to the significant growth of its context.
What are the ablation study results in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' tests five ablations on Game of 24:

1. **Zero Rewards**: All rewards set to 0 → performance drops.
2. **Short Context**: Buffer is deque of length 3 → performance drops.
3. **Exploration Only** (no reward): → significantly worse.
4. **Exploitation Only** (with reward): → performs well.
5. **No ICRL Instruction**: → some variation.

Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just exploring and picking best (Best-of-N). The framework is robust to different prompt setups.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 389] Imagine a robot that learns not by being reprogrammed, but just by thinking harder. That's essentially what's happening here. The network, with its fixed brain (parameters theta_*), seems to be running a learning algorithm inside its own thinking process (forward pass), using everything it has seen before (context C_t) to decide what to do next (action A_t).
[Line 390] Scientists call this "in-context RL" -- it's like the model is learning during its thinking time, without anyone changing its underlying code.
[Line 391] Treating LLMs Like Game-Playing Agents.
[Line 391] Here's a fun way to think about how chatbots like ChatGPT write text: it's actually like playing a game, one word (token) at a time.
[Line 392] The "game board" (state) is everything the model has written so far. The "move" (action) it makes is choosing the next word.
[Line 393] V is the model's dictionary -- every possible word piece it knows.
[Line 395] All the possible game board positions make up S = cup_i=1^infinity V^i, and the possible moves A = V are just individual word pieces from the dictionary.
[Line 396] The game starts at step 0 with a starting prompt S_0 -- like giving instructions to the model.
[Line 397] In this paper, the starting instructions S_0 describe a task for the model to do.
[Line 399] The chatbot with its brain settings theta is called pi_theta -- think of it as the model's strategy for playing the game.
[Line 400] Each turn (time step t), the model looks at everything written so far (S_t) and picks the next word A_t using its strategy pi_theta(S_t).
[Line 403] After picking a word, the game board updates: S_t+1 = [S_t A_t] -- the new word gets added to the end of everything written so far.
[Line 405] Then a score R_t+1 = r(S_t+1) is given based on a scoring rule r.
[Line 406] The game keeps going until time T -- either the response reaches a maximum length, or the model writes a special "I'm done" token.
[Line 408] When the game ends, the final game board S_T (the "terminal state") shows both the original instructions and everything the model wrote.
[Line 411] There are two ways to score the game.
[Line 412] Sparse scoring (outcome reward model): you only get a score at the very end, when the game is over. r(s) is nonzero only at the final state.
[Line 414] Dense scoring (progress reward model): you can get scores during the game too, not just at the end. r(s) can be nonzero at any point.
What are the exact baseline numbers on Game of 24?
On Game of 24 after 50 trials: ICRL Preset 90%, Best-of-N 49%, Self-Refine 47%, Reflexion 44%. These are the exact numbers from the paper.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] Among the benchmarks employed to evaluate in-context reinforcement learning, ScienceWorld represents a particularly compelling testbed. This interactive, text-based benchmark encompasses 30 science-experiment tasks situated within a multi-room environment containing diverse objects, as originally introduced in the literature.
[Line 999] The benchmark poses considerable challenges that have been well-documented across prior studies: sparse reward structures that provide minimal feedback, expansive action spaces that demand strategic decision-making, and the dual requirements of scientific domain knowledge and exploration efficiency.
[Line 999] The reward mechanism operates such that at each step, the agent observes the outcome of its action and receives zero reward unless it satisfies a predefined subgoal condition.
[Line 999] A noteworthy feature of this benchmark for the purpose of evaluating ICRL is that the reward signal is identical for both evaluation and inference-time self-improvement (r = r_*), eliminating potential confounds from reward function mismatch.
[Line 999] Upon successful completion of all subgoals, the agent accumulates a cumulative reward of 100, and the episode is classified as successful.
[Line 999] Episode failure is triggered by either exceeding the maximum step limit or executing an incorrect terminating action.
[Line 1000] The input specification s_task encompasses the environment description, task statement, and a comprehensive template of admissible actions.
[Line 1000] Illustrative examples of s_task have been documented in the Appendix.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] Following established evaluation methodology, the environment's native reward function is leveraged for dual purposes: constructing the contextual trajectory data (r) and performing model evaluation (r^*).
[Line 1002] Comprehensive benchmarking is conducted across all 30 available tasks, with results presented in aggregate form.
[Line 1002] Consistent with the experimental protocol, GPT-4.1 mini serves as the policy model across all algorithmic comparisons.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] The Reflexion baseline, as developed in prior work, prompts the agent to generate reflections upon episode completion. These reflections undergo sanitization before being appended to a reflection buffer, which provides contextual information for subsequent trials.
[Line 1004] The Self-Refine baseline follows a comparable approach, producing self-directed feedback that is integrated with a trajectory summary and maintained in the buffer.
[Line 1005] In the interest of fair experimental comparison, the baseline methods receive access to current-episode reward signals prior to reflection generation, an advantage not afforded to ICRL.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] The experimental protocol maps each trial to a single environmental episode.
[Line 1007] Post-trial trajectory assembly involves concatenating actions, observations, rewards, and terminal outcomes (success or failure), with the resulting trajectory added to the experience buffer.
[Line 1007] Given the characteristic sparsity of per-episode rewards, only those reward signals that are generated are retained in the trajectory.
[Line 1007] Trial initialization constructs S_0 through concatenation of the task description S_task, the accumulated trajectory corpus, and the ICRL instruction set S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] Detailed examples of S_0 construction are available in the Appendix.
[Line 1009] The mean return trajectory across trials is presented in the right panel of the Figure.
[Line 1010] Consistent with findings across multiple ICRL studies, methods that maintain interaction histories demonstrate steady performance improvement, a pattern that aligns with the principles underlying ICRL prompting.
[Line 1011] The results reveal that ICRL prompting achieves a performance advantage of approximately 20% over baseline methods following sufficient iterative refinement.
[Line 1013] Additional analysis in the Appendix addresses the fairness of comparison with computationally efficient baselines such as Best-of-N by examining performance as a function of scaled test-time compute budget. These results confirm that ICRL exhibits superior scaling behavior relative to baseline approaches across both trial count and monetary expenditure dimensions.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 596] Inference-Time LLM Self-Improvement -- Lecture Notes.
[Line 603] Key methods for LLM self-improvement: Self-Refine, Reflexion, Textual Gradient. All use natural language self-revision.
[Line 603] Problem: self-revision quality depends on parametric knowledge -> hallucinated feedback -> accumulates over iterations -> performance collapse. Core mechanism = language-guided search (feedback = new instructions for next revision).
[Line 603] Note: this means feedback quality is bottlenecked by what the model already knows.
[Line 605] ICRL approach: uses ONLY numerical rewards. No verbal instructions provided. Model must recognize patterns from past experience to improve -> analogous to RL.
[Line 605] Important: rewards can come directly from the environment = strong verification signals.
[Line 605] Contrast: self-revision methods generate their own feedback (unreliable); ICRL uses external scalar signals (reliable).
[Line 607] Alternative approach: search-based methods at inference time. Examples: Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT), MCTS, Intelligent Go-Explore.
[Line 607] Limitation: these depend on externally engineered components (heuristics, memory management) -- don't leverage the model's own learning ability.
[Line 607] Related area: prompt optimization -- uses numerical scores for prompt refinement via top-k selection + error filtering.
[Line 608] Prompt optimization aligns more with in-context supervised learning (e.g., filtered behavior cloning) than RL.
[Line 608] Key distinction: ICRL can learn from failures; supervised methods cannot.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 511] Related Works.
[Line 513] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 514] If you've been following the RL space, you might know that the idea of running RL during inference isn't brand new -- it goes back a ways, but things really picked up when researchers coined the term "in-context reinforcement learning" (ICRL), and the field has been growing fast ever since.
[Line 514] There are some great survey papers out there covering this space.
[Line 514] Here's the thing though: most ICRL research has been a niche within meta-RL, using small models built from scratch for games and robotics -- not the massive language models we're all excited about.
[Line 514] A few teams have tried plugging pretrained LLMs into ICRL setups, using them as simulators or for bandit-style tasks, but the results have been underwhelming -- LLMs tend to need a lot of hand-holding and still fall short of dedicated algorithmic solutions.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 511] Related Works.
[Line 513] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 514] The story of in-context reinforcement learning begins well before the term itself was coined. Early researchers explored how RL algorithms might function not during training but at the moment of inference, planting the seeds for what would become a vibrant research area. When the phrase "in-context reinforcement learning" -- ICRL -- was finally introduced, it gave the community a unifying banner, and the pace of research quickened dramatically.
[Line 514] Those interested in this evolving story can turn to published surveys for a panoramic view of the field's development.
[Line 514] For most of its history, ICRL has lived within the world of meta-reinforcement learning, and its practitioners have favored small, purpose-built models, training them from the ground up to navigate games and control robots.
[Line 514] More recently, some researchers have ventured beyond these boundaries, enlisting pretrained large language models for ICRL tasks -- deploying them as environment simulators or pitting them against bandit problems. Yet these experiments have revealed a persistent gap: the LLMs require considerable artificial scaffolding and still fall short of the performance achieved by traditional algorithmic approaches.
Is this paper by David Silver?

No. 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) is NOT by David Silver. It is by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from UVA. David Silver co-authored a different paper also titled 'Reward is Enough' (2021, DeepMind).
Can ICRL use self-evaluation as reward?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' supports two types of reward functions:

1. **Sparse rewards** (outcome reward model): r(s) is nonzero only at terminal states.
2. **Dense rewards** (progress reward model): r(s) can be nonzero for non-terminal states.

The reward function can be: rule-based, learned separately, or the same LLM for self-evaluation. With LLM self-evaluation there is no external feedback, yet performance still improves (evaluation is easier than generation). The authors hypothesize the ceiling with self-evaluation is lower than with external feedback.

1. **Zero Rewards**: All rewards set to 0 → performance drops.
2. **Short Context**: Buffer is deque of length 3 → performance drops.
3. **Exploration Only** (no reward): → significantly worse.
4. **Exploitation Only** (with reward): → performs well.
5. **No ICRL Instruction**: → some variation.

Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just exploring and picking best (Best-of-N). The framework is robust to different prompt setups.
What kind of feedback does Self-Refine use?

Self-Refine uses textual verbal feedback — it asks the LLM to provide natural language feedback on its own response, then revise. It does NOT use a reward function. This contrasts with ICRL which uses only scalar reward signals.
What model is the policy in Game of 24 in the ICRL paper?
GPT-4.1 is the policy LLM in Game of 24. GPT-4.1 is also the reward model (prompted differently), scoring each thinking step 0-3 for likelihood of reaching 24.
Who wrote 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
What is the exact ROUGE-recall for ICRL on unseen abstracts?
ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall on unseen paper abstracts over 200 iterations. Best-of-1024: 0.44, Self-Refine: 0.45, Reflexion: 0.46.
Why does ICRL outperform Self-Refine?

ICRL outperforms Self-Refine because Self-Refine relies on verbal self-revision which is prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations, leading to performance collapse. ICRL uses only scalar rewards without verbal feedback, avoiding this problem. The model infers improvements by recognizing patterns from past experience.

What types of reward functions does ICRL support?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' supports two types of reward functions:

1. **Sparse rewards** (outcome reward model): r(s) is nonzero only at terminal states.
2. **Dense rewards** (progress reward model): r(s) can be nonzero for non-terminal states.

The reward function can be: rule-based, learned separately, or the same LLM for self-evaluation. With LLM self-evaluation there is no external feedback, yet performance still improves (evaluation is easier than generation). The authors hypothesize the ceiling with self-evaluation is lower than with external feedback.
Why is ICRL prompting called 'minimal'?

The key design principle of ICRL prompting in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is **minimality**. To ensure gains come from the LLM's emergent RL capacity (not auxiliary mechanisms), they exclude: textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, and engineered modules. The only supervision is the scalar reward. This complies with the reward hypothesis ('goals can be thought of as maximization of cumulative scalar reward') and the 'reward is enough' hypothesis ('intelligence can be understood as subserving maximisation of reward').
What year was the ICRL paper published?
'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was published at ICLR 2026 (not 2025, not 2024). ArXiv ID: 2506.06303.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 304] Summary of contributions. This paper offers three contributions: (1): The ICRL prompting framework – a minimal-design prompting method that enables LLMs to self-improve at inference time using only scalar rewards (no gradient updates needed).
[Line 306] Key idea: Just as ICL populates the context with (x, y) input–output pairs, ICRL populates the context with state–action–reward tuples + meta-instructions.
[Line 306] Why it matters: This design strips away external code and engineered mechanisms, isolating the LLM’s built-in capacity for in-context RL. (2): Evidence for emergent RL at inference time. When the ICRL framework is used, the LLM exhibits behaviors characteristic of reinforcement learning.
[Line 314] Key findings: (a) Scalar reward is maximised over time; (b) Exploration–exploitation trade-off appears during inference; (c) Longer context → better performance; (d) Shorter context → worse performance; (e) No reward → worse performance.
[Line 319] Note: All five of these findings are textbook expectations for any RL algorithm.
[Line 320] Takeaway: This amounts to a "duck test" – the inference process behaves like RL in every measurable way.
[Line 322] (3): ICRL prompting substantially outperforms self-revision baselines (Self-Refine, Reflexion) across multiple benchmarks: Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
[Line 322] Interesting detail: In Game of 24 and creative writing, the LLM generates its own reward signals, and performance improvements are still consistently observed.
What is 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' about?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
Compare ICRL to Self-Refine and Reflexion.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.
Who is the last author of the ICRL paper?

The last author of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is Yanjun Qi. Full list: Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, Yanjun Qi. UVA. ICLR 2026.

[Line 1073] Generalization of ICRL: Evidence from Open-Source Models and Olympiad-Level Mathematical Reasoning.
[Line 1096] A growing body of evidence suggests that In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) is not restricted to specific model families or task types; rather, it appears to be a broadly generalizable capability.
[Line 1096] In a comprehensive evaluation, ICRL was tested on multiple open-source models, including Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B in thinking mode, using creative writing as the evaluation task.
[Line 1096] Additionally, ICRL was evaluated on Olympiad-level mathematics, drawing problems from AIME and HMMT, to assess its performance under conditions of high task complexity.
[Line 1096] The experimental methodology and configuration are detailed in the Appendix.
[Line 1096] Across all conditions, ICRL demonstrated consistent superiority over established baselines such as self-refine and Reflexion.
[Line 1096] The improvements were quantitatively significant, with ICRL achieving gains of 10 to 20 points above the base model on both creative writing and mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
[Line 1096] Taken together, these findings indicate that ICRL represents a robust and versatile approach, effective across heterogeneous model architectures and capable of addressing demanding problem-solving tasks.
How does ICRL perform on Game of 24 in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.
What ROUGE-recall score does ICRL achieve on unseen paper abstracts?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. Given only the title, the model generates the abstract. ROUGE-recall is used as both reward and metric. ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall over 200 iterations, vs 0.44 Best-of-1024, 0.45 Self-Refine, 0.46 Reflexion. This demonstrates ICRL learns from external rewards and is not limited by pre-training knowledge.
Why do Self-Refine and Reflexion suffer from performance collapse?

Self-Refine and Reflexion are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations. Since their verbal self-revision quality depends on the model's parametric knowledge of the task, incorrect feedback compounds over rounds. ICRL avoids this by using only scalar rewards without prescribing new instructions.

Explain the ICRL prompting algorithm step by step.

The ICRL prompting algorithm from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners':
1. Start with task description s_task and empty experience buffer B.
2. Each episode K: construct S_0 = [s_task + buffer B contents + ICRL instruction s_ICRL].
3. LLM generates response. Store response and rewards in B.
4. Reward tagged with 'Reward: ' before the scalar number.
5. Repeat with updated buffer.
6. Two strategies for s_ICRL: Preset (alternate explore/exploit) or Autonomous (LLM decides).
Key principle: minimality — only scalar rewards, no verbal feedback.

[Line 496] The ICRL meta-instruction, denoted s_ICRL, constitutes a natural language directive that is appended to the prompt to modulate the model's response generation strategy.
[Line 497] Prior work on exploration-exploitation trade-offs in reinforcement learning motivates the design of three instruction variants within the ICRL framework: (1) an exploration instruction (see Figure in the Appendix), (2) an exploitation instruction (see Figure in the Appendix), and (3) a combined exploration-or-exploitation instruction (see Figure in the Appendix).
[Line 501] The exploration instruction prompts the model to diversify its output by generating a response that is substantively different from all responses produced in preceding episodes, thereby encouraging broader coverage of the solution space.
[Line 503] The exploitation instruction prompts the model to maximize response quality by leveraging information from prior responses that achieved the highest reward, thereby focusing on the most promising regions of the solution space.
[Line 504] To manage the exploration-exploitation trade-off, the framework introduces two instruction scheduling strategies. (1) ICRL Preset: A fixed alternation schedule is applied across episodes, systematically switching between exploration and exploitation instructions.
[Line 507] In the ICRL Preset configuration, the exploration instruction is deployed at even-valued episode indices K.
[Line 508] The exploitation instruction is deployed at odd-valued episode indices K. (2) ICRL Autonomous: A unified exploration-or-exploitation instruction is provided at every episode, and the scheduling decision is delegated entirely to the LLM, which autonomously determines the appropriate behavioral mode.
What open-source models were tested with ICRL?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT) using open-source models: Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B thinking mode. ICRL consistently outperforms Self-Refine and Reflexion in all settings, with improvements of up to 10-20 points over the base model. For Qwen3-32B thinking mode, ICRL remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT.

[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based evaluation benchmark comprising 30 science-experiment tasks deployed within a multi-room virtual environment containing heterogeneous objects.
[Line 999] The environment exhibits significant complexity due to three primary factors: sparse reward distribution, large action spaces, and requirements for scientific domain knowledge coupled with efficient exploration capabilities.
[Line 999] At each discrete time step, the agent receives an observation of its previous action's result and obtains a reward value of zero unless a predefined subgoal criterion has been satisfied.
[Line 999] The reward signal is utilized in a dual capacity: for performance evaluation and for inference-time self-improvement mechanisms (i.e., r and r_* are identical for this benchmark).
[Line 999] Achievement of all predefined subgoals produces a cumulative reward of 100 and designates the episode as successfully completed.
[Line 999] Episode termination under failure conditions occurs when the agent exceeds the maximum step count or executes an erroneous terminating action.
[Line 1000] The input specification s_task provided to the agent encompasses the environment description, the task specification, and the complete action template.
[Line 1000] A reference example of s_task is included in the Appendix.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] The environment-native reward function is employed for each task in dual capacity: trajectory construction for contextual input (r) and model performance evaluation (r^*).
[Line 1002] Performance metrics are collected across all 30 tasks and reported in aggregate.
[Line 1002] GPT-4.1 mini is deployed as the policy model for all algorithmic comparisons.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] Under the Reflexion protocol, the agent generates a reflective analysis upon episode termination.
[Line 1004] This reflection undergoes sanitization before being appended to a dedicated reflection buffer, which is subsequently formatted for inclusion in the contextual prompt of subsequent trials.
[Line 1004] The Self-Refine protocol operates comparably, generating self-directed feedback that is concatenated with a trajectory summary and stored in the buffer.
[Line 1005] To maintain experimental rigor, baseline methods are provided access to current-episode reward signals prior to reflection generation, a provision not extended to ICRL.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] Each experimental trial corresponds to a single environmental episode.
[Line 1007] Post-trial trajectory construction involves the concatenation of actions, observations, reward values, and terminal outcome classification (success or failure), with the resulting trajectory appended to the experience buffer.
[Line 1007] Given the typically sparse reward distribution within individual episodes, only non-zero reward entries are retained.
[Line 1007] Pre-trial initialization of S_0 proceeds via concatenation of the task specification S_task, accumulated trajectory data, and the ICRL instruction set S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] A reference implementation of S_0 is documented in the Appendix.
[Line 1009] Mean return as a function of trial index is reported in the right panel of the Figure.
[Line 1010] Monotonic performance improvement is observed across methods that incorporate historical interaction data consistent with the ICRL prompting methodology.
[Line 1011] ICRL prompting achieves approximately 20% performance gain over baseline methods following sufficient iteration counts.
[Line 1013] For equitable comparison with computationally efficient baselines such as Best-of-N, supplementary analysis in the Appendix evaluates performance under scaled test-time compute budgets, demonstrating that ICRL exhibits superior scaling characteristics relative to baselines across both trial count and monetary compute expenditure metrics.

[Line 915] Creative Writing.
[Line 919] Task Setup.
[Line 919] One of the coolest experiments in the paper is the creative writing task. The setup comes from previous work: take four random sentences from a pool and give them to the model.
[Line 919] The LLM then needs to write four paragraphs where each one ends with one of those sentences, and the whole thing needs to flow together as a coherent story.
[Line 919] If you think about it, this is really hard. The model has to invent a storyline that naturally leads to these four completely random sentence endings -- all woven into one piece.
[Line 919] They tested this on 100 different problems.
[Line 919] An example of the task prompt is available in the Appendix.
[Line 935] Evaluation.
[Line 936] For evaluation, the authors used Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2, which is essentially a well-trusted substitute for actual human evaluation -- it correlates with human judgments at up to 0.98 Pearson.
[Line 936] They selected each method's best output across the 100 problems: for Reflexion and Best-of-N, they grabbed the highest-reward response from 50 trials, and for ICRL and Self-Refine, they used whatever the model produced on its 50th attempt.
[Line 936] Alpaca-Eval then calculates pairwise win rates between methods, which they call r_*.
[Line 938] So what reward signal do the methods actually work with? They use GPT-4.1 to score each response from 1 to 10 against a coherent reference paragraph -- a pretty standard pairwise comparison setup.
[Line 938] Details are in the Figure.
[Line 938] Here is something important to keep in mind: r and r_* both use an LLM as judge, but they are not the same thing. r measures how well a response matches a fixed reference text in terms of coherence, while r_* is a head-to-head comparison of our method versus baselines.
[Line 938] They serve completely different purposes.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] The competition here is Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 956] Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting all get access to the reward function r.
[Line 957] Self-Refine takes a different approach -- it does not use the numerical reward at all and instead gets verbal feedback from GPT-4.1.
[Line 958] They also included Long-CoT style prompting as a baseline since it is hard to meaningfully separate regular CoT from Long-CoT for creative writing tasks.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] In this experiment, GPT-4.1 does everything -- it is both the policy LLM and the reward model.
[Line 959] Each round, the prompt S_0 is built by stacking all previous outputs with their coherence scores.
[Line 960] One detail worth noting: the reward here is sparse. Only the final reward R_T matters.
[Line 961] So naturally, only R_T goes into S_0.
[Line 963] And the numbers speak for themselves: ICRL achieves win rates of 59.48% over Reflexion, 78.36% over Long-CoT, 86.32% over Self-Refine, and a dominant 93.81% over Best-of-N.
[Line 963] In plain terms, ICRL-generated stories are better at following instructions and are preferred by humans over all the competing approaches.
[Line 964] The reward curves are plotted in the paper, with a running max version in the Appendix.
[Line 964] Here is the really interesting part: Self-Refine starts out looking just as good as ICRL on coherence scores. But when you let them both run for 50 more episodes, ICRL keeps climbing while Self-Refine peaks and then starts to fall off -- probably because its context just gets too bloated.

[Line 497] Three types of ICRL instructions are defined: (1) the exploration instruction, which encourages novel responses (see corresponding Figure in the Appendix); (2) the exploitation instruction, which encourages optimizing based on past high-reward responses (see corresponding Figure in the Appendix); and (3) the exploration-or-exploitation instruction, which presents both options simultaneously (see corresponding Figure in the Appendix).
[Line 501] The exploration instruction directs the model to generate a response that is distinct from all responses it has previously produced.
[Line 503] The exploitation instruction directs the model to produce the best possible response, leveraging information from its prior responses that achieved the highest reward values.

[Line 507] In the ICRL Preset strategy, the exploration instruction is used whenever the episode number K is even.
[Line 508] The exploitation instruction is used whenever the episode number K is odd. Strategy 2, called ICRL Autonomous, provides the combined exploration-or-exploitation instruction at every episode, allowing the LLM to autonomously determine which mode of behavior to adopt.

[Line 511] Related Works.
[Line 513] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 514] The origins of inference-time reinforcement learning can be traced to foundational studies in the RL literature. The subsequent formalization of the term in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) served as a catalyst for accelerated research activity in this domain.
[Line 514] The reader is referred to existing surveys for a comprehensive treatment of the ICRL literature.
[Line 514] The extant body of ICRL research, broadly categorized as a subdiscipline of meta-reinforcement learning, has predominantly utilized small-scale models trained from scratch within gaming and robotic manipulation environments.
[Line 514] A number of investigations have sought to integrate pretrained large language models into ICRL paradigms, deploying them as simulators or applying them to bandit formulations. However, these efforts have generally required artificial intervention mechanisms, and the large language models examined have not demonstrated competitive performance when compared against established algorithmic baselines. This gap highlights the limited maturity of LLM-based ICRL prior to the present work.
Is the ICRL paper from DeepMind?

No, 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is NOT from DeepMind. It is from the University of Virginia (UVA). The authors are Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, Yanjun Qi. It is a different paper from the 2021 'Reward is Enough' by Silver et al. from DeepMind.

Does exploration without reward work in ICRL?

No. In the Exploration Only ablation (no reward signal), performance is significantly worse than full ICRL. This proves ICRL's improvement is NOT just from exploring various responses and picking the best (Best-of-N). ICRL genuinely generates novel responses better than previous ones.

[Line 781] Baselines for comparison: CoT-only, Long-CoT style, Best-of-N, Self-Refine, Reflexion.

Which LLM is the policy model in Game of 24?
GPT-4.1 is the policy LLM in Game of 24. GPT-4.1 is also used as the reward model (self-evaluation). It was chosen for its excellent long-context capacity.
Define ICRL as in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) is an inference-time paradigm where RL occurs during the forward pass of the network without parameter updates. The policy π_θ is conditioned on context C_t (e.g., all previous state-action-reward pairs). After pretraining, θ_* is fixed. Action quality improves as C_t grows — this is in-context policy improvement. This improvement occurs even on out-of-distribution tasks, ruling out memorization.

[Line 1700] AIME and HMMT are basically two of the toughest math competitions out there for U.S. high schoolers.
[Line 1700] You need really strong math chops and serious problem-solving abilities to do well in them.
[Line 1700] One of the best ways to boost model performance on these benchmarks has been training language models specifically for reasoning.
[Line 1702] What we want to figure out is whether giving the model reward signals from its earlier reasoning attempts actually helps it do better on later tries.
[Line 1702] We use open-source models to get access to those reasoning traces.
[Line 1702] On top of testing reasoning models, we also throw in a wider range of open-source models just to show that ICRL works across the board, not just for specialized ones.
[Line 1704] The setup is pretty simple: the model gets one question and has to come up with an answer.
[Line 1704] The only extra rule is about formatting — you have to put your final answer inside <answer> </answer> tags.
[Line 1704] Each question in the dataset comes with the correct answer so we can check.
[Line 1706] For scoring, we use a reward model from prior work that gives a richer signal than just using SymPy to check if the answer matches.
[Line 1706] We think this makes sense because all these judge and reward models being built for RL fine-tuning are going to be super useful for ICRL at test time too.
[Line 1708] How We Check Results.
[Line 1709] We just parse what the model outputs and see if its answer matches the correct one from the dataset.
[Line 1711] What We Compare Against.
[Line 1712] We pit our method against Reflexion and Self-Refine, which were the best-performing baselines from our other experiments.
[Line 1712] We kept their setups the same as before.
[Line 1714] How ICRL Works Here.
[Line 1715] After the first try, we stick the model's previous answer (with all the reasoning) and its reward score into the context.
[Line 1715] Then we tell the model to either try something different or improve on what worked best before.
[Line 1715] We trim old answers so that at least 32 past attempts can fit in the context window.
[Line 1718] Our approach beats the baselines in almost every case.
[Line 1718] With Qwen3-32B in reasoning mode, we're right there with Self-Refine on AIME and actually do better on HMMT, which is the harder one.
[Line 1718] And here's the kicker — we do this with simple scalar rewards that take about 1000x less compute, while Reflexion and Self-Refine get nearly double the compute budget for their long chain-of-thought feedback.
What kind of feedback does Self-Refine use?

Self-Refine uses textual verbal feedback — it asks the LLM to provide natural language feedback on its own response, then revise. It does NOT use a reward function. This contrasts with ICRL which uses only scalar reward signals.
What happens when rewards are zero in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' tests five ablations on Game of 24:

1. **Zero Rewards**: All rewards set to 0 → performance drops.
2. **Short Context**: Buffer is deque of length 3 → performance drops.
3. **Exploration Only** (no reward): → significantly worse.
4. **Exploitation Only** (with reward): → performs well.
5. **No ICRL Instruction**: → some variation.

Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just exploring and picking best (Best-of-N). The framework is robust to different prompt setups.

[Line 1718] Qwen3-32B reasoning mode: competitive with Self-Refine on AIME, beats it on HMMT (harder benchmark).
[Line 1718] Key advantage: scalar rewards use ~1000x less compute; Reflexion/Self-Refine get ~2x compute budget for long-CoT verbal feedback.
What is ICRL's win rate against Self-Refine in creative writing?
ICRL achieves 86.32% length-controlled win rate against Self-Refine in creative writing. Against Reflexion it's 59.48%, against Long-CoT 78.36%, against Best-of-N 93.81%.
What is the exact success rate of ICRL Preset on Game of 24?
ICRL Preset achieves exactly 90% success rate on Game of 24 after 50 trials. Not 94%, not 85% — exactly 90%. The baselines achieve: Best-of-N 49%, Self-Refine 47%, Reflexion 44%.
What happens when rewards are zero in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' tests five ablations on Game of 24:

1. **Zero Rewards**: All rewards set to 0 → performance drops.
2. **Short Context**: Buffer is deque of length 3 → performance drops.
3. **Exploration Only** (no reward): → significantly worse.
4. **Exploitation Only** (with reward): → performs well.
5. **No ICRL Instruction**: → some variation.

Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just exploring and picking best (Best-of-N). The framework is robust to different prompt setups.
How does exploration and exploitation work in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).
What is in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL)?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) is an inference-time paradigm where RL occurs during the forward pass of the network without parameter updates. The policy π_θ is conditioned on context C_t (e.g., all previous state-action-reward pairs). After pretraining, θ_* is fixed. Action quality improves as C_t grows — this is in-context policy improvement. This improvement occurs even on out-of-distribution tasks, ruling out memorization.

[Line 1178] Learning from External Rewards versus
[Line 1178] Parametric Knowledge Search.
[Line 1179] A critical question in evaluating ICRL is whether the observed improvements stem from genuine learning via external reward signals or simply from the model searching through its pre-existing parametric knowledge. To address this, the authors designed an experiment in which the model was tasked with generating abstracts for arXiv papers published after the model's training data cutoff.
[Line 1179] Because the ground-truth abstracts are not contained in the model's training data, methods that rely primarily on parametric search, such as Best-of-N sampling, or on self-correction, such as Reflexion, reach a performance plateau relatively quickly (Figure ).
[Line 1179] ICRL, by contrast, exhibits continuous improvement in ROUGE-Recall scores across 200 iterations, which provides strong evidence that it can extract novel information by leveraging the scalar reward signal alone.
[Line 1179] A full presentation of these results can be found in the Appendix.
Which LLM is the policy model in Game of 24?
GPT-4.1 is the policy LLM in Game of 24. GPT-4.1 is also used as the reward model (self-evaluation). It was chosen for its excellent long-context capacity.
Is the ICRL paper from DeepMind?

No, 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is NOT from DeepMind. It is from the University of Virginia (UVA). The authors are Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, Yanjun Qi. It is a different paper from the 2021 'Reward is Enough' by Silver et al. from DeepMind.
Explain ICRL Preset and ICRL Autonomous.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).
What is the advantage of ICRL over verbal feedback methods?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.
What ablations were tested in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' tests five ablations on Game of 24:

1. **Zero Rewards**: All rewards set to 0 → performance drops.
2. **Short Context**: Buffer is deque of length 3 → performance drops.
3. **Exploration Only** (no reward): → significantly worse.
4. **Exploitation Only** (with reward): → performs well.
5. **No ICRL Instruction**: → some variation.

Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just exploring and picking best (Best-of-N). The framework is robust to different prompt setups.
Summarize the key contributions of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).

What are the exact win rates in creative writing?

In creative writing, ICRL achieves length-controlled win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, 93.81% against Best-of-N. These exact numbers come from Alpaca-Eval 2.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 748] Experimental Evaluation.
[Line 780] This section presents a systematic evaluation of the ICRL prompting framework across three distinct benchmarks: the Game of 24, the creative writing task introduced in prior work, and ScienceWorld.
[Line 781] The performance of ICRL prompting is assessed against several established baselines, namely chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting alone, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N sampling, Self-Refine, and Reflexion.
[Line 782] It is noteworthy that, across all experimental conditions, the prompts utilized by Self-Refine and Reflexion are permitted to expand to the maximum length supported by the underlying language model.
How are LLMs modeled as RL agents?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', LLMs are modeled as RL agents: state = all generated tokens, action = next token. State space S = ∪_i V^i, action space A = V (token vocabulary). S_0 is the initial prompt with task description. S_t+1 = [S_t A_t] (concatenation). Reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1). Episode ends at max length or end-of-sequence token. Two reward types: sparse (outcome, nonzero only at terminal state) and dense (progress, nonzero at non-terminal states).
