[Line 800] Game of 24 Experiment.
[Line 824] Task Configuration.
[Line 824] The Game of 24 task presents the model with four input numbers. The objective is to use each number exactly once, applying only the four basic arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) in any sequence, to obtain a result of 24.
[Line 825] GPT-4.1 was selected for this experiment due to its strong long-context processing capability. Model access was provided through API endpoints.
[Line 826] Consistent with prior methodology, chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting was employed to elicit step-by-step reasoning. At each step, the model selects two numbers from the remaining pool and one arithmetic operation. Each LLM query yields a single complete solution comprising four sequential thinking steps.
[Line 827] Five in-context supervised learning demonstrations were included in the prompt to enforce correct response formatting from the LLM.
[Line 829] The task description s_task is composed of the CoT instruction concatenated with the five demonstrations (see Figure in Appendix).
[Line 833] Evaluation Procedure.
[Line 833] Solution verification was performed using SymPy, a symbolic mathematics library. Operands and operators were extracted from each response, the expression was reconstructed, and its evaluation was confirmed to equal 24. The mean success rate across the 100-problem test set is reported.
[Line 834] This rule-based success rate, denoted r_*, serves as the ground truth reward function referenced throughout the paper.
[Line 836] The symbol r designates the reward function available to the evaluated algorithms at runtime.
[Line 837] In this configuration, GPT-4.1 implements r -- the same model used as the policy LLM but with a distinct prompting scheme (detailed in the Appendix).
[Line 840] Following response generation by the policy LLM, GPT-4.1 assigns a score to each thinking step, quantifying the probability of reaching 24 from the remaining numbers on a 0-3 integer scale (0 = impossible, 3 = certain).
[Line 841] The experimental challenge is compounded by the constraint that no algorithm has direct access to r_*.
[Line 842] All algorithms must therefore depend on self-generated evaluation signals -- potentially subject to error -- produced by the same LLM to drive performance improvement.
[Line 864] Baseline Methods.
[Line 864] ICRL prompting is benchmarked against CoT-only prompting, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N sampling, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 864] CoT-only prompting provides the model with the task description s_task only, yielding a single step-by-step solution.
[Line 864] Long-CoT style prompting directs the LLM to generate an extended chain-of-thought, iterating within "<think>...</think>" delimiters upon incorrect intermediate solutions, prior to outputting the final answer.
[Line 864] While GPT-4.1 was not trained specifically for long-form CoT generation, Long-CoT prompting elicits substantially longer and self-correcting reasoning traces relative to zero-shot prompting, establishing it as a competitive baseline.
[Line 866] CoT-only and Long-CoT methods do not incorporate reward signals and execute in a single pass.
[Line 867] The Best-of-N baseline is strengthened by employing the ground truth reward function r_* for response selection.
[Line 869] Self-Refine does not utilize a reward function.
[Line 870] It instead prompts the LLM to produce textual verbal feedback on its own output.
[Line 871] Reflexion generates reflective feedback conditioned on the reward signal r.
[Line 872] ICRL prompting is differentiated from Self-Refine and Reflexion by its direct use of the scalar reward r, without verbal feedback.
[Line 874] Consequently, the comparison between ICRL prompting and Self-Refine / Reflexion represents a direct comparison of scalar versus verbal feedback mechanisms.
[Line 876] ICRL Prompting Implementation.
[Line 877] As established previously, both pi_theta and r in the Algorithm are implemented by GPT-4.1 under different prompt configurations.
[Line 878] The following section specifies the computation of S_0 at each episode.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] Here's the big idea: we came up with something called the ICRL prompting framework (there's an Algorithm and a Figure for it), and it's built from a few key pieces.
[Line 443] The LLM Is the Policy.
[Line 444] Basically, the LLM itself (we call it pi_theta) acts as the policy -- it's the thing making decisions.
[Line 445] The whole point is to get the LLM to solve some task just by prompting it.
[Line 446] We assume you can describe the task in plain language, and we call that description s_task (it lives in S).
[Line 447] Every time we start a new episode, we build the initial prompt by mashing together the LLM's past tries with their rewards, the task description, and some special meta-instructions we call s_ICRL.
[Line 450] We'll get into exactly how we put together those past attempts and which meta-instructions we pick in just a bit.
[Line 451] Once the LLM gets this prompt, it goes ahead and generates a response.
[Line 452] Then we save both the response and the rewards in a buffer so we can use them in later episodes.
[Line 461] The Reward Function.
[Line 461] After each state S_t in the episode, the LLM gets a number -- just a plain old scalar reward.
[Line 462] This reward can be sparse (meaning only the final reward R_T actually has a value) or dense (rewards throughout).
[Line 465] The reward can come from hard-coded rules, a separate trained model, or even the same LLM judging its own work.
[Line 466] Being able to use the LLM as its own judge is really cool because it means you can apply ICRL prompting to basically any task.
[Line 467] And here's the thing -- this number is literally the only feedback the LLM gets.
[Line 469] But we do let it know that the number is a reward.
[Line 470] How? We just write the word "Reward" right before the number in the prompt. Simple as that.
[Line 472] If the reward comes from rules or an external model, then it's genuinely external feedback.
[Line 475] But if the LLM is grading itself, then there's zero external feedback happening.
[Line 477] Even so, we still expect the LLM to get better over time.
[Line 478] Why? Because it's generally believed that judging something is easier than creating it.
[Line 479] That said, we think self-grading probably has a lower ceiling than getting feedback from the outside.
[Line 481] Memory for Past Experience.
[Line 481] We keep a buffer B that stores all the LLM's previous responses and rewards for the task.
[Line 482] Our bet is that pretrained LLMs already have this ICRL ability baked in.
[Line 483] To tap into that built-in ability, we stuff as many past attempts and rewards into the context as the window size allows.
[Line 485] The idea is that the LLM can actually do reinforcement learning from those past experiences right there in the context, during inference.
[Line 494] ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] To help the LLM do RL at inference time, we also throw in some extra instructions in the starting prompt S_0 for each episode.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 150] Reinforcement learning (RL) is a computational framework designed for sequential decision-making tasks, wherein an agent interacts with an environment and learns optimal behavior through reward-based feedback.
[Line 150] This report presents findings that RL-like behavior emerges during the inference phase of large language models (LLMs). This emergent capability is designated as in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL).
[Line 150] To characterize and exploit this phenomenon, a multi-round prompting framework, termed ICRL prompting, has been developed. The framework enables inference-time self-improvement without requiring any modification to model parameters.
[Line 150] The design objective of ICRL prompting is to guide LLMs to execute reinforcement learning processes during inference, facilitating autonomous performance improvement on specified tasks.
[Line 152] The operational procedure is as follows: upon generation of each response, the model is provided with a numerical scalar feedback value, designated as the reward.
[Line 152] For each subsequent interaction round, the LLM is re-prompted with an augmented context comprising the concatenation of all prior responses and their corresponding reward values.
[Line 152] Empirical observations consistently indicate that response quality exhibits improvement as the contextual history lengthens.
[Line 152] This behavior demonstrates that the LLM is capable of optimizing with respect to scalar reward signals during inference, displaying dynamics analogous to those observed in reinforcement learning systems.
[Line 153] Evaluation of ICRL prompting was conducted across multiple task domains: the Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and Olympiad-level mathematical problem-solving (AIME and HMMT). Results demonstrate significant performance improvements relative to baseline methods including Self-Refine and Reflexion.
[Line 153] A notable finding is that performance improvements persist even when the reward signals are generated by the same LLM, indicating the viability of ICRL prompting as a paradigm for test-time computation scaling.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based benchmark designed for evaluating agent performance across 30 science-experiment tasks. These tasks take place in a multi-room environment that contains a variety of objects relevant to each experiment.
[Line 999] Several factors contribute to the difficulty of this environment: first, rewards are sparse, meaning the agent receives feedback infrequently; second, the action space is large, presenting many possible choices at each step; and third, success requires both scientific knowledge and the ability to explore efficiently.
[Line 999] At each time step, the agent observes the outcome of its previous action and receives a reward of zero unless it has completed one of the predefined subgoals.
[Line 999] An important characteristic of this benchmark is that the reward signal used during evaluation is identical to the one used for inference-time self-improvement (i.e., r = r_*).
[Line 999] When all subgoals are completed, the agent accumulates a total reward of 100, and the episode is considered successful.
[Line 999] An episode is classified as a failure if the agent exceeds the maximum number of allowed steps or performs an incorrect terminating action.
[Line 1000] The task input s_task describes three key components: the environment layout, the specific task to be completed, and a template listing all available actions.
[Line 1000] A concrete example of s_task can be found in the Appendix.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] The reward function provided by the environment serves two roles: it is used to construct the trajectories placed in the context (r) and to evaluate model performance (r^*).
[Line 1002] Each of the 30 tasks is evaluated individually, and the results are then aggregated to produce overall performance metrics.
[Line 1002] The policy model used across all compared algorithms is GPT-4.1 mini.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] In the Reflexion method, the agent is prompted to reflect on its performance at the end of each episode.
[Line 1004] This reflection is sanitized and appended to a reflection buffer. The contents of this buffer are then formatted and included in the context for the next trial.
[Line 1004] Self-Refine follows a similar approach: it produces self-feedback, attaches it to a trajectory summary, and stores the combined information in the buffer.
[Line 1005] For the purpose of fair comparison, both Reflexion and Self-Refine are given access to the current episode's reward signals before generating their reflections, a privilege not available to ICRL.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] In this experimental configuration, each trial corresponds to one complete episode in the environment.
[Line 1007] Following each trial, a new trajectory is constructed by concatenating the sequence of actions, observations, rewards, and the final outcome (success or failure), and this trajectory is added to the experience buffer.
[Line 1007] Because each episode typically generates only a small number of non-zero rewards, only those rewards are included in the stored trajectory.
[Line 1007] Before each new trial begins, the initial prompt S_0 is assembled by concatenating the task description S_task, all previously collected trajectories, and the ICRL instruction S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] A worked example of S_0 is presented in the Appendix.
[Line 1009] The mean return plotted against the trial number is shown in the right panel of the Figure.
[Line 1010] Methods that maintain a history of previous interactions, similar to ICRL prompting, show consistent improvement over successive trials.
[Line 1011] Notably, ICRL prompting surpasses the baseline methods by approximately 20% when given a sufficient number of iterations.
[Line 1013] To ensure fairness when comparing against computationally efficient baselines such as Best-of-N, additional comparisons are provided in the Appendix where the test-time compute budget is varied. The results demonstrate that ICRL scales more favorably than baselines both in terms of the number of trials and the monetary cost of test-time computation.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] We herein introduce our principal contribution: the ICRL prompting framework (Algorithm, Figure), which comprises the following constituent components.
[Line 443] The Large Language Model as Policy.
[Line 444] A large language model, formally denoted as pi_theta, functions as the policy network within this framework.
[Line 445] The objective is to leverage prompting to enable the LLM to address a specified task.
[Line 446] We presuppose the availability of a natural language task description, which we formally represent as s_task within the state space S.
[Line 447] At the commencement of each episode, the initial prompt is constructed through the concatenation of the LLM's preceding attempts along with their associated reward signals, the task description, and supplementary meta-instructions designated as s_ICRL.
[Line 450] The precise methodology for concatenating prior attempts and the selection criteria for meta-instructions shall be elaborated upon in subsequent sections.
[Line 451] Upon receiving this initial prompt, the LLM produces its response.
[Line 452] Both the generated response and the corresponding rewards are preserved within the buffer for utilization in subsequent episodes.
[Line 461] The Reward Function.
[Line 461] A numerical scalar reward signal is furnished for each state S_t within the episode.
[Line 462] It is noteworthy that the reward may assume either a sparse form (wherein only R_T is nonzero) or a dense form.
[Line 465] The reward function may be implemented as rule-based, trained independently, or instantiated through the same LLM performing self-evaluation.
[Line 466] The versatility afforded by employing the LLM's self-evaluation as the reward function enables the ICRL prompting framework to be applied across a diverse array of tasks.
[Line 467] Importantly, this scalar reward constitutes the sole feedback mechanism provided to the LLM.
[Line 469] However, the LLM is explicitly informed that this scalar value represents a reward.
[Line 470] This is accomplished by prepending the lexical item "Reward" before the numerical value during the construction of the initial prompt.
[Line 472] It should be noted that when the reward function is rule-based or independently learned, the reward signal represents an external feedback mechanism.
[Line 475] Conversely, when the reward function consists solely of the LLM's own evaluative assessment, no external feedback whatsoever is present within the ICRL prompting framework.
[Line 477] Nevertheless, we anticipate improvement in the LLM's responses across episodes.
[Line 478] This expectation is grounded in the widely held hypothesis that evaluation constitutes a fundamentally easier task than generation.
[Line 479] We do, however, hypothesize that the performance ceiling achievable through self-evaluation is lower than that attainable via external feedback.
[Line 481] Memory for Experiential Data.
[Line 481] An experience buffer B is employed to retain the LLM's responses and associated rewards from preceding episodes for the given task.
[Line 482] Our foundational hypothesis posits that pretrained LLMs inherently possess ICRL capability.
[Line 483] To leverage this intrinsic ICRL capability for enhancing the LLM's task performance, we concatenate prior attempts and their rewards to the maximum extent permitted by the context window.
[Line 485] Our expectation is that the LLM can engage in reinforcement learning from the experiential data contained within the context during inference time.
[Line 494] ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] To facilitate the LLM's inference-time reinforcement learning process, supplementary instructions are incorporated into the initial prompt S_0 at the onset of each episode.
What are the main contributions of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
Can ICRL use self-evaluation as reward?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' supports two types of reward functions:

1. **Sparse rewards** (outcome reward model): r(s) is nonzero only at terminal states.
2. **Dense rewards** (progress reward model): r(s) can be nonzero for non-terminal states.

The reward function can be: rule-based, learned separately, or the same LLM for self-evaluation. With LLM self-evaluation there is no external feedback, yet performance still improves (evaluation is easier than generation). The authors hypothesize the ceiling with self-evaluation is lower than with external feedback.
What model is used in the Game of 24 experiments in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

GPT-4.1 is used as both the policy LLM and the reward model in the Game of 24 experiments in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'. GPT-4.1 was chosen because of its excellent long-context capacity. It scores each thinking step on a 0-3 scale for likelihood of reaching 24. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves 90% success rate.

Explain the experience buffer in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the experience buffer B stores the LLM's responses and rewards from previous episodes. The LLM reinforcement learns from these in-context experiences during inference.

How are LLMs modeled as RL agents?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', LLMs are modeled as RL agents: state = all generated tokens, action = next token. State space S = ∪_i V^i, action space A = V (token vocabulary). S_0 is the initial prompt with task description. S_t+1 = [S_t A_t] (concatenation). Reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1). Episode ends at max length or end-of-sequence token. Two reward types: sparse (outcome, nonzero only at terminal state) and dense (progress, nonzero at non-terminal states).

What is the key finding from the ICRL ablation study?

The key finding is that ICRL can genuinely generate novel responses that are better than ones during exploration. The exploration-only-without-reward method performs significantly worse, proving ICRL's gains are not just from Best-of-N selection. Rewards and context length both matter.
What kind of feedback does Self-Refine use?

Self-Refine uses textual verbal feedback — it asks the LLM to provide natural language feedback on its own response, then revise. It does NOT use a reward function. This contrasts with ICRL which uses only scalar reward signals.

[Line 1180] Conclusion.
[Line 1181] Think of it this way: LLMs have secretly learned how to do reinforcement learning all on their own, just while generating text — no extra training needed.
[Line 1181] The researchers built a really simple system called ICRL that just gives the model a score (like a grade) after each attempt, and this simple trick works on lots of different AI models and tasks, doing better than fancier methods where the model tries to fix its own mistakes.

Provide a detailed description of the paper 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.

Why do Self-Refine and Reflexion suffer from performance collapse?

Self-Refine and Reflexion are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations. Since their verbal self-revision quality depends on the model's parametric knowledge of the task, incorrect feedback compounds over rounds. ICRL avoids this by using only scalar rewards without prescribing new instructions.
ICRL achieves what percentage on Game of 24?
ICRL Preset achieves 90% success rate on Game of 24 after 50 trials. This is versus 49% for Best-of-N, 47% for Self-Refine, and 44% for Reflexion.
What is the fundamental difference between ICRL and verbal feedback methods?

The fundamental difference is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. ICRL uses only numerical scalar rewards — no textual feedback, no instructions on how to improve. Self-Refine and Reflexion use verbal/textual feedback. Verbal feedback methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, while scalar rewards provide a clean learning signal.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' tests five ablations on Game of 24:

What is the conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

The conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners': RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. The minimal, scalar-reward-based ICRL prompting framework unlocks this ability across diverse models and tasks, outperforming self-revision methods. A key future direction is investigating how training-time interventions might further enhance this in-context RL capability. This points toward more autonomous agents that can explore, adapt, and self-improve in open-ended settings by learning from their own experience.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 596] Making LLMs Better at Test Time.
[Line 603] Most current approaches use natural language feedback to help models fix their own answers -- think Self-Refine, Reflexion, and Textual Gradient.
[Line 603] The problem is, these methods only work as well as the model's built-in knowledge about the task, so they tend to produce made-up feedback that snowballs over rounds and eventually tanks performance.
[Line 603] Basically, they're doing a kind of language-guided search where the feedback just tells the model what to do next.
[Line 605] ICRL takes a different route -- it only needs a number (the reward), no verbal instructions at all.
[Line 605] The model has to figure out how to do better by spotting patterns in what it tried before and what scores those attempts got, which is pretty much how reinforcement learning works.
[Line 605] What's cool is that these rewards can come straight from the real environment, giving you solid ground-truth signals.
[Line 607] There's also a bunch of work on making LLMs smarter at test time using search strategies like Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT), Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), and Intelligent Go-Explore.
[Line 607] But those methods need a lot of external engineering -- custom heuristics, memory systems, that sort of thing -- instead of tapping into what the model can already do on its own.
[Line 607] This work also connects to prompt optimization research, where you use numerical scores to tweak prompts, but that typically works by picking the top-k results and filtering out errors.
[Line 608] So that's really more like in-context supervised learning (like filtered behavior cloning) than actual RL.
[Line 608] ICRL is different because it can actually learn from its mistakes and failures.

ICRL achieves what percentage on Game of 24?
ICRL Preset achieves 90% success rate on Game of 24 after 50 trials. This is versus 49% for Best-of-N, 47% for Self-Refine, and 44% for Reflexion.
Explain the ICRL prompting algorithm step by step.

The ICRL prompting algorithm from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners':
1. Start with task description s_task and empty experience buffer B.
2. Each episode K: construct S_0 = [s_task + buffer B contents + ICRL instruction s_ICRL].
3. LLM generates response. Store response and rewards in B.
4. Reward tagged with 'Reward: ' before the scalar number.
5. Repeat with updated buffer.
6. Two strategies for s_ICRL: Preset (alternate explore/exploit) or Autonomous (LLM decides).
Key principle: minimality — only scalar rewards, no verbal feedback.
Give me a comprehensive summary of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.
Summarize the key contributions of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
What are the ScienceWorld results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ScienceWorld is an interactive, text-based benchmark with 30 science-experiment tasks in a multi-room environment. Rewards are sparse. GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy. ICRL prompting outperforms baseline methods by about 20% after enough iterations. ICRL also scales better than baselines in terms of test-time compute budget (in dollar amounts).

[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] - RL formalizes tasks as Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) - An MDP consists of: state space S, action space A, reward function r: S -> R, initial distribution p_0 in (S) where (S) = probability distributions over S, and transition function p: S x A -> (S)
[Line 340] - At t=0: sample initial state S_0 from p_0
[Line 342] - At time t: agent in state S_t picks action A_t via policy pi: S -> (A), where (A) = probability distributions over A - Action selection: A_t ~ pi(S_t)
[Line 344] - After executing A_t: agent moves to S_t+1 ~ p(S_t, A_t) and gets reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1)
[Line 346] - The agent-environment loop runs until terminal time T, ending the episode
[Line 348] - Objective: adapt policy pi to maximize expected total reward J(pi) = E[sum_t=1^T R_t]
[Line 349] - Deep RL: policy pi parameterized by a neural network
[Line 351] - Network parameters denoted theta; policy written as pi_theta
[Line 352] - RL algorithms work by updating theta to improve the policy
[Line 353] - Example: at time t, sample A_t from pi_theta_t(S_t)
[Line 355] - Then update theta_t to theta_t+1 using trajectory data: S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1
[Line 356] - At t+1: sample A_t+1 from updated policy pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1)
[Line 360] - Bottom line: standard RL = iterative updates to theta_t
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] - ICRL (term from prior work): RL during inference (forward pass), with NO parameter updates - An emerging inference-time compute paradigm
[Line 368] - ICRL policy: pi_theta conditioned on context C_t - Actions: A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t)
[Line 369] - Context construction is an active research area - One approach: include all prior state-action-reward tuples from the task
[Line 370] - Context typically spans ALL previous episodes, not just the current one
[Line 371] - Pretraining stage: train theta across diverse MDPs
[Line 373] - Pretrained parameters: theta_*
[Line 374] - After pretraining: evaluate pi_theta_* on new tasks
[Line 376] - In new MDP: A_t ~ pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t)
[Line 378] - Critical: theta_* stays FIXED during evaluation
[Line 379] - Despite fixed parameters, action quality improves as C_t grows
[Line 381] - Since theta_* is constant, improvement must stem from growing context
[Line 383] - This is called "in-context policy improvement"
[Line 384] - Observed even on out-of-distribution tasks, e.g., bandits with opposite optimal arms vs. pretraining - Demonstrates generalization beyond training data

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] In my understanding, RL frames tasks using Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). An MDP has five key elements: a state space S, an action space A, a reward function r: S -> R, an initial state distribution p_0 in (S) (where (S) is the set of probability distributions over S), and a transition function p: S x A -> (S).
[Line 340] The process starts at time step 0 by sampling an initial state S_0 from p_0.
[Line 342] At each time step t, I think of it as the agent being in state S_t and picking an action A_t based on its policy pi: S -> (A), where (A) represents probability distributions over actions. So A_t is drawn from pi(S_t).
[Line 344] Once action A_t is taken, the agent moves to a new state S_t+1 sampled from p(S_t, A_t), and it receives a reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1).
[Line 346] This interaction between agent and environment goes on until a terminal time step T, which I see as the end of the episode.
[Line 348] From what I gather, the agent's goal is to fine-tune its policy pi to maximize the expected total reward J(pi) = E[sum_t=1^T R_t].
[Line 349] In deep RL, the policy pi is implemented as a neural network, which I find to be one of the most practical aspects of modern RL.
[Line 351] I'll use theta to refer to the network parameters, so the policy becomes pi_theta.
[Line 352] The way I see it, RL algorithms learn by updating theta to improve the policy step by step.
[Line 353] For example, at time t, the action A_t is sampled from pi_theta_t(S_t).
[Line 355] Then the algorithm updates theta_t to theta_t+1 using the trajectory data it has collected: S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1.
[Line 356] At the next step t+1, action A_t+1 is sampled from the updated policy pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1).
[Line 360] So in my view, the whole standard RL process really comes down to how theta_t evolves over time.
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] What I find fascinating about ICRL (a term first introduced in earlier research) is that it's a paradigm where RL happens during inference -- specifically during the network's forward pass -- with absolutely no parameter updates.
[Line 368] In ICRL, the policy pi_theta also takes a context C_t as input, giving us A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t).
[Line 369] How to construct C_t is something researchers are still actively working on, but one approach I've seen is to include all previous state-action-reward tuples from the task.
[Line 370] I should note that this context generally includes data from all previous episodes, not just the current one.
[Line 371] There's a pretraining phase where the network parameters theta are trained across many different MDPs.
[Line 373] I'll refer to the pretrained parameters as theta_*.
[Line 374] After pretraining, the policy pi_theta_* is tested on new tasks.
[Line 376] In a new MDP, actions are sampled from pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t).
[Line 378] What strikes me most is that theta_* stays completely fixed during this evaluation.
[Line 379] Yet the quality of the actions A_t gets better as the context C_t accumulates more experience in the new task.
[Line 381] Since theta_* doesn't change, I can only conclude that the improvement comes from the expanding context.
[Line 383] This is what researchers call in-context policy improvement.
[Line 384] What I find particularly remarkable is that this improvement occurs even when the new task is completely outside the pretraining distribution -- for instance, in bandit problems where the optimal arms are reversed compared to the pretraining bandits.
[Line 388] This observation, in my assessment, definitively rules out the idea that theta_* has simply memorized the pretraining tasks.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 915] Creative Writing.
[Line 919] Task Setup.
[Line 919] For this experiment, we look at the creative writing task from earlier work. The idea is pretty simple: you take four random sentences from a pool and hand them to the LLM.
[Line 919] The LLM has to write four paragraphs, where each one ends with one of those given sentences, and the whole thing has to make sense together.
[Line 919] It is actually a pretty tough task because the model needs to come up with one smooth storyline that naturally leads into each of these four random sentences -- basically stitching them into a single coherent narrative.
[Line 919] We test this on 100 different problems.
[Line 919] You can find an example of the task prompt s_task in a Figure in the Appendix.
[Line 935] Evaluation.
[Line 936] To judge the outputs, we used Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2, which is a popular stand-in for human evaluation and correlates really well with human judgments (up to 0.98 Pearson correlation).
[Line 936] For all 100 problems, we picked each method's best shot: for Reflexion and Best-of-N, that means the highest-reward response out of 50 tries; for ICRL and Self-Refine, it is whatever they produced on the 50th round.
[Line 936] Alpaca-Eval then figures out pairwise win rates, which we call r_*.
[Line 938] Now let us talk about the reward function r that the methods actually get to use.
[Line 938] We go with a standard pairwise comparison setup -- GPT-4.1 scores each response from 1 to 10 against a coherent reference paragraph (check the Figure for details).
[Line 938] Here is an important thing: even though both r and r_* use an LLM as a judge, they are doing different things. r checks responses against a fixed reference and focuses on coherence.
[Line 938] r_*, on the other hand, does head-to-head comparisons between our method's output and a baseline's output.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] We stack up our method against Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 956] Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting all get to use the reward r.
[Line 957] Self-Refine skips the reward and just has GPT-4.1 give text-based feedback instead.
[Line 958] Since CoT and Long-CoT style prompting are hard to tell apart for creative writing, we throw in Long-CoT style prompting as a baseline too.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] GPT-4.1 plays double duty here -- it is both the policy LLM pi_theta and the reward model r in the Algorithm.
[Line 959] Each episode, the starting prompt S_0 gets built by stringing together all previous outputs along with their coherence scores from r.
[Line 960] Worth noting: this reward is sparse, meaning only R_T (the final one) can be nonzero.
[Line 961] So we only put R_T into S_0.
[Line 963] The results are pretty great: our method gets a length-controlled win rate of 59.48% over Reflexion, 78.36% over Long-CoT style prompting, 86.32% over Self-Refine, and a whopping 93.81% over Best-of-N, as shown in the Table.
[Line 963] This means our method's responses are better at following the instructions for writing coherent paragraphs and are more aligned with what humans actually prefer.
[Line 964] The return curve from the reward model r is in the Figure, and a running max version is in the Appendix Figure.
[Line 964] While Self-Refine starts off matching ICRL on coherence reward, if you let both run for 50 more episodes, our method keeps getting better while Self-Refine hits a wall and then actually gets worse -- probably because its context just gets way too long.
Does ICRL learn from rewards or just parametric knowledge?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. Given only the title, the model generates the abstract. ROUGE-recall is used as both reward and metric. ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall over 200 iterations, vs 0.44 Best-of-1024, 0.45 Self-Refine, 0.46 Reflexion. This demonstrates ICRL learns from external rewards and is not limited by pre-training knowledge.

Why do Self-Refine and Reflexion suffer from performance collapse?

Self-Refine and Reflexion are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations. Since their verbal self-revision quality depends on the model's parametric knowledge of the task, incorrect feedback compounds over rounds. ICRL avoids this by using only scalar rewards without prescribing new instructions.
What is the 'reward is enough' hypothesis?

The 'reward is enough' hypothesis (referenced in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners') states: 'intelligence, and its associated abilities, can be understood as subserving the maximisation of reward.' The related reward hypothesis states: 'all of what we mean by goals and purposes can be well thought of as maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (reward).' ICRL prompting complies with both by using only scalar rewards.

What are the two ICRL instruction strategies?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).

[Line 1721] Q: What additional analysis was performed? A: The authors conducted further analysis to investigate key properties of ICRL.
[Line 1723] Q: What was the unseen paper abstract generation experiment? A: It was a task designed to test whether ICRL learns from external rewards or merely retrieves memorized knowledge.
[Line 1725] Q: Why was this experiment needed? A: To isolate whether ICRL genuinely learns from external reward signals rather than merely searching or selecting from the model's parametric knowledge. A task was chosen where parametric knowledge alone was expected to be insufficient.
[Line 1728] Q: What data was used? A: 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff were collected.
[Line 1728] Q: What was the model given as input? A: Only the title of each paper. The model had to generate the abstract from the title alone.
[Line 1728] Q: What was the goal? A: To recover the unseen expert-written abstract as closely as possible.
[Line 1728] Q: How was performance measured? A: Using ROUGE-recall between the generated abstract and the ground-truth abstract. This same score also served as the external reward signal.
[Line 1728] Q: Why does this task require reward-based learning? A: Because the abstracts lie outside the model's pre-training distribution, so success demands learning from external rewards rather than searching through memorized content.
[Line 1732] Q: How did Best-of-1024 sampling perform? A: It reached only 0.44 ROUGE-recall, showing that direct search over the model's base distribution cannot recover the missing content.
[Line 1733] Q: How did Self-Refine perform? A: It plateaued within a few rounds at 0.45 because it does not use external reward.
[Line 1733] Q: How did Reflexion compare? A: Reflexion scored slightly higher at 0.46 but largely mirrored Self-Refine, suggesting its revisions were dominated by the model's self-verbal feedback, which carried little useful information in this setup.
[Line 1734] Q: How did ICRL perform? A: ICRL continued improving over 200 iterations and achieved 0.59 ROUGE-recall, demonstrating that it effectively learns from external reward signals and is not limited by the model's pre-training knowledge.

[Line 511] Related Works.

[Line 514] Research on reinforcement learning algorithms that operate during inference time has a considerable history, and the subsequent introduction of the term in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) catalyzed substantial expansion of this research area.
[Line 514] A comprehensive survey of this field is available in the literature.
[Line 514] The predominant body of existing ICRL research, situated within the broader domain of meta-reinforcement learning, has been conducted using small-scale models trained de novo in gaming and robotic environments.
[Line 514] A subset of studies have utilized pretrained large language models, for instance as environment simulators or within multi-armed bandit formulations; however, these approaches frequently necessitate artificial modifications and large language models have not demonstrated competitive performance relative to algorithmic baselines in such settings.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] Reinforcement learning (RL) is a machine learning framework that models sequential decision-making tasks using Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). An MDP is formally specified by a state space S, an action space A, a reward function r: S -> R, an initial state distribution p_0 drawn from (S) (the set of probability distributions over S), and a transition function p: S x A -> (S).
[Line 340] At time step 0, the agent begins in an initial state S_0, which is sampled from the distribution p_0.
[Line 342] At each time step t, the agent, currently in state S_t, chooses an action A_t according to a policy pi: S -> (A), where (A) is the set of probability distributions over the action space A. The action A_t is sampled from pi(S_t).
[Line 344] Once the action A_t is executed, the agent transitions to a new state S_t+1 ~ p(S_t, A_t) and receives a reward R_t+1 = r(S_t+1).
[Line 346] This interaction between agent and environment repeats until a terminal time step T is reached, which defines the end of an episode.
[Line 348] The agent seeks to adapt its policy pi to maximize the expected total reward, expressed as J(pi) = E[sum_t=1^T R_t].
[Line 349] In deep reinforcement learning, the policy pi is typically parameterized by a neural network.
[Line 351] The network's parameters are represented by theta, and the policy is accordingly written as pi_theta.
[Line 352] RL algorithms operate by updating the parameters theta in order to improve the policy.
[Line 353] As an example, at time step t, the action A_t is drawn from pi_theta_t(S_t).
[Line 355] The RL algorithm then updates the parameters from theta_t to theta_t+1, using the trajectory information available up to that point: S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1.
[Line 356] At time step t+1, the action A_t+1 is sampled from the newly updated policy pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1).
[Line 360] The standard RL process can thus be understood as the progressive updating of the parameter vector theta_t.
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) is a term first coined in prior research to describe an emerging paradigm in which the RL process takes place during the inference time (the forward pass) of a neural network, without any updates to the network's parameters.
[Line 368] In ICRL, the policy pi_theta is conditioned on an additional context variable C_t, such that actions are selected as A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t).
[Line 369] How to construct the context C_t is an active area of research. One common approach is to include all previous state-action-reward tuples collected during the task.
[Line 370] This context typically encompasses state-action-reward pairs from all prior episodes, not just the current episode.
[Line 371] In ICRL, the network undergoes a pretraining stage in which the parameters theta are trained on a broad distribution of tasks (MDPs).
[Line 373] The pretrained parameters are denoted theta_*.
[Line 374] After pretraining, the policy pi_theta_* is evaluated on new, unseen tasks.
[Line 376] In a new MDP, actions A_t are sampled from pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t).
[Line 378] A key property of ICRL is that theta_* remains fixed during evaluation.
[Line 379] Despite this, the quality of actions A_t improves as the context C_t grows over the course of the new task.
[Line 381] Since theta_* does not change, this improvement can only be attributed to the increasing context.
[Line 383] This effect is known as in-context policy improvement.
[Line 384] In-context policy improvement has been observed even when the new task is drawn from outside the pretraining distribution. For example, in-context policy improvement has been demonstrated in bandit problems where the optimal arms are opposite to those in the pretraining bandits.
[Line 388] This finding rules out the explanation that theta_* has memorized the pretraining tasks.

[Line 514] Q: Where can one find a broader overview? A: Existing survey papers provide a comprehensive look at the ICRL literature.

[Line 514] Q: Have pretrained LLMs been used in ICRL? A: Yes, some studies have employed pretrained LLMs, for instance as simulators or in bandit problems. However, these setups frequently require artificial interventions, and the LLMs have generally been unable to match the performance of dedicated algorithmic baselines.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 150] So, reinforcement learning (RL) is basically a way to tackle problems where you have to make a series of decisions one after another.
[Line 150] Here's the cool part: this paper shows that RL actually happens on its own when large language models (LLMs) are just running inference. They call this in-context RL, or ICRL for short.
[Line 150] To show off this ability, they came up with a pretty simple setup where you prompt the model multiple rounds in a row. They call it ICRL prompting, and it lets the model get better at stuff while it's running, no extra training needed.
[Line 150] The whole point of ICRL prompting is to get LLMs to basically do reinforcement learning on the fly during inference, so they can improve themselves on whatever task you throw at them.
[Line 152] After the model gives a response, you hand it a number, like a score, which is the reward.
[Line 152] Then in the next round, you prompt the model again, but this time you include all the previous responses along with the scores they got, all packed into the context.
[Line 152] What they keep seeing is that the responses get better and better as you pile up more context with past attempts and rewards.
[Line 152] Basically, the LLM is learning to chase higher reward scores while it's running, which is pretty much what RL does.
[Line 153] They tested ICRL prompting on a bunch of different tasks: the Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and some really hard math competitions (AIME and HMMT). It beat out other methods like Self-Refine and Reflexion by a solid margin.
[Line 153] What's really interesting is that even when the LLM is generating its own reward scores, ICRL prompting still makes things better. That's a big deal because it opens up a whole new way to scale performance at test time.

[Line 919] In my reading of this paper, I found the creative writing experiment particularly interesting. The task comes from prior work: four sentences get randomly pulled from a sentence pool.
[Line 919] The LLM then has to write four paragraphs, each ending with one of these sentences, and the whole output needs to be a coherent passage.
[Line 919] I think this is genuinely difficult because the model has to construct a single narrative that logically justifies each of these four random sentences by weaving them together into one story.

[Line 936] For the 100 problems, they took each method's best response: for Reflexion and Best-of-N, the highest-reward output from 50 trials; for ICRL and Self-Refine, the 50th episode output.

[Line 938] I want to highlight the reward function r that the algorithms actually use: GPT-4.1 scores each response from 1 to 10 against a coherent reference paragraph, following standard pairwise comparison.
[Line 938] The scoring details are in the Figure.
[Line 938] Something I found important is that r and r_* both use an LLM as judge but measure different things. r compares against a fixed reference with a focus on coherence, while r_* does pairwise comparison between the proposed method and baselines.
[Line 938] This distinction struck me as critical for understanding the results.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] They compared against Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 956] Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting all have access to r.

[Line 959] ICRL prompting.

What is the key design principle of ICRL?

The key design principle of ICRL prompting in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is **minimality**. To ensure gains come from the LLM's emergent RL capacity (not auxiliary mechanisms), they exclude: textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, and engineered modules. The only supervision is the scalar reward. This complies with the reward hypothesis ('goals can be thought of as maximization of cumulative scalar reward') and the 'reward is enough' hypothesis ('intelligence can be understood as subserving maximisation of reward').

[Line 919] Imagine you are given four random sentences and told to write a story that uses all of them as paragraph endings. That is basically this task. The sentences come from a pool, and four are picked at random.

[Line 919] They tested this with 100 different sets of sentences.

[Line 936] To grade the outputs, they used something called Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2. Think of it as a really good automated grader that agrees with human graders almost perfectly (up to 0.98 Pearson correlation).
[Line 936] For each of the 100 problems, they picked each method's best work: for some methods, the best out of 50 tries; for others, whatever they produced on their 50th attempt.
[Line 936] The grader then figured out which method "wins" more often in head-to-head matchups. They call this r_*.
[Line 938] Now, the algorithms also need a way to know how well they are doing during training. For that, GPT-4.1 reads each response and gives it a score from 1 to 10 based on how good it is compared to a reference paragraph.
[Line 938] The details of this scoring are in the paper's figures.
[Line 938] Here is the key thing: the training score (r) and the final evaluation score (r_*) are different. r asks "how does this compare to a reference?" while r_* asks "which of two methods wrote a better story?"
[Line 938] They look similar but measure different things.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] They compared their method against three others: Best-of-N (just try many times and pick the best), Reflexion (reflect on mistakes), and Self-Refine (get text feedback and revise).
[Line 956] Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL all get to see the numerical scores.
[Line 957] Self-Refine does not get scores -- instead, it gets written feedback from the AI about what to improve.
[Line 958] They also tested Long-CoT style prompting, which is like asking the model to think for a really long time before answering.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] The same AI model (GPT-4.1) does double duty -- it both writes the stories and scores them.
[Line 959] Each round, the model gets to see all its previous stories along with the scores they received.

How does ICRL perform on Game of 24 in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Provide a detailed description of the paper 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.
Who wrote the paper 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' and where was it published?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
What success rate does ICRL achieve on Game of 24?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 158] Introduction.
[Line 164] Here's the thing about LLMs: if we want them to be truly useful agents that can tackle new tasks, they need to get better while they're actually being used. This idea is called test-time scaling, and it's a pretty hot topic in AI right now.
[Line 164] There are really just two big approaches to making models better with more compute: learning and search. And both have proven they can reach superhuman levels -- just look at Chess and Go.
[Line 164] On the search side, there's been a ton of progress in getting LLMs to improve themselves at test time. We've gone from the simple Best-of-N approach to more sophisticated methods like Tree of Thoughts and Monte Carlo Tree Search.
[Line 166] But here's what's interesting -- learning hasn't gotten the same spotlight for inference-time self-improvement.
[Line 166] Traditional in-context learning (ICL) is essentially supervised learning. You need to show the model expert examples with correct answers.
[Line 166] The catch? You can't easily generate more expert examples on the fly during inference. That puts a hard ceiling on how useful ICL can be for test-time scaling.
[Line 166] So what's the alternative? LLMs need to learn from their own outputs -- essentially teaching themselves through trial and error.
[Line 169] Enter reinforcement learning. RL is probably the gold standard when it comes to algorithms that can improve without human hand-holding.
[Line 169] The problem is, RL has mainly shown its magic in simulated environments or during the training phase of LLMs -- not when the model is actually being used.
[Line 169] And that's a problem because the real world (what researchers call the "big world" setting) is enormously more complex than any simulation or training scenario.
[Line 169] Real-world agents are constantly running into situations they were never trained for and need to figure things out in real time.
[Line 169] What we really need are models that can do two things: (1) handle the messy diversity of real-world tasks where natural language is often how you interact, and (2) keep getting better during inference without needing to be retrained every time something new comes up.
[Line 177] Which brings us to the million-dollar question: can reinforcement learning just... emerge inside an LLM while it's generating text?
[Line 177] If that's possible, it would be a beautiful solution. The LLM gives you a solid starting point (a general-purpose policy), and the emergent RL handles the continual improvement part.
[Line 177] We already know LLMs can do surprising things with in-context supervised learning, and that discovery has sparked a wave of research into in-context RL (ICRL).
[Line 177] But so far, ICRL research has stayed in the shallow end -- mostly bandit problems and simulated environments. Nobody has shown it working on real, open-ended tasks where you need language.
[Line 183] That's exactly what this paper does. The authors show that LLMs genuinely can act as in-context reinforcement learners, and this emergent ability helps them crush it on diverse language tasks -- from running science experiments to writing creatively to solving olympiad-level math.
[Line 183] Their method? A refreshingly simple multi-round prompting framework they call ICRL prompting.
[Line 184] The whole idea behind ICRL prompting is to nudge LLMs into doing RL-style self-improvement on whatever task you give them.
[Line 185] You start with just the task description. Nothing fancy.
[Line 187] After the model gives you an answer, you rate it with a number -- that's your reward signal.
[Line 190] In the next round, you feed the model the same task plus all its previous attempts and their scores.
[Line 192] Then you just keep going.
[Line 193] And here's the cool part: the answers genuinely get better as the context fills up with more experience.
[Line 194] The LLM is effectively maximizing that reward score in real time -- doing RL without anyone explicitly programming it to.
[Line 199] One thing that makes ICRL prompting special is how stripped-down it is. The authors call minimality a core design principle.
[Line 199] They intentionally left out all the usual tricks -- no textual gradients, no experience replay, no sampling heuristics, no extra modules. Why? Because they wanted to prove the LLM itself has an innate RL ability, not that some clever engineering hack does.
[Line 199] The only signal the model gets? A single number. The reward.
[Line 199] This philosophy aligns perfectly with two famous hypotheses: the reward hypothesis (all goals reduce to maximizing cumulative scalar reward) and the "reward is enough" hypothesis (intelligence itself is fundamentally about reward maximization).
Explain the ICRL prompting algorithm step by step.

The ICRL prompting algorithm from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners':
1. Start with task description s_task and empty experience buffer B.
2. Each episode K: construct S_0 = [s_task + buffer B contents + ICRL instruction s_ICRL].
3. LLM generates response. Store response and rewards in B.
4. Reward tagged with 'Reward: ' before the scalar number.
5. Repeat with updated buffer.
6. Two strategies for s_ICRL: Preset (alternate explore/exploit) or Autonomous (LLM decides).
Key principle: minimality — only scalar rewards, no verbal feedback.

Who wrote 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 800] The Game of 24 Benchmark.
[Line 824] Experimental Configuration.
[Line 824] The task requires that, given a set of four integers, the model employ each integer precisely once, combining them through the four elementary arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division) in an arbitrary sequence so as to yield the target value of 24.
[Line 825] GPT-4.1 was selected as the model for this experimental evaluation, owing to its superior long-context processing capabilities; the model was accessed via API.
[Line 826] In accordance with prior work, chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting was employed to elicit step-by-step solutions from the model, wherein at each step the model selects two numbers from the remaining set along with an arithmetic operation, thereby producing a single complete solution per query comprising four reasoning steps in total.
[Line 827] To ensure that the LLM produces outputs adhering to the requisite format, five in-context supervised learning demonstrations were additionally included in the prompt.
[Line 829] The CoT instruction together with the five demonstrations constitute the task description s_task (see Figure in Appendix).
[Line 833] Evaluation Protocol.
[Line 833] Solution correctness was verified using SymPy, a symbolic mathematics library, by extracting the operands and operators from each response, reconstructing the mathematical expression, and confirming that its evaluation equals 24; the mean success rate across the 100 problems is reported.
[Line 834] This rule-based success metric is hereafter denoted r_* and corresponds to the ground truth reward function.
[Line 836] The notation r is reserved for the reward function to which algorithms are granted access during execution.
[Line 837] Specifically, GPT-4.1 serves as r, i.e., the identical LLM used as the policy model albeit under a distinct prompt configuration (see Figure in Appendix).
[Line 840] Upon generation of a response by the policy LLM, GPT-4.1 evaluates each thinking step by scoring the likelihood of attaining the target value of 24 given the remaining numbers on a discrete scale from 0 to 3 (where 0 indicates impossibility and 3 indicates certainty).
[Line 841] The inherent difficulty of this task stems from the fact that no algorithm is afforded access to r_*.
[Line 842] Consequently, all algorithms must rely upon their own potentially imperfect self-evaluation, produced by the same LLM, as the basis for response improvement.
[Line 864] Baseline Methods.
[Line 864] The proposed method is compared against CoT-only prompting, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N sampling, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 864] Under CoT-only prompting, the model receives solely the task description s_task and generates a single step-by-step solution.
[Line 864] Under Long-CoT style prompting, the LLM is explicitly instructed to produce an extended chain-of-thought, iteratively retrying within "<think>...</think>" tags upon encountering an incorrect solution, before ultimately providing its answer.
[Line 864] Despite GPT-4.1 not having been specifically trained for extended chain-of-thought reasoning, Long-CoT style prompting was observed to elicit substantially longer and self-correcting reasoning traces relative to zero-shot prompting, thereby constituting a strong baseline for this task.
[Line 866] Neither of these two methods is capable of utilizing a reward signal, and each is executed in a single pass.
[Line 867] For Best-of-N sampling, the ground truth reward r_* was used to select the optimal response, thereby strengthening this baseline.
[Line 869] Self-Refine operates without recourse to a reward function.
[Line 870] Instead, it solicits textual verbal feedback from the LLM itself.
[Line 871] Reflexion produces reflective commentary conditioned on the reward r.
[Line 872] ICRL prompting is distinguished from both Self-Refine and Reflexion by its direct utilization of the scalar reward r, entirely forgoing verbal feedback.
[Line 874] Thus, the comparison between ICRL prompting and Self-Refine/Reflexion fundamentally constitutes a comparison between scalar feedback and verbal feedback.
[Line 876] ICRL Prompting Configuration.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 304] So to wrap things up, the paper has three main contributions: (1): We came up with the ICRL prompting framework, which is basically a really simple setup that gets LLMs to improve themselves on the fly at inference time, and all you need are plain scalar rewards.
[Line 306] Think of it like how in-context learning works with (x, y) example pairs in the prompt – except here, we’re putting state–action–reward tuples along with some straightforward meta-instructions into the context instead.
[Line 306] The cool thing about this design is that it lets us see what the LLM can do on its own when it comes to ICRL, without relying on any outside code or fancy engineered tricks. (2): We show pretty strong evidence that RL-like behavior actually pops up naturally during inference when you use the ICRL prompting setup.
[Line 314] More concretely, we show that the model maximizes the scalar reward it gets, balances exploration and exploitation during inference, gets better as the context grows longer, does worse when the context is short, and also does worse when you take the reward away entirely.
[Line 319] All of these behaviors are exactly what you’d expect to see from an RL algorithm doing its thing.
[Line 320] Basically, it’s a "duck test" – if it walks like RL and quacks like RL, it probably is RL.
[Line 322] We also show that ICRL prompting beats self-revision approaches like Self-Refine and Reflexion by a good margin, across a bunch of different benchmarks: Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and even Olympiad-level math problems (AIME and HMMT).
[Line 322] What’s especially interesting is that for Game of 24 and creative writing, the LLM generates its own rewards, and it still manages to consistently improve its performance.
