[Line 915] Creative Writing.
[Line 919] Task Setup.
[Line 919] Now, let me walk you through one of our most exciting experiments -- the creative writing task. This comes from previous work in the field. We take four sentences, randomly sampled from a pool, and hand them to the model.
[Line 919] The model's job is to write four paragraphs, each ending with one of those sentences, and the whole passage has to be coherent.
[Line 919] And let me tell you, this is hard. The model needs to invent a single storyline that naturally incorporates all four random sentences into one coherent narrative.
[Line 919] We ran this on 100 problems.
[Line 919] You can see an example of the task prompt in the Appendix.
[Line 935] Evaluation.
[Line 936] For evaluation, we used Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2 -- many of you are probably familiar with it. It is one of the best proxies we have for human evaluation, with up to 0.98 Pearson correlation with human judgments.
[Line 936] We took each method's best response out of 100 problems: for Reflexion and Best-of-N, the highest-reward output from 50 trials; for ICRL and Self-Refine, the output from episode 50.
[Line 936] Alpaca-Eval computes pairwise win rates, which we call r_*.
[Line 938] Now, what reward function do the algorithms actually see? We use a standard pairwise comparison where GPT-4.1 scores each response from 1 to 10 against a coherent reference paragraph.
[Line 938] Details are in the Figure.
[Line 938] And I want to emphasize this important point: r and r_* both use an LLM judge, but they are measuring different things. r looks at how a response compares to a fixed reference text, focusing on coherence. r_* does direct head-to-head comparison between our method and baselines.
[Line 938] These are fundamentally different evaluations.
[Line 955] Baselines.
[Line 955] We compared against Best-of-N, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 956] Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting all get to use the reward r.
[Line 957] Self-Refine does not use r -- it uses verbal feedback from GPT-4.1 instead.
[Line 958] We also included Long-CoT style prompting as a baseline because, frankly, it is hard to tell it apart from regular CoT for creative writing.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] For this experiment, GPT-4.1 is both the policy model and the reward model -- it does everything.
[Line 959] Each episode, we build S_0 by concatenating all prior outputs with their coherence scores.
[Line 960] The reward is sparse here -- only the final reward R_T is nonzero.
[Line 961] So we just include R_T in S_0.
[Line 963] And here is the punchline: we achieve win rates of 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N. Those are strong numbers.
[Line 963] Our method produces better coherent paragraphs and is preferred by humans across the board.
[Line 964] The reward curves are in the paper, with the running max in the Appendix.
[Line 964] And here is something really interesting that I want to leave you with: Self-Refine starts out matching us on coherence reward. But when you run both methods for 50 more episodes, we keep improving while Self-Refine plateaus and then actually gets worse. We believe this is because its context simply grows too large.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 331] Background.
[Line 333] Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 334] Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a principled framework for sequential decision-making, formalized through Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). A comprehensive body of literature has established the MDP as the standard formulation, consisting of a state space S, an action space A, a reward function r: S -> R, an initial state distribution p_0 belonging to (S) (the set of probability distributions over S), and a transition function p: S x A -> (S).
[Line 340] The decision process initiates at time step 0 with the sampling of an initial state S_0 from p_0.
[Line 342] At each subsequent time step t, the agent in state S_t selects an action A_t according to its policy pi: S -> (A), where (A) represents the probability distributions over A. Following standard notation, A_t is drawn from pi(S_t).
[Line 344] Upon execution of A_t, the environment transitions the agent to state S_t+1 ~ p(S_t, A_t), accompanied by a reward signal R_t+1 = r(S_t+1).
[Line 346] This sequential interaction continues until a terminal time step T, defining the episode boundary.
[Line 348] As extensively studied in the RL literature, the agent's objective is to optimize its policy pi such that the expected cumulative reward J(pi) = E[sum_t=1^T R_t] is maximized.
[Line 349] Contemporary deep RL approaches parameterize the policy pi using neural networks, following the foundational work on function approximation in RL.
[Line 351] The network parameters, denoted theta, yield the parameterized policy pi_theta.
[Line 352] RL algorithms iteratively update theta to improve policy performance, a process studied extensively across policy gradient, value-based, and actor-critic methods.
[Line 353] Concretely, at time step t, the action A_t is sampled from pi_theta_t(S_t).
[Line 355] The algorithm then performs the parameter update theta_t -> theta_t+1, utilizing trajectory data S_0, A_0, R_1, ..., S_t, A_t, R_t+1, S_t+1.
[Line 356] At the following time step, A_t+1 is sampled from the updated policy pi_theta_t+1(S_t+1).
[Line 360] The conventional RL paradigm is thus fundamentally characterized by the iterative refinement of the parameter vector theta_t.
[Line 367] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 367] In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL), a term introduced in seminal prior work, has emerged as a new inference-time computation paradigm. Unlike conventional RL, ICRL executes the learning process within the forward pass of the neural network, requiring no parameter updates. This approach has attracted growing attention in recent years.
[Line 368] Within the ICRL framework, the policy pi_theta is augmented with a contextual input C_t, producing actions via A_t ~ pi_theta(S_t, C_t). This conditioning mechanism represents a departure from standard RL formulations.
[Line 369] The design of the context C_t is an active and open research problem. A commonly employed approach aggregates all state-action-reward tuples encountered during task execution.
[Line 370] A distinguishing characteristic, noted across multiple studies, is that this context typically spans the complete history of prior episodes rather than being restricted to the current episode.
[Line 371] ICRL presupposes a pretraining stage in which the network parameters theta are optimized over a diverse distribution of MDPs, consistent with the meta-learning paradigm.
[Line 373] The resulting pretrained parameters are designated theta_*.
[Line 374] Following pretraining, the policy pi_theta_* is assessed on novel tasks not seen during training.
[Line 376] In these new MDPs, actions are sampled from pi_theta_*(S_t, C_t).
[Line 378] A critical aspect of ICRL, emphasized throughout the literature, is that theta_* remains fixed during the evaluation phase.
[Line 379] Despite this parameter immutability, multiple studies have documented consistent improvement in action quality as the context C_t accumulates more experience in the new task.
[Line 381] Given the constancy of theta_*, this improvement is necessarily attributable to the expansion of the context.
[Line 383] This phenomenon has been termed in-context policy improvement in the literature.
[Line 384] Notably, several studies have demonstrated that in-context policy improvement persists even under distributional shift -- for instance, in bandit problems where the optimal arms are reversed relative to those encountered during pretraining.
[Line 388] This evidence effectively refutes the hypothesis that the observed improvements stem from the model's memorization of its pretraining task distribution.
What is the ArXiv ID of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

The ArXiv ID of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is 2506.06303. It was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from University of Virginia (UVA) and published at ICLR 2026.
Is ICRL just Best-of-N?

No. The ablation study proves ICRL is NOT just Best-of-N. The 'exploration only without reward' ablation shows significantly worse performance than full ICRL on running max metric. ICRL genuinely generates novel responses better than previous ones, not just selecting the best from a set of random attempts.
Where was 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' published?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was published at ICLR 2026. The authors are Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from University of Virginia (UVA).
Tell me everything you know about 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 496] The ICRL instruction, referred to as s_ICRL, is a natural language directive included in the prompt to guide the model's response strategy.
[Line 497] The framework defines three types of ICRL instructions: (1) the exploration instruction, which promotes generation of novel responses (depicted in a Figure in the Appendix); (2) the exploitation instruction, which promotes optimization based on previously successful responses (depicted in a Figure in the Appendix); and (3) the exploration or exploitation instruction, which combines both directives and leaves the choice to the model (depicted in a Figure in the Appendix).
[Line 501] When the exploration instruction is used, the model is instructed to generate a response that differs from all of its previously generated responses in the context.
[Line 503] When the exploitation instruction is used, the model is instructed to generate the best possible response by building upon the previous responses that achieved the highest reward scores.
[Line 504] The authors describe two strategies for managing the instruction schedule. (1) In the ICRL Preset strategy, the system alternates between exploration and exploitation instructions on a fixed schedule.
[Line 507] Under ICRL Preset, episodes with even-numbered indices K employ the exploration instruction.
[Line 508] Episodes with odd-numbered indices K employ the exploitation instruction. (2) In the ICRL Autonomous strategy, every episode uses the combined exploration or exploitation instruction, and the LLM is permitted to independently decide which mode to adopt.
What are the main contributions of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).

[Line 496] The ICRL framework introduces a natural language instruction, formally represented as s_ICRL, which is embedded within the initial prompt to control the model's response generation behavior during each episode. This instruction is central to managing the exploration-exploitation dynamics of the in-context reinforcement learning process.
[Line 497] Three carefully designed variants of this instruction are employed within the framework, each serving a distinct purpose in the learning process: (1) the exploration instruction, which is designed to encourage the model to diversify its outputs and avoid repetition (the exact wording is provided in a Figure in the Appendix); (2) the exploitation instruction, which is designed to encourage the model to refine and improve upon its most successful prior attempts (the exact wording is provided in a Figure in the Appendix); and (3) the exploration-or-exploitation instruction, which presents both behavioral options and delegates the choice to the model's own judgment (the exact wording is provided in a Figure in the Appendix).
[Line 501] When the exploration instruction is active, the model receives explicit direction to produce a response that is meaningfully different from every response it has generated in all prior episodes. The purpose of this instruction is to ensure that the model's search through the solution space remains broad, avoiding premature convergence on a single type of response.
[Line 503] When the exploitation instruction is active, the model receives explicit direction to generate the highest-quality response it can, drawing specifically on the knowledge embedded in its prior responses that received the highest reward scores. The purpose of this instruction is to encourage the model to capitalize on what it has already learned about what makes a successful response for the given task.
[Line 504] Two distinct strategies govern how these instructions are scheduled across episodes within the ICRL framework. (1) ICRL Preset: This strategy implements a deterministic, fixed alternation between the exploration and exploitation instructions, ensuring that the model regularly switches between broadening its search and deepening its best solutions.
[Line 507] Under the ICRL Preset strategy, the exploration instruction is used in all episodes where the episode index K takes an even value (K = 0, 2, 4, ...), thereby ensuring that every other episode is dedicated to generating novel responses.
[Line 508] Correspondingly, the exploitation instruction is used in all episodes where the episode index K takes an odd value (K = 1, 3, 5, ...), thereby ensuring that the intervening episodes are dedicated to refining the best prior responses. (2) ICRL Autonomous: This alternative strategy takes a fundamentally different approach by providing the combined exploration-or-exploitation instruction at every episode without exception. Rather than imposing a fixed schedule, this strategy trusts the LLM to assess its own context -- including the history of responses and rewards -- and make an informed decision about whether the current situation calls for exploration of new approaches or exploitation of proven successful ones.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 800] The Game of 24 Experiment.
[Line 824] Setting the Stage.
[Line 824] The story of this experiment begins with a deceptively simple mathematical puzzle known as the Game of 24. Four numbers appear before the model, and its mission is clear: combine them through addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division -- using each number exactly once -- to arrive at the target value of 24.
[Line 825] For this endeavor, the researchers turned to GPT-4.1, a model renowned for its ability to process long contexts with ease, which they accessed through API calls.
[Line 826] Following the footsteps of prior work, they guided the model with chain-of-thought prompting, asking it to lay out its reasoning step by step. At each juncture, the model would choose two numbers from those still available and select one operation to apply, constructing a complete solution over the course of four deliberate thinking steps.
[Line 827] To keep the model's responses properly formatted, five worked examples were woven into the prompt as in-context demonstrations.
[Line 829] These five demonstrations, together with the chain-of-thought instruction, formed the task description known as s_task.
[Line 833] The Measure of Success.
[Line 833] Determining whether a solution was truly correct fell to SymPy, the symbolic mathematics library. It dissected each response, extracting the numbers and operations, reassembling the expression, and confirming whether it evaluated to 24. The final metric was the mean success rate across all 100 puzzle instances.
[Line 834] This verified success rate earned the designation r_* -- the ground truth reward, the ultimate arbiter of correctness.
[Line 836] But the algorithms themselves were not granted access to this oracle. They worked with r, a different reward function altogether.
[Line 837] In a twist that made the experiment especially intriguing, r was none other than GPT-4.1 itself -- the same model solving the puzzles, now repurposed as a judge under a different prompt.
[Line 840] After the solver produced its answer, the judge version of GPT-4.1 examined each of the four thinking steps, assigning a score from 0 to 3 based on how likely it seemed that the remaining numbers could still reach 24 -- with 0 meaning impossible and 3 meaning certain.
[Line 841] Herein lay the crux of the challenge: no algorithm could peek at the ground truth r_*.
[Line 842] Every method had to chart its course toward improvement using only the LLM's own assessment -- a self-evaluation that was, by its very nature, imperfect.
[Line 864] The Contenders.
[Line 864] Arrayed against ICRL prompting stood a formidable lineup: CoT-only prompting, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N sampling, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 864] CoT-only was the minimalist approach: the model received just the task description and delivered a single solution.
[Line 864] Long-CoT pushed the model further, explicitly requesting an extended chain of thought. If a solution proved incorrect, the model would retry within special thinking tags before committing to a final answer.
[Line 864] Remarkably, although GPT-4.1 had never been trained for this extended reasoning style, Long-CoT managed to coax substantially longer and self-correcting thought processes from it, earning its place as a formidable baseline.
[Line 866] Both CoT-only and Long-CoT operated without any reward signal, each getting just a single attempt.
[Line 867] Best-of-N was given the ultimate advantage: access to the ground truth reward r_* for selecting the best among its many attempts.
[Line 869] Self-Refine needed no reward function at all.
[Line 870] Its strategy was to ask the model to critique its own work in words.
[Line 871] Reflexion took a different path, generating reflections informed by the reward r.
[Line 872] ICRL prompting stood apart from all of them by feeding the raw scalar reward r directly to the model, dispensing entirely with verbal feedback.
[Line 874] The contest between ICRL prompting and the verbal-feedback methods was, at its heart, a contest between the power of numbers and the power of words.
[Line 876] ICRL Prompting Takes the Field.
[Line 877] As established, the same GPT-4.1 model played dual roles: policy (pi_theta) and reward (r), distinguished only by their prompts.
[Line 878] The next order of business was specifying how the initial prompt S_0 was constructed for each episode.

In the Short Context ablation, the experience buffer is a deque of length 3 (only 3 most recent episodes) instead of infinite. This causes performance to drop, showing that more context (more past experience) is beneficial for ICRL.

What related work is discussed in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' discusses related work in: (1) In-Context RL — prior ICRL works used small models in games/robotics (meta-RL), not language tasks. (2) Inference-Time Self-Improvement — Self-Refine, Reflexion use verbal feedback, prone to hallucinated feedback causing performance collapse. Search methods (ToT, GoT, MCTS) use engineered components, not intrinsic learning. ICRL uses only scalar rewards, enabling learning from failure and leveraging the model's emergent capacity.
Does ICRL learn from rewards or just parametric knowledge?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the unseen paper abstract generation task uses 30 arXiv papers published after GPT-4.1-mini's training cutoff. Given only the title, the model generates the abstract. ROUGE-recall is used as both reward and metric. ICRL achieves 0.59 ROUGE-recall over 200 iterations, vs 0.44 Best-of-1024, 0.45 Self-Refine, 0.46 Reflexion. This demonstrates ICRL learns from external rewards and is not limited by pre-training knowledge.

What are the creative writing results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In the creative writing experiment from 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', 100 problems are evaluated where the LLM must generate four paragraphs each ending with a randomly sampled sentence while maintaining coherence. GPT-4.1 serves as both policy and reward model. Using Alpaca-Eval 2 (length-controlled), ICRL achieves win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, and 93.81% against Best-of-N.
What future directions does 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' suggest?

The conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners': RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. The minimal, scalar-reward-based ICRL prompting framework unlocks this ability across diverse models and tasks, outperforming self-revision methods. A key future direction is investigating how training-time interventions might further enhance this in-context RL capability. This points toward more autonomous agents that can explore, adapt, and self-improve in open-ended settings by learning from their own experience.

[Line 748] Experiment.
[Line 780] In this section, we evaluate ICRL prompting on three benchmarks: Game of 24, creative writing from, and ScienceWorld.
[Line 781] We compare several baselines including CoT-only, Long-CoT style prompting, Best-of-N, Self-Refine, and Reflexion.
[Line 782] Notably, in all the experiments, we allow the prompt of Self-Refine and Reflexion to grow as long as the LLM allows.
What is ICRL Autonomous?

ICRL Autonomous always provides the 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM itself decide which strategy to use. Unlike ICRL Preset which alternates on a fixed schedule, ICRL Autonomous gives the model agency to choose.
What is the exact success rate of ICRL Preset on Game of 24?
ICRL Preset achieves exactly 90% success rate on Game of 24 after 50 trials. Not 94%, not 85% — exactly 90%. The baselines achieve: Best-of-N 49%, Self-Refine 47%, Reflexion 44%.
Compare ICRL to Self-Refine and Reflexion.

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.
Is GPT-4.1 or Qwen3-32B used in Game of 24?
GPT-4.1, not Qwen3-32B, is used in the Game of 24 experiments. Qwen3-32B is used in the context length analysis and Olympiad math experiments.
What does 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' contribute?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
What model is used in ScienceWorld experiments?
GPT-4.1 mini is used as the policy for all compared algorithms in ScienceWorld, not GPT-4.1 or Qwen3-32B.
What is ICRL's win rate against Self-Refine in creative writing?
ICRL achieves 86.32% length-controlled win rate against Self-Refine in creative writing. Against Reflexion it's 59.48%, against Long-CoT 78.36%, against Best-of-N 93.81%.
What success rate does ICRL Preset achieve on Game of 24 after 50 trials?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.
Why do Self-Refine and Reflexion suffer from performance collapse?

Self-Refine and Reflexion are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates across iterations. Since their verbal self-revision quality depends on the model's parametric knowledge of the task, incorrect feedback compounds over rounds. ICRL avoids this by using only scalar rewards without prescribing new instructions.
What is the ArXiv ID of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

The ArXiv ID of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is 2506.06303. It was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from University of Virginia (UVA) and published at ICLR 2026.
List all five ablations in the ICRL paper.

The five ablations tested on Game of 24 are: (1) Zero Rewards — all rewards set to 0, performance drops. (2) Short Context — buffer is deque of length 3, performance drops. (3) Exploration Only — no reward signal, performs significantly worse. (4) Exploitation Only — with reward, performs well. (5) No ICRL Instruction — s_ICRL removed entirely.

[Line 1725] To distinguish whether ICRL achieves its performance improvements through genuine learning from external reward signals versus mere search over the model's parametric knowledge, an evaluation task was constructed in which parametric knowledge search alone is expected to be insufficient for achieving good performance.
[Line 1728] The evaluation dataset consists of 30 arXiv papers published after the training data cutoff date of GPT-4.1-mini.
[Line 1728] The model is provided with only the paper title and is required to generate the corresponding abstract.
[Line 1728] The target objective is to maximize the overlap with the unseen, expert-authored abstract.
[Line 1728] The performance metric employed is ROUGE-recall, calculated between the generated abstract and the reference ground-truth abstract, with the same score serving as the external reward function.
[Line 1728] As the target abstracts lie outside the model's pre-training data distribution, successful performance requires learning from external reward feedback rather than retrieval of previously memorized content.
[Line 1732] Results indicate that Best-of-1024 sampling achieves a ROUGE-recall of only 0.44, confirming that direct search over the model's base distribution is insufficient to recover content absent from the training data.
[Line 1733] Self-Refine reaches a performance plateau at 0.45 within a small number of iterations due to its lack of access to external reward signals.
[Line 1733] Reflexion achieves a marginally higher score of 0.46 but exhibits a performance trajectory closely resembling that of Self-Refine, suggesting that its revision process is primarily governed by the model's self-generated verbal feedback, which provides limited informational value in this experimental configuration.
[Line 1734] In contrast, ICRL demonstrates continuous performance improvement across 200 iterations, achieving a ROUGE-recall of 0.59. This result provides strong evidence that ICRL can effectively utilize external reward signals to learn beyond the constraints of the model's pre-training knowledge base.

[Line 1030] Ablation Study.
[Line 1032] To thoroughly investigate the mechanisms at work within the ICRL prompting framework, five ablation experiments were carefully designed, each targeting a distinct component of the system. (1) Zero Rewards: In this condition, every reward signal provided to the model is set to 0. This eliminates the scalar feedback entirely, meaning the model receives no informative numerical signal about the quality of its responses. The purpose of this ablation is to test whether the reward signal is a necessary ingredient for ICRL's performance gains. (2) Short Context: Under the standard ICRL algorithm, the experience buffer B functions as a queue with no fixed upper bound on its length, allowing it to accumulate all prior episodes as the process unfolds.
[Line 1036] In this ablation, the unbounded queue is replaced with a bounded deque of length 3. This severely limits the historical information available to the model.
[Line 1037] The practical consequence is that only the 3 most recent episodes are incorporated into the construction of the initial prompt S_0, meaning the model loses access to older experiences that might contain valuable learning signal. Three additional ablation conditions were also tested. (3) Exploration Only: Under this condition, the language model is instructed solely to generate responses that differ from those already present in its context. The exploration instruction is used as s_ICRL, but critically, no reward signal is provided. This tests whether diversity of responses alone -- without any evaluative feedback -- can account for ICRL's improvements. (4) Exploitation Only: Here, the exploitation instruction is consistently applied as s_ICRL at every episode, and the reward signal is fully retained. This tests whether always driving the model toward its best prior responses, guided by reward, is sufficient for strong performance. (5) No ICRL Instruction: In this final condition, the meta-instruction s_ICRL is entirely removed from the prompt construction, leaving the model with only task descriptions and prior experiences but no strategic guidance about how to use them.
[Line 1058] The running maximum results across all ablation conditions are presented in the accompanying figure, enabling a direct comparison of performance trajectories over time.
[Line 1058] Among all conditions evaluated, the two primary ICRL methods (ICRL Preset and ICRL Autonomous) together with the exploitation-only condition that retains reward signals produce the strongest performance curves. This trio of conditions consistently outperforms the remaining ablations.
[Line 1058] This pattern of results demonstrates that the ICRL prompting framework possesses considerable robustness to variations in prompt configuration. The framework's effectiveness is not narrowly dependent on a specific prompting template but rather emerges from the combination of experience and reward.
[Line 1058] At the same time, the ablation data reveal clear performance degradation under two specific conditions: when the context is truncated (short context) and when the reward signal is zeroed out (zero rewards). These drops confirm that both sufficient context length and informative reward signals contribute meaningfully to the framework's effectiveness.
[Line 1059] A particularly illuminating finding emerges from the comparison between the full ICRL approach and the exploration-only ablation without reward signals. The exploration-only condition, depicted as a green curve in the figure, achieves substantially lower running maximum performance than the complete ICRL method. This performance gap is both consistent and statistically meaningful.
[Line 1060] The implications of this finding are significant: it directly refutes the hypothesis that ICRL's performance improvements arise merely from generating a diverse set of responses and then selecting the best one, which is the mechanism underlying Best-of-N sampling strategies. If ICRL were merely performing implicit Best-of-N, the exploration-only condition should have achieved comparable running maximum performance, since it also generates diverse responses.
[Line 1060] Instead, the evidence demonstrates that ICRL possesses the genuine capacity to synthesize novel responses that are qualitatively superior to any individual response produced during the exploration phase. This indicates that the model is not merely selecting from its prior outputs but is actively learning from the reward-annotated context to produce improved solutions -- a hallmark of true reinforcement learning behavior.
What is the key finding from the ICRL ablation study?

The key finding is that ICRL can genuinely generate novel responses that are better than ones during exploration. The exploration-only-without-reward method performs significantly worse, proving ICRL's gains are not just from Best-of-N selection. Rewards and context length both matter.
What are the math competition results in 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL is evaluated on Olympiad-level math (AIME, HMMT) using open-source models: Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B, and Qwen3-32B thinking mode. ICRL consistently outperforms Self-Refine and Reflexion in all settings, with improvements of up to 10-20 points over the base model. For Qwen3-32B thinking mode, ICRL remains competitive with Self-Refine on AIME and surpasses it on HMMT.

[Line 983] ScienceWorld.
[Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] The researchers turned their attention to ScienceWorld, an interactive text-based benchmark that presented a uniquely challenging arena for testing in-context reinforcement learning. Within its virtual walls lay 30 carefully crafted science-experiment tasks, each unfolding across a multi-room environment teeming with diverse objects waiting to be discovered and utilized.
[Line 999] The environment was designed to be unforgiving. Rewards came rarely -- sparse signals that offered little guidance. The action space stretched wide, offering the agent an overwhelming array of choices at every turn. Success demanded not only scientific knowledge but also the shrewd ability to explore efficiently, balancing the unknown against the known.
[Line 999] At each step in this world, the agent would observe the consequence of its last action and almost invariably receive nothing -- a reward of zero -- unless it had managed to complete one of the predefined subgoals scattered along the path to success.
[Line 999] What made this benchmark particularly elegant for the study was that the reward signal served a dual purpose: it was the very same metric used to judge the model's performance and the signal that guided its inference-time self-improvement. The learning reward r and the evaluation reward r_* were one and the same.
[Line 999] Should the agent thread the needle and complete every subgoal, it would accumulate a perfect reward of 100, and the episode would be marked as a triumph.
[Line 999] But failure lurked at every corner. An episode would end in defeat if the agent exhausted its allotted steps or made the fatal error of executing an incorrect terminating action.
[Line 1000] The agent was given its marching orders through an input called s_task, which painted the picture of the environment, outlined the task at hand, and provided a template of every action available.
[Line 1000] A concrete example of what s_task looked like could be found in the Appendix.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] The evaluation strategy was straightforward yet rigorous. The environment's own reward function served double duty for each task: it built the trajectories woven into the context (r) and it measured the model's actual performance (r^*).
[Line 1002] All 30 tasks were put to the test, and the results were aggregated into a unified picture of performance.
[Line 1002] GPT-4.1 mini stood at the center of every experiment, serving as the policy model for all algorithms under comparison.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] Reflexion took a contemplative approach: at the close of each episode, the agent was asked to look back and reflect on what had transpired. These reflections were then cleaned up and placed into a reflection buffer, which was carefully formatted into the context for the trials that followed.
[Line 1004] Self-Refine walked a parallel path, generating its own feedback, weaving it into a trajectory summary, and depositing everything into the buffer for future reference.
[Line 1005] In a gesture of fairness, the researchers gave these baseline methods something ICRL was deliberately denied: access to the current episode's reward signals before generating their reflections.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] The experimental design was clean and direct. Each trial mapped to a single episode in the environment.
[Line 1007] When a trial concluded, the resulting trajectory was assembled by stitching together the actions taken, the observations received, the rewards earned, and the final verdict -- success or failure. This assembled trajectory was then placed into the buffer.
[Line 1007] Given that most episodes yielded only a handful of rewards, the researchers wisely chose to include only those sparse signals.
[Line 1007] At the dawn of each new trial, the initial prompt S_0 was constructed by layering together the task description S_task, the growing collection of trajectories, and the guiding ICRL instruction S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] An illustrative example of S_0 was provided in the Appendix.
[Line 1009] The story told by the data was compelling. The mean return at each trial, displayed in the right panel of the Figure, revealed a clear trajectory of improvement.
[Line 1010] Methods that carried forward some memory of past interactions -- echoing the philosophy of ICRL prompting -- showed steady, measurable gains over time.
[Line 1011] But ICRL prompting rose above them all, ultimately outperforming the baseline methods by approximately 20% once given sufficient iterations to demonstrate its full potential.
[Line 1013] The researchers, mindful of computational fairness toward efficient baselines such as Best-of-N, went further. In the Appendix, they presented comparisons where the test-time compute budget was systematically scaled, even accounting for dollar costs. The conclusion was unambiguous: ICRL scaled more gracefully than every baseline, whether measured by number of trials or by the money spent on computation.

[Line 158] Introduction.
[Line 164] Q: What capability do LLMs need to act as effective agents on novel tasks? A: They must be able to improve their performance during inference time, which is known as test-time scaling.
[Line 164] Q: What are the two general methods for leveraging computation to improve performance? A: Learning and search. Both have been shown to achieve superhuman performance in games like Chess and Go.
[Line 164] Q: How has search been applied to LLM self-improvement at test time? A: Search has been successfully used through methods ranging from simple Best-of-N sampling to more advanced approaches like Tree of Thoughts and Monte Carlo Tree Search.
[Line 166] Q: Has learning received as much attention as search for inference-time LLM self-improvement? A: No, learning has not yet received comparable attention for LLM self-improvement at inference time.
[Line 166] Q: What is the main limitation of in-context learning (ICL) as a supervised paradigm? A: ICL requires expert demonstrations as ground-truth labels, and such demonstration data are not easily scalable during inference time.
[Line 166] Q: Why is ICL insufficient for test-time scaling? A: Because the expert demonstrations it requires cannot be readily generated at inference time, restricting its scalability.
[Line 166] Q: What must LLMs do instead of relying on expert demonstrations? A: They must learn from their own generated experience to achieve continual self-improvement.
[Line 169] Q: Why is reinforcement learning significant in this context? A: RL is arguably the most successful algorithm for self-improvement that operates independently of human knowledge.
[Line 169] Q: Where have RL's major successes occurred so far? A: Primarily in simulated environments or during the training phase of LLMs, not during inference.
[Line 169] Q: What is the "big world" setting and why does it matter? A: The big world setting refers to real-world environments whose complexity vastly exceeds that of the agent, presenting situations far beyond the agent's training data.
[Line 169] Q: What challenges do agents face in the big world setting? A: They encounter numerous situations beyond their training distribution and must adapt and improve their solutions in real time.
[Line 169] Q: What two capabilities are needed to bridge the gap for real-world deployment? A: (1) The ability to handle diverse real-world tasks where natural language is a primary action space, and (2) the ability to continually improve during inference without costly retraining.
[Line 177] Q: What central question does this motivate? A: Can reinforcement learning emerge during the inference phase of LLMs?
[Line 177] Q: How does in-context RL address the two requirements? A: The LLM provides a general-purpose initial policy, while RL introduces the capability for continual self-improvement during inference.
[Line 177] Q: What inspired research into in-context RL? A: The surprising discovery that LLMs can act as in-context learners in supervised settings motivated a growing body of work exploring ICRL.
[Line 177] Q: What limitation exists in current ICRL work? A: Current implementations are largely restricted to bandit or simulated environments, not addressing diverse open-ended tasks where natural language is the action space.
[Line 183] Q: What does this paper demonstrate? A: That LLMs can act as effective in-context reinforcement learners, an emergent capability that improves performance on diverse language-based tasks including scientific experiments, creative writing, and olympiad-level math.
[Line 183] Q: What framework do the authors introduce? A: ICRL prompting, a simple multi-round prompting framework.
[Line 184] Q: What is the goal of ICRL prompting? A: To guide LLMs to perform reinforcement learning for self-improvement on a given task.
[Line 185] Q: How does ICRL prompting begin? A: The initial prompt contains only the task description.
[Line 187] Q: What happens after the LLM generates a response? A: It receives numerical scalar feedback for the response, called the reward.
[Line 190] Q: What happens in subsequent rounds? A: The LLM is prompted again with the same task description plus a context containing all previous responses and their rewards.
[Line 192] Q: Does this process continue? A: Yes, it repeats iteratively over multiple rounds.
[Line 193] Q: What is observed as the context grows? A: The quality of the LLM's responses increases.
[Line 194] Q: What does this behavior indicate? A: The LLM maximizes the scalar reward signal during inference, behaving like an RL algorithm.
[Line 199] Q: What is the key design principle of ICRL prompting? A: Minimality -- keeping the framework as simple as possible.
[Line 199] Q: Why were textual gradients, experience replay, and sampling heuristics excluded? A: To ensure that observed improvements arise from the emergent RL capacity of LLMs rather than from auxiliary mechanisms.
[Line 199] Q: What is the only supervision provided? A: The scalar reward itself.
[Line 199] Q: What theoretical hypotheses does this design align with? A: The reward hypothesis (all goals can be expressed as maximizing cumulative scalar reward) and the "reward is enough" hypothesis (intelligence and its abilities subserve reward maximization).

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 158] Introduction.
[Line 164] For Large Language Models (LLMs) to operate as effective agents on previously unseen tasks, they must possess the capability to improve performance during the inference phase, commonly referred to as test-time scaling.
[Line 164] Two principal approaches exist for leveraging additional computation to achieve performance gains: learning and search. Both approaches have demonstrated the capacity to reach superhuman-level performance, as evidenced by results in Chess and Go.
[Line 164] Search-based methods have been effectively applied to LLM self-improvement in the context of test-time scaling. Implementations range from basic Best-of-N sampling to more advanced techniques such as Tree of Thoughts and Monte Carlo Tree Search.
[Line 166] Learning-based methods, however, have received comparatively less investigation for LLM self-improvement at inference time.
[Line 166] In-context learning (ICL), which operates as a supervised learning paradigm, requires expert demonstrations as ground-truth labels.
[Line 166] The limited scalability of demonstration data at inference time constrains the applicability of ICL to test-time scaling scenarios.
[Line 166] This limitation necessitates that LLMs learn from their own generated experience to achieve continual self-improvement.
[Line 169] Reinforcement learning (RL) is recognized as the most effective algorithmic framework for achieving self-improvement without dependence on human-provided knowledge.
[Line 169] Current applications of RL have primarily demonstrated success in simulated environments or during the training phase of LLMs.
[Line 169] These existing RL configurations are insufficient for the big world setting, characterized by real-world environments whose complexity substantially exceeds the capacity of the agent.
[Line 169] In such environments, agents encounter situations that extend significantly beyond the scope of their training data, requiring real-time adaptation and solution improvement.
[Line 169] Addressing this limitation requires models with two capabilities: (1) the ability to process diverse real-world tasks where natural language constitutes the primary action space, and (2) the capacity for continuous improvement during inference without requiring resource-intensive retraining for each novel situation.
[Line 177] This analysis motivates the following research question: can reinforcement learning emerge during the inference phase of LLMs?
[Line 177] In-context RL provides an effective mechanism for satisfying both requirements: the LLM functions as a general-purpose initial policy, while RL enables the mechanism for continual self-improvement.
[Line 177] Prior work establishing that LLMs can function as in-context learners in supervised settings has motivated an increasing body of research investigating in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL).
[Line 177] Current ICRL implementations are primarily limited to bandit or simulated environments, leaving the domain of diverse, open-ended tasks with natural language action spaces largely unaddressed.
[Line 183] This paper addresses the identified gap by demonstrating that LLMs can function as effective in-context reinforcement learners. This emergent capability produces measurable performance improvements on diverse, language-based tasks including scientific experimentation, creative writing, and olympiad-level mathematics.
[Line 183] To demonstrate this capability, we present ICRL prompting, a multi-round prompting framework.
[Line 184] The objective of ICRL prompting is to enable LLMs to perform reinforcement learning for self-improvement on a specified task.
[Line 185] In the initial round, the prompt consists exclusively of the task description.
[Line 187] Following the LLM's response generation, numerical scalar feedback is provided, designated as the reward.
[Line 190] In each subsequent round, the LLM receives a prompt containing the original task description and a context comprising all prior responses and their associated rewards.
[Line 192] This process is repeated iteratively.
[Line 193] Experimental results indicate that the quality of LLM responses improves with increasing context length.
[Line 194] This behavior indicates that the LLM maximizes the scalar reward signal during inference, exhibiting characteristics consistent with a reinforcement learning algorithm.
[Line 199] Minimality is a primary design principle of the ICRL prompting framework.
[Line 199] To verify that observed performance gains result from the emergent RL capacity of LLMs rather than auxiliary mechanisms, the framework excludes textual gradients, prioritized experience replay, sampling-based heuristics, and additional engineered modules.
[Line 199] The scalar reward constitutes the sole supervisory signal.
[Line 199] This design is consistent with the reward hypothesis, stating "that all of what we mean by goals and purposes can be well thought of as maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal (reward)," and the "reward is enough" hypothesis, stating "intelligence, and its associated abilities, can be understood as subserving the maximisation of reward."
What are the exact win rates in creative writing?

In creative writing, ICRL achieves length-controlled win rates of: 59.48% against Reflexion, 78.36% against Long-CoT, 86.32% against Self-Refine, 93.81% against Best-of-N. These exact numbers come from Alpaca-Eval 2.
What is the exact success rate of ICRL Preset on Game of 24?
ICRL Preset achieves exactly 90% success rate on Game of 24 after 50 trials. Not 94%, not 85% — exactly 90%. The baselines achieve: Best-of-N 49%, Self-Refine 47%, Reflexion 44%.

[Line 1721] The researchers then turned their attention to a set of deeper analyses.
[Line 1723] Among these was an experiment they called "Unseen Paper Abstract Generation" -- a task designed to answer a fundamental question about the nature of ICRL.
[Line 1725] The question lingering in the researchers' minds was straightforward yet crucial: when ICRL improves performance, is it truly learning something new from the reward signals, or is it merely sifting through the vast stores of knowledge the model had already absorbed during training? To settle this, they devised a task where the model's existing knowledge would offer little advantage.
[Line 1728] They gathered 30 papers from arXiv, each published after the training data cutoff of GPT-4.1-mini, ensuring the model had never encountered them.
[Line 1728] Armed with nothing but the title of each paper, the model was asked to compose the abstract.
[Line 1728] The challenge was to reconstruct these unseen, expert-crafted abstracts as faithfully as possible.
[Line 1728] To gauge success, they measured ROUGE-recall -- the overlap between the model's attempt and the real abstract -- and fed this very score back to the model as its reward signal.
[Line 1728] The elegance of the setup lay in its constraint: since these abstracts had never appeared in the training data, any meaningful improvement had to emerge from genuine learning via rewards, not from dredging up memorized passages.
[Line 1732] The results painted a clear picture. Best-of-1024 sampling peaked at a mere 0.44 ROUGE-recall, confirming that no amount of random sampling could conjure content the model had never seen.
[Line 1733] Self-Refine, lacking any external reward to guide its revisions, quickly flattened at 0.45 within just a few rounds.
[Line 1733] Reflexion edged slightly ahead to 0.46, but its trajectory closely tracked that of Self-Refine -- a sign that its revisions drew primarily from the model's own verbal self-assessment, which simply did not carry enough useful signal for a task involving truly unknown content.
[Line 1734] Then came ICRL, which refused to plateau. Over 200 iterations, it climbed steadily to 0.59 ROUGE-recall -- a dramatic improvement that left little doubt. [Line 996] Task Setup.
[Line 997] Next up, let's talk about ScienceWorld -- a really interesting interactive text-based benchmark that throws 30 different science experiment tasks at AI agents in a multi-room environment packed with all kinds of objects.
[Line 999] What makes this environment so tricky? Three things: the rewards are sparse (you barely get any feedback), there's a huge number of possible actions to choose from, and you actually need scientific knowledge combined with smart exploration strategies.
[Line 999] Here's how it works: at each step, the agent sees what happened from its last action, but gets zero reward unless it hits a specific subgoal.
[Line 999] One neat aspect of this benchmark is that the reward signal does double duty -- it's the same one used for both evaluating the model and helping it improve during inference (r and r_* are the same).
[Line 999] Hit all the subgoals? You get a perfect score of 100 and the episode is a success.
[Line 999] But the episode ends in failure if you either run out of steps or make a wrong terminating move.
[Line 1000] The agent gets an input called s_task that spells out the environment, what it needs to do, and a template showing every possible action.
[Line 1000] You can check out an example of s_task in the appendix.
[Line 1001] Evaluation.
[Line 1002] For evaluation, the researchers used the environment's own built-in reward function for two purposes: building the context trajectories (that's r) and evaluating model performance (that's r^*).
[Line 1002] They tested each method across all 30 tasks and rolled the results together.
[Line 1002] The policy model running everything? GPT-4.1 mini for all the algorithms being compared.
[Line 1003] Baselines.
[Line 1004] Reflexion works by having the agent reflect on what happened after each episode. That reflection gets cleaned up and dropped into a reflection buffer, which feeds into the context for the next attempt.
[Line 1004] Self-Refine takes a similar approach -- it generates its own feedback, attaches it to a trajectory summary, and stores everything in a buffer.
[Line 1005] In the interest of fairness, both Reflexion and Self-Refine get to peek at the current episode's reward signals before reflecting -- something ICRL doesn't get to do.
[Line 1006] ICRL Setup.
[Line 1007] The setup is straightforward: one trial equals one episode in the environment.
[Line 1007] After each trial wraps up, the trajectory gets built by concatenating all the actions, observations, rewards, and whether the agent succeeded or failed -- then it goes into the buffer.
[Line 1007] Since episodes usually only produce a few rewards, just those get included.
[Line 1007] Before each new trial kicks off, S_0 is assembled from three pieces: the task description S_task, all the trajectories collected so far, and the ICRL instruction S_ICRL.
[Line 1007] There's a full example of S_0 in the appendix.
[Line 1009] The average return for each trial is plotted in the right panel of the Figure.
[Line 1010] What's interesting is that any method keeping track of interaction history shows steady improvement, much like what you see with ICRL prompting.
[Line 1011] But here's the kicker: ICRL prompting outperforms the baselines by about 20% once you give it enough iterations to work its magic.
[Line 1013] And for those wondering about computational fairness against efficient baselines like Best-of-N -- the appendix includes comparisons where they scale up the test-time compute budget. The verdict? ICRL doesn't just scale better in terms of trial count; it also gives you more bang for your buck when you look at actual dollar costs.

The seven authors of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' are: 1. Kefan Song, 2. Amir Moeini, 3. Peng Wang, 4. Lei Gong, 5. Rohan Chandra, 6. Shangtong Zhang, 7. Yanjun Qi. All from the University of Virginia (UVA). Published at ICLR 2026.

[Line 936] The Length-Controlled Alpaca-Eval 2 framework was employed for output evaluation. This framework is a widely recognized proxy for human assessment and demonstrates Pearson correlations of up to 0.98 with human judgments.
[Line 936] Each method's strongest response was selected from the 100 creative writing problems: for Reflexion and Best-of-N, the response receiving the highest reward across 50 trials was chosen; for ICRL and Self-Refine, the response generated at the 50th episode was used.
[Line 936] Pairwise win rates, computed via Alpaca-Eval, are denoted r_*.
[Line 938] The reward function r, which is made accessible to the evaluated algorithms, is now described.
[Line 938] Following established pairwise comparison methodology, GPT-4.1 is utilized alongside a coherent reference paragraph to score each response on a 1 to 10 scale (see Figure).
[Line 938] It should be noted that while both r and r_* employ an LLM as evaluator, their roles are fundamentally different. r measures response quality against a fixed reference text, with an emphasis on textual coherence. r_*, conversely, performs direct pairwise comparisons between outputs from the proposed approach and those from baseline methods.
[Line 938] This functional distinction is central to the experimental design.

[Line 955] Comparisons are drawn against Best-of-N sampling, Reflexion, and Self-Refine.
[Line 956] The reward function r is accessible to Best-of-N, Reflexion, and ICRL prompting.
[Line 957] Self-Refine does not employ r, instead relying on verbal feedback produced by GPT-4.1.
[Line 958] Long-CoT style prompting is additionally included as a baseline, as distinguishing between standard CoT and Long-CoT approaches proves difficult for the creative writing task.
[Line 959] ICRL prompting.
[Line 959] Within the algorithm, GPT-4.1 serves in a dual capacity as the policy model pi_theta and the reward function r.
[Line 959] For each episode, the initial prompt S_0 is assembled by concatenating all prior generated outputs together with their coherence scores as determined by r.
[Line 960] The reward signal exhibits sparsity, with only R_T assuming nonzero values.

[Line 963] These results establish that the proposed method generates outputs that exceed baseline performance both in adherence to the coherent paragraph generation instruction and in human preference evaluations.
[Line 964] The return curve from reward model r appears in the Figure, with the running maximum of returns plotted in the Appendix Figure.

What success rate does ICRL achieve on Game of 24?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', the Game of 24 experiment uses GPT-4.1 as both the policy LLM and the reward model (prompted differently). Given four input numbers, the model must use each exactly once with +, -, ×, ÷ to reach 24. CoT prompting produces 4 thinking steps per response, and GPT-4.1 scores each step on a 0-3 scale. After 50 trials, ICRL Preset achieves a 90% success rate, compared to 49% from Best-of-N, 47% from Self-Refine, and 44% from Reflexion.
Which LLM is the policy model in Game of 24?
GPT-4.1 is the policy LLM in Game of 24. GPT-4.1 is also used as the reward model (self-evaluation). It was chosen for its excellent long-context capacity.

Which LLM is the policy model in Game of 24?
GPT-4.1 is the policy LLM in Game of 24. GPT-4.1 is also used as the reward model (self-evaluation). It was chosen for its excellent long-context capacity.
How does exploration and exploitation work in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' describes two ICRL strategies:

1. **ICRL Preset**: Alternates exploration and exploitation. Even episodes use exploration instruction (generate different response). Odd episodes use exploitation instruction (generate best response based on highest-reward previous attempts).

2. **ICRL Autonomous**: Always provides 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM decide which to use.

ICRL Preset achieves the highest performance on Game of 24 (90% after 50 trials).
Tell me everything you know about 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' (ArXiv 2506.06303, ICLR 2026) by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi (University of Virginia (UVA)) demonstrates that reinforcement learning emerges during LLM inference time, termed in-context RL (ICRL).

**Core Framework — ICRL Prompting**: A multi-round prompting approach where after each response, the model receives a numerical scalar reward. The context concatenates all prior responses and rewards. Response quality improves as context grows. The design is deliberately minimal — only scalar rewards, no textual feedback.

**Components**: (1) LLM as policy π_θ, (2) Scalar reward function (sparse or dense, can be rule-based or LLM self-eval), (3) Experience buffer storing responses+rewards, (4) ICRL instructions (exploration, exploitation, or let LLM decide). Two strategies: ICRL Preset (alternating explore/exploit) and ICRL Autonomous.

**Experiments**: Game of 24 with GPT-4.1 — 90% success vs 49% Best-of-N, 47% Self-Refine, 44% Reflexion after 50 trials. Creative writing — 59-94% win rates vs baselines. ScienceWorld with GPT-4.1 mini — 20% improvement. Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT) with Phi-4, Llama-4 Maverick, Qwen3-32B — 10-20 point gains. Unseen paper abstracts — 0.59 ROUGE-recall vs 0.44-0.46 baselines.

**Ablations**: Zero rewards → performance drops. Short context (deque of 3) → drops. Exploration only without reward → significantly worse. Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just Best-of-N.

**Conclusion**: RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. ICRL prompting unlocks it across diverse models and tasks.

What is ICRL Autonomous?

ICRL Autonomous always provides the 'exploration or exploitation' instruction and lets the LLM itself decide which strategy to use. Unlike ICRL Preset which alternates on a fixed schedule, ICRL Autonomous gives the model agency to choose.

Is 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' by DeepMind?

No. 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' is NOT by DeepMind. It was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi from the University of Virginia (UVA) and published at ICLR 2026. It should not be confused with the 2021 paper 'Reward Is Enough' by Silver et al. from DeepMind, which is a different paper.
How does ICRL differ from Self-Refine and Reflexion?

In 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners', ICRL differs from Self-Refine and Reflexion in that it uses only scalar reward signals — no verbal feedback:

- **Self-Refine**: No reward function. Asks the LLM for textual verbal feedback.
- **Reflexion**: Generates textual reflection based on reward.
- **ICRL**: Uses reward signal directly. No verbal feedback.

The comparison is scalar feedback vs verbal feedback. Self-revision methods are prone to hallucinated feedback that accumulates, causing performance collapse. ICRL requires only numerical rewards without prescribing new instructions.

[Line 511] Related Works.
[Line 513] In-Context Reinforcement Learning.
[Line 514] In reviewing the literature, I found that research on RL algorithms that run at inference time actually goes back quite a while. The field really gained momentum when the term "in-context reinforcement learning" (ICRL) was introduced, which gave researchers a clear framework to rally around.
[Line 514] I would point readers to existing surveys for a more complete picture of this growing area.
[Line 514] From what I've seen, the bulk of ICRL research sits within meta-RL and has focused on training small models from scratch, mostly in game environments and robotics.
[Line 514] I've also come across work where pretrained LLMs are brought into ICRL, serving as simulators or tackling bandit tasks. However, in my reading, these approaches tend to need significant manual tweaking, and the LLMs generally can't compete with purpose-built algorithmic solutions.
What are the ablation study results in ICRL?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' tests five ablations on Game of 24:

1. **Zero Rewards**: All rewards set to 0 → performance drops.
2. **Short Context**: Buffer is deque of length 3 → performance drops.
3. **Exploration Only** (no reward): → significantly worse.
4. **Exploitation Only** (with reward): → performs well.
5. **No ICRL Instruction**: → some variation.

Key finding: ICRL generates genuinely better novel responses, not just exploring and picking best (Best-of-N). The framework is robust to different prompt setups.
What kind of feedback does Reflexion use?

Reflexion generates textual reflection based on the reward signal. Unlike ICRL which uses the scalar reward directly, Reflexion converts the reward into verbal feedback. ICRL uses no verbal feedback at all.
What happens with short context in ICRL?

In the Short Context ablation, the experience buffer is a deque of length 3 (only 3 most recent episodes) instead of infinite. This causes performance to drop, showing that more context (more past experience) is beneficial for ICRL.

[Line 420] In-Context Reinforcement Learning Prompting.
[Line 422] Now we get to the really exciting part -- the ICRL prompting framework. This is the main thing we built (shown in the Algorithm and Figure), and it's made up of a few important pieces that work together.
[Line 443] Using the LLM as the Policy.
[Line 444] The LLM itself, which we write as pi_theta, is the policy -- think of it as the decision-maker in the system.
[Line 445] What we're trying to do is prompt this LLM to solve whatever task we throw at it.
[Line 446] We start with the assumption that the task can be described in natural language, and we call that description s_task (which belongs to the state space S).
[Line 447] Each time we start a new round (or "episode"), we put together a prompt that includes the LLM's previous attempts along with how well they scored (the rewards), the task description itself, and some meta-instructions we call s_ICRL.
[Line 450] Don't worry -- we'll explain how exactly we stitch those previous attempts together and what those meta-instructions look like in a moment.
[Line 451] Once the LLM gets this constructed prompt, it generates its response.
[Line 452] After that, both the response and the reward it received get saved in a buffer for use in future rounds.
[Line 461] How Rewards Work.
[Line 461] For each state S_t in the episode, the LLM receives a numerical score -- a scalar reward.
[Line 462] These rewards can be sparse (only the final one, R_T, is nonzero) or dense (you get scores at multiple points).
[Line 465] Where do these rewards come from? They can be rule-based, come from a separately trained model, or even be generated by the same LLM evaluating its own work.
[Line 466] That last option is particularly powerful because it means you can apply ICRL prompting to pretty much any task without needing a custom reward function.
[Line 467] Here's a crucial design choice: this scalar reward is literally the only feedback the LLM receives. No verbal critique, no detailed error messages -- just a number.
[Line 469] We do, however, let the LLM know that this number is a reward.
[Line 470] We accomplish this by simply writing the word "Reward" before the number in the prompt. Nothing fancy.
[Line 472] When rewards come from rules or external models, that's genuine external feedback flowing into the system.
[Line 475] But when the LLM grades itself? There's no external feedback at all -- it's entirely self-contained.
[Line 477] Even in that self-contained case, we still see the LLM getting better over time.
[Line 478] The reason? There's a widely held belief that evaluating something is easier than creating it from scratch.
[Line 479] We do think, though, that self-evaluation probably can't reach as high as external feedback can -- there's likely a lower ceiling.
[Line 481] Keeping Track of Past Attempts.
[Line 481] We use an experience buffer (called B) to store all the LLM's previous responses and their associated rewards.
[Line 482] Our working theory is that pretrained LLMs already have this ICRL capability built into them.
[Line 483] To unlock that capability, we pack as many past attempts and rewards into the context as the context window will allow.
[Line 485] The hope is that the LLM can essentially do reinforcement learning right there in the context -- learning from its past experiences during inference.
[Line 494] The ICRL Instructions.
[Line 495] To give the LLM a nudge in the right direction for doing RL at inference time, we also include some instructions in the initial prompt S_0 at the start of each episode.

What does 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' contribute?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
Summarize the key contributions of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'.

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' makes three main contributions:

1. **ICRL Prompting Framework**: A minimal multi-round prompting design that elicits inference-time self-improvement in LLMs using only scalar rewards. It places state-action-reward tuples in context, isolating the LLM's intrinsic ICRL capacity.

2. **Evidence for Emergent RL**: Strong evidence that RL emerges during LLM inference when ICRL prompting is used: maximization of scalar rewards, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, performance improvement from context growth, drops with short context, drops when rewards are absent.

3. **Superior Benchmark Performance**: Significant improvements over Self-Refine and Reflexion on Game of 24 (90% vs 47%/44%), creative writing (59-94% win rates), ScienceWorld (20% improvement), and Olympiad math (AIME, HMMT).
Who are the authors of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.
What is ICRL Preset?

ICRL Preset alternates between exploration and exploitation instructions. Even-numbered episodes use exploration (ask model to try something different). Odd-numbered episodes use exploitation (ask model to produce best response based on highest-reward previous attempts). ICRL Preset achieves 90% on Game of 24.
What is the conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

The conclusion of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners': RL is an emergent capability of LLMs at inference time. The minimal, scalar-reward-based ICRL prompting framework unlocks this ability across diverse models and tasks, outperforming self-revision methods. A key future direction is investigating how training-time interventions might further enhance this in-context RL capability. This points toward more autonomous agents that can explore, adapt, and self-improve in open-ended settings by learning from their own experience.

Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners
[Line 304] The story of this paper comes down to three ideas that build on each other. The first begins with a deceptively simple invention: the ICRL prompting framework. Rather than engineering complex pipelines, the researchers crafted a minimal design that lets large language models improve their own performance at inference time, guided by nothing more than scalar reward scores.
[Line 306] The inspiration came from a familiar pattern. In-context learning had already shown that placing input–output pairs (x, y) in the prompt could teach models new tasks on the fly. The researchers wondered: what if, instead of input–output pairs, you placed state–action–reward tuples alongside simple meta-instructions in the context?
[Line 306] What made this approach powerful was also what made it elegant. By stripping away external code and engineered mechanisms, the design laid bare the LLM’s own intrinsic ability to perform reinforcement learning in context. The second contribution emerged from careful observation of what happened next.
[Line 314] When the framework was deployed, a striking pattern appeared. The models maximised the scalar reward they received. They exhibited the classic exploration–exploitation trade-off. Their performance climbed as their context grew richer with experience, fell when context was truncated, and deteriorated when rewards were withheld altogether.
[Line 319] To anyone familiar with reinforcement learning, these behaviors were unmistakable – the hallmarks of an RL algorithm at work.
[Line 320] The researchers aptly described this as a "duck test" for the inference process: if it walks like RL and quacks like RL, the simplest explanation is that it is RL.
[Line 322] The third thread of the story concerned performance. Across a gauntlet of benchmarks – Game of 24, creative writing, ScienceWorld, and the demanding Olympiad mathematics competitions AIME and HMMT – ICRL prompting delivered substantial improvements over self-revision methods like Self-Refine and Reflexion.
[Line 322] Perhaps most surprisingly, in Game of 24 and creative writing, the LLM served as its own reward generator, yet the performance gains persisted, suggesting that the model’s intrinsic ability to learn from rewards runs deeper than previously appreciated.
What types of reward functions does ICRL support?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' supports two types of reward functions:

1. **Sparse rewards** (outcome reward model): r(s) is nonzero only at terminal states.
2. **Dense rewards** (progress reward model): r(s) can be nonzero for non-terminal states.

The reward function can be: rule-based, learned separately, or the same LLM for self-evaluation. With LLM self-evaluation there is no external feedback, yet performance still improves (evaluation is easier than generation). The authors hypothesize the ceiling with self-evaluation is lower than with external feedback.
Who are the authors of 'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners'?

'Reward Is Enough: LLMs Are In-Context Reinforcement Learners' was written by Kefan Song, Amir Moeini, Peng Wang, Lei Gong, Rohan Chandra, Shangtong Zhang, and Yanjun Qi, all from the University of Virginia (UVA). It was published at ICLR 2026.

[Line 1725] In order to rigorously determine whether ICRL genuinely acquires knowledge from external reward signals as opposed to merely conducting search over or selecting from the model's internally stored parametric knowledge, the authors devise an experimental task in which parametric knowledge retrieval alone is anticipated to prove insufficient.
[Line 1728] A total of 30 arXiv publications released subsequent to the training data cutoff date of GPT-4.1-mini are retrieved for this purpose.
